Exercise 3
Foundation Builder

## Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs):

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has four choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONLY ONE is correct.

1. The largest number of neurons are found in:
(a) Brain
(b) Retina
(c) Spinal cord
(d) Tongue
2. Reflex action immediately involves:
(a) Spinal cord
(b) Cerebellum
(c) Medulla oblongata
(d) Optical lobe
3. The nerves leading to the central nervous system are called:
(a) Efferent
(b) Afferent
(c) Motor
(d) Relay
4. Which of the following is a plant hormone?
(a) Insulin
(b) Thyroxin
(c) Oestrogen
(d) Cytokinin
5. Olfactory receptor organs are sensitive for:
(a) Light
(b) Sound
(c) Smell
(d) Equilibrium
6. Which of the following is not an endocrine gland ?
(a) Pancreas
(b) Liver
(c) Ovary
(d) Thyroid
7. In our body, calcium and phosphorus ions are controlled by
(a) Thyroid gland
(b) Pituitary gland
(c) Adrenal gland
(d) Parathyroid gland
8. Brain stem is formed by the union of:
(a) Optic lobes
(b) Cerebellum with optic lobes
(c) Corpora striata
(d) Corpora quadrigemina
9. Breathing rate in mammals is controlled by a part of the brain called the:
(a) Thalamus
(b) Hypothalamus
(c) Medulla oblongata
(d) Cerebellum
10. The major hormones involved in the maintenance of blood glucose levels are produced by the:
(a) Liver
(b) Pancreas
(c) Spleen
(d) Gall bladder
11. A high concentration of synthetic auxins is generally used for
(a) weed control.
(b) enhancing root initiation.
(c) controlling of cell enlargement.
(d) preventing the growth of the lateral buds.
12. Which of the following acts both as Endocrine (ductless) and Exocrine (with duct) gland?
(a) Pancreas
(b) Liver
(c) Adrenal
(d) Kidney
13. The nervous control of pituitary secretion lies in:
(a) Infundibulum
(b) Pituitary centre
(c) Hypothalamus
(d) Medulla oblongata
14. Neuron becomes an electrically charged cell by the diffusion of:
(a) K
(b) Na
(c) P
(d) Ca
15. Nerve cell do not divide because they do not have:
(a) Nucleus
(b) Centrioles
(c) Golgi body
(d) Mitochondria
16. Artificial ripening of fruits is carried out by
(a) Auxin
(b) Gibberellin
(c) Abscisic acid
(d) Ethylene
17. Name the hormone that lowers the blood sugar level?
(a) Adrenaline
(b) Oestrogen
(c) Glucagon
(d) Insulin
18. Chemicals which are released at the synaptic junction are called
(a) cerebrospinal fluid
(b) hormones
(c) neurotransmitters
(d) $1 y m p h$
19. Which of the following tissues provide control and coordination in animals ?
(a) Nervous and Skeletal
(b) Muscular and Skeletal
(c) Muscular and Transport
(d) Nervous and Muscular
20. The pineal body is considered as
(a) an endocrine gland.
(b) an organ concerned with voluntary actions.
(c) an organ concerned with vision.
(d) a vestige of third eye and endocrine gland.
21. In reflex action, the reflex arc is formed by
(a) brain → spinal cord → muscles
(b) receptor → spinal cord → muscles
(c) muscle → receptor → brain
(d) muscles → spinal cord → receptor
22. Which of the following receptors is incorrectly paired with what it senses?
(a) Chemoreceptors - chemicals
(b) Photoreceptors - pain
(c) Thermoreceptors - heat
(d) Nociceptors - pain
23. The main effect of cytokinin in plants is to
(a) improve the quality of fruits
(b) prevent the growth of lateral buds
(c) regulate opening and closing of stomata
(d) stimulate cell division
24. Which of the following protects the brain from shocks?
(a) Durameter
(b) Cerebrospinal fluid
(c) Arachnoid membrane
(d) Cranium
25. Name the hormone which controls the basal metabolic rate in animals.
(a) Adrenaline
(b) Thyroxine
(c) Aldosterone
(d) Oxytocin
26. Which of the following comments applies to the brains of most animals?
(a) Within the brain, neurons exchange information with one another.
(b) Brains usually lie as near as possible to the important sensory structures in an animal.
(c) Brains send action potentials to the hindmost portion of the animal by means of major nerves.
(d) All of the above
27. Phytohormones are:
(a) hormones regulating growth from seed to adulthood.
(b) growth regulators synthesised by plants and influencing physiological processes.
(c) hormones regulating flowering.
(d) hormones regulating secondary growth.
28. Cytokinins are known to:
(a) inhibit cytoplasmic movement.
(b) help in retention of chlorophyll.
(c) influence water movement.
(d) promote abscission layer formation.
29. The stimulus for geotropism in roots is perceived by:
(a) Root cap
(b) Quiescent centre
(c) Root tip
(d) None of these
30. Thymus in mammals is mainly concerned with:
(a) Regulation of body growth
(b) Secretion of thyrotropin
(c) Regulation of body temperature
(d) Immunological functions
31. The primary function of neurotransmitters is:
(a) rapid relaying of nerve signals.
(b) graded control of development.
(c) regulation of cyclical processes.
(d) communication within a nerve cell.
32. Which hormone causes the milk ejection from the breasts when the baby begins to suckle?
(a) Prolactin
(b) Oxytocin
(c) Progesterone
(d) Oestrogen
33. The hormone important in the glucose metabolism is produced by:
(a) Liver
(b) Thyroid
(c) Pituitary
(d) Pancreas
34. Phototropic and geotropic movements in plants have been traced to be linked with:
(a) Enzymes
(b) Starch
(c) Gibberellin
(d) Auxin
35. Which of the following events would likely occur if a plant tissue culture is treated with a solution containing a relatively high concentration of cytokinin and a relatively low concentration of auxin?
(a) Bud and shoot formation; rapid cell expansion; decreased cell division.
(b) Bud and shoot formation; slow cell expansion; increased cell division.
(c) Bud and shoot formation; rapid cell expansion; increased cell division.
(d) Root formation; rapid cell expansion; decreased cell division.
36. What causes a green plant exposed to the light on only one side, to bend toward the source of light as it grows?
(a) Green plants seek light because they are phototropic.
(b) Light stimulates plant cells on the lighted side to grow faster.
(c) Auxin accumulates on the shaded side, stimulating greater cell elongation there.
(d) Green plants need light to perform photosynthesis.
37. Which one of the following hormones is not involved in sugar metabolism?
(a) Aldosterone
(b) Insulin
(c) Glucagon
(d) Cortisone
38. Which of the following regions of the brain is incorrectly paired with its function?
(a) Cerebellum - Language comprehension
(b) Corpus callosum - Communication between the left and right cerebral cortices
(c) Cerebrum - Calculation and contemplation
(d) Medulla oblongata - Homeostatic control
39. When touched, the leaflets of Touch-me-not plant are closed. Closing of leaflets starts from the point of contact to the leaflets away. The leaflets are closed due to
[NTSE]
(a) change in turgor pressure
(b) specialized proteins
(c) growth hormone retardation
(d) capillary action
40. Small cut pieces of soft stems are placed in growth medium with following plant hormones. Which combination of plant hormones will show slowest growth?
[NTSE]
(a) Auxin + Cytokinin
(b) Gibberellins + Auxin
(c) Gibberellins + Cytokinin
(d) Abscisic Acid + Auxin

4. (c) Axon terminals are that part of a nerve cell that make synaptic connections with another nerve cell or with an effector cell (e.g. muscle cell or gland cell). Axon terminals contain various neurotransmitters and release them at the synapse.
5. (c) The hypothalamus controls the autonomic nervous system. The autonomic nervous system is the portion of the nervous system responsible for maintaining homeostasis. The hypothalamus contains the thirst center, the hunger center and the body's thermostat.

## Assertion \& Reason:

1. (a) Medulla oblongata control heart rate and blood pressure. Furthermore, it is responsible for many reflexes in living organism or things that usually cannot be voluntarily control, such as vomiting, sneezing, and coughing.
2. (a) The receptors receive the neurotransmitters. These neurotransmitters stimulate action potential in the postsynaptic cell. Thus, transmission of nerve impulses across a synapse is through the release of neurotransmitters by the axon.
3. (a) Cerebrum is the largest part of the brain and is composed of right and left hemispheres. It performs higher functions like interpreting touch, vision and hearing, as well as speech, reasoning, emotions, learning and fine control of movement. It is responsible for intelligence, memory and judgement.
4. (a) Cytokinin is one group of senescence delayers. These hormones are involved in cell growth, and high levels can help to prevent senescence. It prevents breakdown of chlorophyll, proteins and nucleic acid.
5. (a) In many plant species, the growth of axillary meristems in inhibited by the primary shoot or primary inflorescence. This phenomenon is generally known as apical dominance. Auxin has an inhibitory effect on the growth of axillary buds.

## Very Short Answer Questions:

1. The brain is enclosed within a bony box called cranium.
2. Synapse is a narrow gap containing junction between an axon terminal of one neuron and dendrite terminal of next neuron for transmission of nerve impulse.
3. Neurotransmitter is a chemical extruded by an axon terminal for passage of impulse to the next neuron, muscle, gland or organ.
4. Neuron or nerve cell is a structural and functional unit of nervous system that is specialised to receive, conduct and transmit impulses.
5. Meninges are three membranes that cover brain and spinal cord inside the cranial cavity and neural canal respectively.
6. Ganglion is a mass of nervous tissue lying outside brain and spinal cord that is principally made of cell bodies of neurons.
7. Thyroid gland
8. The main function of spinal cord is to control reflex action and transmits neural signal between brain and body.
9. The two parts which constitute pituitary gland are Adenohypophysis (anterior) and Neurohpyophysis (posterior).
10. Geotropism
11. The brain and spinal cord constitute our central nervous system.
12. Auxin hormone promotes the growth of a plant.
13. Medulla of hind-brain controls the involuntary actions like coughing, sneezing and vomiting.
14. The shoot will bend towards the light if sunlight falls on it from one direction only.
15. (i) Auxin (ii) Abscisic acid
16. Pituitary gland
17. Goitre
18. (i) Prolactin (ii) Oxytocin (iii) Adrenaline
19. The person is suffering from Diabetes.
20. Testosterone in male and oestrogen in female.
21. The hormone is thyroxine. It is synthesized in the thyroid gland.
22. (a) One olfactory and one gustatory receptors present in human beings are nose and tongue.
(b) a-cell body; b-axon. The information travels as an electrical impulse from the dendrite to the cell body and then along the axon to its end.

## Short Answer Questions:

1. It is directional growth movement of curvature which occurs in response to force of gravity. The region of gravity perception is root cap in root, nodes and apex in shoots. The region of response or curvature is the zone of elongation in case of stem and root. For nodes, the curvature producing region lies nearby.
2. Thigmotropism is the directional response of a plant organ to touch or physical contact with a solid object. This directional response is generally caused by the induction of some pattern of differential growth. This phenomenon is clearly illustrated by the climbing tendrils of some plants, such as the sweet pea.
3. The directional movement of a plant in response to light is called phototropism. The shoot responds by bending towards light and roots respond by bending away from light.
This happens as follows:
(i) When sunlight falls straight on the plant, the auxin hormone synthesised at the tip of the stem spreads uniformly down the stem and due to equal concentration of auxin, stem grows straight.
(ii) When sunlight falls on only one side of the plant, the auxin diffuses towards the shady side of shoot.

The concentration of auxin stimulates the cells to grow longer. Therefore, the stem appears to bend towards the source of light.
4. The information that are transmitted along the neurons in the form of electric impulses is called as nerve impulse. Dendrites of a neuron collect information from the receptors and pass it to the cell body in the form of electrical impulse. From the cell body, impulse passes along the axon to its end. At end of the axon, the electrical impulse results in the release of certain chemicals (neurotransmitters). These chemicals cross the gap (synapse) between the endings of the axon and dendrites of the next neuron and start a similar electric impulse in the dendrites. Thus, information received and travels along the neurons of a nerve to the effector muscles or gland.
5. These glands are called ductless glands or endocrine glands because these glands pour their secretion directly into the blood and also these do not have any duct for carrying their secretions.
These endocrine glands secrete a complex chemical compound called 'hormone'. The endocrine glands are richly supplied with blood capillaries. The hormones secreted by these glands enter the blood capillaries directly. The blood carries these hormones from the gland to the place of action. In this way the endocrine secretions reach their place of action.
6. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) is a clear colourless bodily fluid produced in the choroid plexus of the brain. It acts as a cushion or buffer for the cortex, providing a basic mechanical and immunological protection to the brain inside the skull and serves a vital function in cerebral autoregulation of cerebral blood flow.
7. Sympathetic nerves accelerate the rate of heart beat, dilate the pupil of eyes, constrict most of the blood vessels and raise the arterial blood pressure.
Parasympathetic nerves decrease the rate of heart beat, constrict the pupil of eyes, dilate most of the blood vessels and lower the arterial blood pressure. This shows that both the systems are antagonistic to each other.
8. The pituitary gland is a part of our endocrine system. Its main function is to secrete hormones into your bloodstream. These hormones can affect other organs and glands, especially thyroid, reproductive organs and adrenal glands
Pituitary gland releases many different hormones which controls many processes like growth, metabolism, sexual development and reproduction.
(i) The excessive activity of this gland results in Gigantism and
(ii) sluggish activity results in Dwarfism.
9. (i) The synergistic effect of auxin and cytokinin is that they both together helps in callus formation during plant tissue culture. This callus will then give rise to new plant. Auxin promote root formation and cytokinin promote shoot formation.
(ii) The increase in auxin concentration with respect to cytokinin will promote root formation. Similarly increase in cytokinin with respect to auxin will promote shoot formation. So auxin inhibits the effect of cytokinin and cytokinin inhibits the effect of auxin. This is known as the antagonistic effect of auxin and cytokinin.
10. (a) (i) Positively phototropic - Shoot
(ii) Negatively geotropic - Shoot
(b) Root
(c) Shoot tip
11. The units which make up the nervous system are called neurons.
(i) End of dendrite tip of nerve cell.
(ii) Dendrite → cell body → axon to its ends.
12. In case of a spinal cord injury, signals for reflex action and involuntary action will get disturbed. Reflex action are monitored and controlled through the spinal cord of nervous system and not by the brain. Infact, nerves from all over the body meet in a bundle in the spinal cord on their way to the brain. In case of any injury to the spinal cord, the signals coming from the nerves as well as signals coming to the receptors will be disturbed.
13. Hormones should be secreted in precise quantities. The timing and amount of hormone released are regulated by feedback mechanism. For example, if the level of sugar in blood rises, they are detected by the cells of the pancreas which respond by producing more insulin. As the blood sugar level falls, insulin secretion is reduced.
14. (a) The man is suffering from diabetes mellitus. The hormone is insulin.
Endocrine gland which secretes insulin is pancreas.
(b) The endorcrine gland that which secretes growth hormone is pituitary gland.
(i) Deficiency of growth hormone causes dwarfism.
(ii) Excess secretion of growth hormone causes gigantism.
15. (a) Thyroid gland secretes thyroxine hormones. The thyroid hormones are involved in regulating many of our bodily functions, such as our breathing, heart rate, temperature, etc.
(b) Pituitary gland secretes growth hormone. It regulates growth and development of body.
(c) Pancreas secretes insulin. It helps in regulating blood sugar level in body.
16. Plant hormones, also termed as "phytohormones" or "plant growth regulators", can be defined as a chemical messenger, which plays a vital role in controlling the entire life processes and other activities in the plant cell like, seed germination, ripening of the fruit, growth, flowering etc.
(i) Growth of stem- Auxin or Gibberellins
(ii) Promotion of cell division- Cytokinins
(iii) Inhibition of growth- Abscisic acid
(iv) Elongation of cells- Auxin

## Long Answer Questions:

1. GH strands for Growth hormone. It is secreted by pituitary gland.

## Functions :

(i) It stimulates body growth by stimulating the retention of proteins and calcium in the body.
(ii) It also stimulates synthesis and deposition of proteins in the tissues.
Somatostatin inhibits the secretion of GH.
Dwarfism is caused due to the failure of secretion of GH.
2. (a) Hormones are the chemical substances which coordinate and control the activities of living organisms and also their growth. The term hormone was introduced by Bayliss and Starling.
(b) Characteristics of Hormones.
(i) Hormones are the secretions of endocrine glands or tissues.
(ii) They are poured directly into the blood and carried throughout the body by blood circulatory system.
(iii) Hormones have their effect at the sites different from the sites where they are made. So, they are also called 'chemical messengers.'
(iv) They act on specific tissues or organs called 'target organs.'
(v) They coordinate the activities of the body and also its growth.
(vi) They are secreted in extremely minute quantities.
(vii) Chemically, hormones may be polypeptides and proteins, amino acids and their derivatives or steroids.
(viii) Hormones help the body to cope with emergency demands such as infection, trauma, dehydration, starvation, haemorrhage and extreme temperature.
(ix) They generally slow in their actions.
(c) The hormone required for the following are as follows:
(i) Functioning of mammary glands - Prolactin.
(ii) Regulation of calcium and phosphate in blood-Calcitonin.
(iii) Lowering of blood glucose - Insulin.
(iv) Development of moustache and beard in human male - Testosterone.
3. Auxin is a plant hormone which perform the following functions:
(i) Cell Enlargement : They bring about growth of cells. In shoots, auxin is effective above a concentration of 10 ppm while in roots the required concentration is 0.0001 ppm .
(ii) Root Formation : They are essential for root formation on stem cuttings. NAA and IBA are used in horticulture for inducing root formation on cuttings.
(iii) Respiration : Auxins stimulate respiration that provides energy for various active processes.
(iv) Movements : Several plant movements are mediated by differential distribution of auxin, e.g., geotropism, phototropism.
(v) Apical Dominance : Apical bud does not allow sprouting of nearby buds due to high concentration of IAA in it.
(vi) Inhibition of Abscission : IAA prevents premature falling of leaves and fruits.
(vii) Fruit Growth : Auxins promote fruit growth.
4. Difference between tropic and nastic movements are as follows -

|  | Tropic movements | Nastic movements |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) | It can be easily observed in stems and roots. | It is clearly observed in bilaterally symmetrical organs such as leaves and petals of flowers. |
| (ii) | It is due to unilateral stimulus which causes unequal growth on the two sides of a stem, root and tendril. | The movements occur due to stimulus of light and temperature. |
| (iii) | Movement is related to stimulus, i.e., plant organs either move towards source of stimulus or away from it. Stimuli which causes movements in plants are gravity, light, touch, water and chemical substances | Opening and closing of flowers of evening primrose and tobacco at the night and day respectively. This is due to unequal growth. |
| (iv) | Example: Bending of root towards gravity and shoot towards light. | Example: Leaves of 'touch-me-not' plant bend and droop on touching. |

5. (i) The pathway taken by nerve impluses in a reflex action is called the reflex arc.
The flow chart is given below -
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-099.jpg?height=323&width=613&top_left_y=1772&top_left_x=386)
(ii) Auxin : It promotes elongation and division of cell and root formation.
Gibberellins: They help in the growth of stem.
Cytokinins: They promote cell division and delay leaf ageing.
Abscisic acid: It prevent wilting of leaves.
6. (i) Specialised tips of some nerve cells which receive information from the surroundings are known as receptors.
Location-Sense organs
Gustatory receptor -detect taste
Olfactory receptor- detect smell
(ii) The information acquired at the end of the dendritic tip of a nerve cell, sets off a chemical reaction that creates an electrical impulse, which travels to the cell body, then along the axon to its end, releases some chemicals, across the synapses.

## Reasoning Based Questions:

1. Smell of an agarbatti (incense stick) is sensed by olfactory receptor present in the nose and then information is transmitted to olfactory lobe located in the forebrain which interpret the information and give us a perception of smell.
2. Iodine is a mineral found in some foods. The body needs iodine to make thyroid hormones. These hormones control the body's metabolism and many other important functions. The body also needs thyroid hormones for proper bone and brain development during pregnancy and infancy.
3. Insulin is a hormone secreted by pancreas that regulates the sugar level in the blood. When this hormone is not synthesised in proper amount then the sugar level in the blood rises and the persons suffers from a disease called diabetes. This is the reason why diabetic patients are treated by giving injections of insulin.
4. It is an example of a reflex action. The stimulus in this example is pain which is felt by the foot on touching the drawing pin. This pain is sensed by a receptor in the foot. The receptor triggers an impulse to a sensory neuron which transmits the message to the spinal cord. Here, the impulse is passed on to a relay neuron which, in turn, passes it to a motor neuron. The motor neuron passes the impulse to a muscle in the foot. The muscle, then, contracts and pulls the foot away from the drawing pin.
5. Tongue is considered as a receptor because it has taste buds which act as receptors for tastes; tongue can be considered to be an effector because it is a muscular organ having muscles which can respond to a stimulus.
6. The closing of a Dandelion flower at dusk is not a tropism because it is a non-directional movement in response to the intensity of light. On the contrary, tropic movement is the movement of plant parts towards the light. It does not depend upon the intensity of light.
7. The folding up of leaves in a sensitive plant on touching is not a tropism because it is a non-directional movement. Tropism is a directional movement where the direction of the movement of the plant part is towards the stimulus.

## HOTS Questions:

1. Oxytocin is called as 'birth hormone' because it stimulates the contractions of the smooth muscles of uterus at the end of pregnancy and helps in child birth.
2. The statement that "Pancreas is the overall controller of the blood glucose level" is correct. Pancreas secrete insulin which helps the cells to absorb glucose, reducing blood sugar and providing the cells with glucose for energy. When blood sugar levels are too low, the pancreas releases glucagon. Glucagon instructs the liver to release stored glucose, which causes blood sugar to rise.
3. Alcohol consumption affects cerebellum, which is the area of the brain responsible for coordinating movement and balancing. So, after drinking, the person looses his/ her balance and walks clumsily.
4. To promote the growth, the farmer should use the following hormonese:
(i) Auxins : It is responsible for the growth of fruits and plants because it promotes cell enlargement and cell differentiation.
(ii) Cytokinins : It is responsible for cell division.
(iii) Gibberellins : It is responsible for the growth of stem.
(iv) Ethylene : It is responsible for ripening of fruits.

## Text - Book Questions:

1. Synapse is a very small region that occurs between the last portion of axon of one neuron and the dendrite of the other neuron. It acts as a one-way passage in which transmission of nerve impulse can takes place in one direction only. At synapse, the nerve ending of a nerve cell converts the nerve impulse into some chemical substance that travels through the gap towards the dendrite of succeeding neuron. The axon terminal of a neuron releases specialised chemicals called neurotransmitters. These chemicals travel through the synapse and reach the dendrites of the successive neuron. These chemical messengers cross the synapse and start an electrical impulse.
2. Cerebellum, a part of hind brain maintains the posture and equilibrium of the body.
3.     - Reflex actions are sudden involuntary responses which does not involve thinking. For example, when we touch a hot object, we withdraw our hand immediately without thinking. Though, spinal reflexes are produced in the spinal cord, but the message of reflex action taken also goes on to reach the brain. The sensory nerves that detect the heat are connected to the nerves that move the muscles of the hand. Such a connection of detecting the signal from the nerves (input) and responding to it quickly (output) is known as reflex arc.

- Reflex arcs are formed in the spinal cord but the information is still sent to the brain. The brain doesn't have a significant response to it.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-100.jpg?height=223&width=554&top_left_y=1925&top_left_x=333)
Fig.: Reflex arc

4. 

| S.No. | Movement of leaves <br> of sensitive plants | Movement of shoot <br> towards light |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) | It is a nastic movement <br> (n o n - d ire c tio n a 1) <br> which does not depend <br> on the direction of <br> stimulus applied. | It is a tropic movement <br> (directional) which <br> depends on the <br> direction of stimulus <br> applied. |


| (ii) | It is sensitive to touch. | It is sensitive to light. |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (iii) | It is caused by the <br> sudden loss of water <br> from the swellings at <br> the base of the leaves. | It is caused by the <br> unequal growth on <br> two sides of the <br> shoots |

5.     - Tendril is a thread-like growth on the stems and leaves of the climbing plants. These tendrils are sensitive to touch.

- Auxin, synthesised at the tip of the shoot, triggers the growth of tendril around a support. Auxin increase in concentration in the part of the tendril which is away from the support. This results in increased cell division in that part. The part which is nearer to the support has lesser rate of cell division. As a result, the external part of the tendril grows more than the internal part and the tendril coils around the support.

6. Chemical coordination in animals takes place by the hormones which are secreted by several endocrine glands. The hormones after secretion reach into blood and after that through blood, it reaches to the various parts of the body. For example, adrenaline which is secreted by adrenal gland and through blood it reaches to its target i.e. heart. As a result, the heart beats faster in order to supply more oxygen to the muscles and the target organs get more oxygen and satisfies its need of oxygen to work.
7. Adrenaline is a hormone secreted by the adrenal glands (medulla) in case of any danger or emergency or any kinds of stress. It is secreted directly into the blood and is transported to different parts of the body. When secreted in large amount, it speeds up the heartbeat and hence supplies more oxygen to the muscles. The breathing rate also increases due to contraction of diaphragm and rib muscles. It also increases the blood pressure. All these responses enable the body to deal with any stress or emergency.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-100.jpg?height=328&width=503&top_left_y=1877&top_left_x=1167)
Fig.: Flowchart of release of adrenaline in blood

Blood rich in oxygen diverted to skeletal muscles from other parts, by reducing blood flow to them

Blood enriched with oxygen by rapid breathing process.
Skeletal muscles ready with excess oxygen for oxidation of food to release energy for fight/flight

## Text - Book Exercise:

1. Phototropism is a directional growth movement which occurs in response to unidirectional exposure to light.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-101.jpg?height=259&width=590&top_left_y=507&top_left_x=331)
Fig.: Response of the plant to the direction of light

When sunlight/light falls from one direction on the shoot, the growth hormone auxin diffuses towards the shady side of the shoot. This increased concentration of auxin stimulates the cells to grow longer on the shaded side of the shoot. This enhanced growth on one side cause bending of shoot towards light.
2. In case of spinal cord injury the following signals will be disrupted:
(i) Reflex actions will be disturbed because reflex arcs are located in the spinal cord. So, the quick responses needed to guard the body will not take place. The delayed responses may pose harm to the body. For example, delayed response to touching a hot object may cause burn of the body part.
(ii) Impulses from various body parts will not be communicated to brain.
(iii) Messages from brain to body parts will not be communicated.
3. Chemical coordination in plants occurs through phytohormones.
These are the chemical compounds which are produced in the cells of the tips of main stem and branches. These hormones are diffused from the originating cells to the neighbouring cells through diffusion. They are responsible for cell elongation, cell multiplication, opening of flowers etc. The important plant hormones are auxins, cytokinins, gibberellins, abscisic acid (ABA) and ethylene.
4. There are various organs and organ systems in a complex organism. These organ systems must be carefully controlled and coordinated for healthy survival of the organism. A lack of coordination between these systems can cause lot of troubles for the organism. In the body of an organism various chemicals are secreted from the glands of the endocrine system. These are referred as hormones which are responsible for the overall growth and development of an organism. All other daily decisions that include voluntary and involuntary action are controlled by central nervous system (CNS).
5. Receptors refers to some cells, tissues, and organs which are capable of receiving particular stimuli and initiate impulses to be picked up by sensory nerves. These nerves are located in our sense organs such as the inner ear, nose, tongue, eye, skin etc. In case, any of the receptors do not
work properly then the person will not perceive that particular information, e.g., if our gustatory receptors located in tongue do not work properly then we will not be able to perceive the taste of food such as sour, sweet, salty or bitter.
6. Difference between involuntary actions and reflex actions -

| S.No. | Involuntary actions | Reflex actions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) | Action which is not controlled by our will is called involuntary action. | Sudden action in response to something in the environment is called reflex action. |
| (ii) | They are connected with functioning of internal body part. | They are connected with response of stimuli. |
| (iii) | It is controlled by brain. | It is controlled by spinal cord. |
| (iv) | Brain takes the judgement and then responds accordingly. | There is no need of thinking. |
| (v) | There is need for thinking and not environmental stimulus for response. | Response is given to the concerned organ just after receiving the stimulus. |
| (vi) | Example- breathing, heartbeat, etc. | Example- Closing the eyes, when bright light is focussed. |

7. Comparison between nervous and hormonal mechanisms.

| S.No. | Nervous system mechanisms for control and coordination | Hormonal system mechanisms for control and coordination |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) | In this type, there is a network of nerves which connects the cells of all the body organs to the central nervous system. | In this type, there is no such system in the body. |
| (ii) | The coordination is controlled by brain and spinal cord. | There is no controlling organ for this coordination. |
| (iii) | All type of functions are controlled by the nerves and the central nervous system. | Special hormone is responsible for particular function. |
| (iv) | There is no quantification effect. | Quantity of hormones affect the organ and the system. |
| (v) | The whole system is fixed at its own place in the body. | Endocrine glands are situated at various places in the body. |


| (vi) | The stimuli and the <br> responses travel in <br> the nerves. | Blood carries the <br> hormones to the <br> concerning body <br> organs. |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (vii) | The system functions <br> according to the <br> environmental <br> stimuli. | The coordination <br> of hormones are <br> not affected by the <br> external environment. |

8. Difference between movement in sensitive plants and the movement in legs of human being.

| S.No. | Movement in sensitive plants | Movements in legs of human beings |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) | The leaves of sensitive plants can sense external stimuli. | No special organs in leg for such action. |
| (ii) | Plant cells do not have specialised movements. | Animal cells have specialised protein which help muscles to contract. |
| (iii) | There is no special tissue in plants for the conduction of information. | It is controlled by the brain and spinal cord. |
| (iv) | The cells change their shape by changing the amount of water in them. | There is no effect of the amount of water on the movement of muscles. |

## Exemplar Questions :

1. 

(a) Auxin
(b) Gibberellin
(c) Cytokinin
(d) Abscisic acid
2.
(a) Pineal gland
(b) Pituitary gland
(c) Thyroid
(d) Thymus
3.
(a) Dendrite
(b) Cell body
(c) Axon
(d) Nerve ending
4. Central Nervous System (CNS) - It is hollow part of nervous system that lies along the mid-dorsal part of the the body. It has two parts, brain (located in the head) and spinal cord (located in the trunk),
Peripheral Nervous System (PNS) - It is solid lateral part of the nervous system that develops from CNS and connects different parts of the body with CNS. It has two components, voluntary and involuntary. Voluntary peripheral nervous system is under the control of will. It consists of cranial nerves from brain and spinal nerves from spinal cord. Involuntary peripheral nervous system works independent of will. It develops from some cranial and spinal nerves. It is also called autonomic nervous system. It has two parts, sympathetic (for emergency) and parasympathetic (for moderation). They control the functioning of various internal body parts.
The components of central nervous system is protected by cranium (brain) and by vertebral column (spinal cord).
5. Five types of plant hormones are - auxin, gibberellin, cytokinin, ethylene and abscisic acid•
Auxin - Cell enlargement, root formation, apical dominance, inhibition of abscission, fruit growth.
Gibberellin - Growth in stem and leaves, higher fruit yield, overcoming dormancy.
Cytokinin - Essential for cell division, differentiation, prevention of senescene and overcoming apical dominance.
Ethylene - Promotes transverse growth, fruit ripening and overcoming dormancy of some parts.
Abscisic Acid (ABA) - Induces dormancy, senescence and abscission, checking excessive activity of growth promoting hormones, closure of stomata under water stress.
6. Brain is the most important coordinating centre in the body. It has three major parts or regions namely the forebrain, midbrain and hindbrain.

| Parts | Functions |
| :--- | :--- |
| Forebrain |  |
| Cerebrum or Cerebral hemispheres <br> Olfactory lobes <br> Diencephalon | Main thinking part of the brain. Intelligence and voluntary actions. <br> Centre of smell <br> Has centre of hunger, thirstm etc. |
| Midbrain |  |
| Optic lobe | Controls reflex movements of the neck, head and trunk in response to visual and auditory stimuli. <br> Also controls the reflex movements of the eye muscles, changes in pupil size and shape of the eye lens. |
| Hindbrain |  |
| Cerebellum | Regulates respiration, Relays information between the cerebellum and the cerebrum. Maintains posture and balance of the body. Enables us to make precise and accurate movements. |
| (a) Thyroxine | Regulates metabolism of |

carbohydrates, fats and proteins, release energy for body activity.
(b) Insulin - Regulates blood glucose by its absorption in liver (for formation of glycogen) and individual cells (for metabolic activiry).
(c) Adrenaline - For meeting an emergency by increasing heart rate and supply of blood to various organs.
(d) Growth Hormone (GH) or Somatotrophic Hormone - Regulates growth and development of the body through anabolic activity for growth of bones, muscles and visceral organs.
(e) Testosterone (Male Sex Hormone) - Controls changes in body associated with puberty in males (beard moustache, low pitch voice, increased growth of bones and muscles)

13. (c) Bipolar neurons is a type of neuron which has two extensions - one axon and one dendrite. Bipolar cells are specialised sensory neurons for the transmission
of special senses. As such, they are part of the sensory pathways for smell, sight, taste, hearing and vestibular functions. They are found in the retina of eye.
14. (a) Sympathetic nervous system and parasympathetic nervous system are subdivisions of autonomic nervous system. The autonomic nervous system controls the involuntary functions of internal organs such as heart rate, digestion, breathing, urination, etc. The parasympathetic nervous system controls homoeostasis of the body and is responsible for "rest and digest" response. The sympathetic nervous system controls perceived threat responses thus is responsible for "fight or flight" responses. Thus parasympathetic nerves and sympathetic nerves are known to function in relaying information of involuntary functions of the body.

21. (a) An example of nastic movement in plants is folding up of the leaves of Mimosa pudica. Nastic movements in plants are reversible and repeatable movements in response to a stimulus whose direction is determined by the anatomy of the plant. The leaves of the Mimosa pudica fold up when touched and returns to full leaf in a few minutes. The leaves of the Mimosa achieve this rapid folding by a change in turgor pressure.
22. (b) The limbic system (emotional motor system) is responsible for the experience and expression of emotion but not movement. It is located in the core of the brain and includes the amygdala, hippocampus and hypothalamus.
23. (a) A pineapple plant flowers only once, and produces one pineapple. Then it dies. But before it dies it also produces offspring.
Auxin and ethylene can be applied to artificially induce flowering in pineapple to increase yield throughout the year. Applied auxin mimic the action of ethylene by stimulating ethylene formation, and that ethylene, not auxin, causes pineapples to flower. Ethylene initiates flowering and synchronising fruit-set in pineapples.
24. (c) Insulin is a pancreatic hormone, secreted from beta cells of islets of Langerhans. Its deficiency leads to diabetes mellitus (characterised by high blood sugar levels over a prolonged period).
Thyroxine is an iodine containing hormone, secreted by the thyroid gland. It plays an important role in regulating the body's metabolic rate, heart and digestive function, muscle control, brain development and bone maintenance. Hyper secretion or hyposecretion of thyroxine can be associated with enlargement of thyroid gland called goitre.
25. (b) Hypothalamus (a region of the forebrain) is the thermoregulatory centre of brain. It coordinates both the autonomic nervous system and the activity of the pituitary, controlling body temperature, thirst, hunger, and other homeostatic systems, and involved in sleep and emotional activity.

## Case Study-1

1. The seedless grapes do not occur naturally except a rare mutant form.
2. Gibbrellin breaks the rice seed dormancy and induces germination.
3. Abscisic acid is synthesized during drought stress. It enhanced plant adaptation to stress and is also known as stress hormone.

## Case Study-2

1. Thyroixine hormone secreted from thyroid gland regulates body metabolism. Its deficiency leads to low basal metabolic rate and gain in body weight.
2. Pineal gland is known as third eye as it is located deep in the centre of the brain. It also secretes melatonin which regulates the body's internal clock.
3. Cortisol is a stress hormone and increases the blood sugar levels at such times by converting proteins to glucose. It can save patient's life.

## 3. How Do Organisms Reproduce

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-106.jpg?height=375&width=442&top_left_y=388&top_left_x=1375)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-106.jpg?height=1720&width=1485&top_left_y=825&top_left_x=261)

## DO ORGANISMS CREATE EXACT COPIES OF THEMSELVES ?

The organisms reproduce and create new individuals which are similar to each other and also to their parents. Organisms look similar as their body designs are similar. If body designs are to be similar, the blue prints for these designs should be similar. Thus, reproduction involves making copies of the blueprints of body design. The chromosomes present in the nucleus of a cell contain information for inheritance of features from parents to next generation in the form of DNA molecules. DNA contains the blue print of the organism. It is the source of information for making proteins. If the information is changed, different proteins will be made. Different proteins will lead to an altered body design. The essential event in reproduction is the creation of a DNA copy. Cells undergo chemical reaction to build copies of their DNA. This process results in the production of two similar copies of DNA in a reproducing cell. These two copies of DNA are passed into the daughter cells.
The two daughter cells may be genetically identical or slightly different from each other. It all depends on how accurately the process of DNA copying has occurred. The copying of DNA is not truely identical each time, there can be a slight variation. The two copies of DNA can be similar, but may not be identical to the original. Sometimes the new copies of DNA may possess such drastic variations that the newborn cells simply die. On the other hand, there could be such variations which give rise to two subtly different organisms

## Importance of Variation

Usually the DNA copying, which is an essential feature of reproduction, remains consistent generations after generations. This helps the offsprings to adapt in the particular kind of niche. However, there are chances that niches can change due to reasons beyond the control of an organism. The changes may be in the form of increased temperature on earth, varying water levels, meteorite hits, or any other ecological disbalances. Under such conditions any alteration in the particular kind of niche is surely going to hit the consistent population. The population of species may be wiped out due to the sudden alteration of niche.
There are chances of changes in DNA copying which results in the production of organisms adapted to the changed niches. Such organisms have higher rate of survival. If the organism has not changed according to the niche then this organism will be wiped out. For example, a population of bacteria is growing in temperate water and the temperature of water is somehow increased due to global warming. Then this bacteria will definitely die. However, only bacteria with heat resistance will survive. Variations therefore are the basis of evolution.

The long neck of giraffe is also an adaptation to avoid competition for food. It has an exclusive
Did You Know! access to the topmost foliage, where no other animal can reach.

## TYPES OF REPRODUCTION

There are two main methods of reproduction in living organisms : Asexual Reproduction and Sexual Reproduction

## Asexual Reproduction

Production of offsprings by a single parent without the formation of gametes is known as asexual reproduction. As only somatic cells are involved, it is also termed as somatogenic reproduction. Due to monoparental reproduction, it produces identical offsprings (clones).

## Characteristics of Asexual Reproduction

- Only one parent is involved.
- Only mitiotic cell division takes place.
- Offspring is a clone of the parent.
- Rapid mode of multiplication.
- No fusion of gametes.
- It is found in protozoans, coelenterates, tunicates, and certain worms. It is rare or absent in higher invertebrates and vertebrates.


## Types of Asexual Reproduction

Asexual Reproduction occurs in the following ways:
(i) Fission
(ii) Fragmentation
(iii) Budding
(iv) Regeneration
(v) Spore formation
(vi) Vegetative propagation in plants
(vii) Tissue culture
(i) Fission: It is the simplest and most common method of asexual reproduction. The whole body acts as a reproductive unit. The nucleus of a unicellular parent organism divides into two followed by division of cytoplasm, resulting in the formation of two daughter cells of almost equal size. The daughter cells grow in size and then divide again. It is commonly seen in Euglena, Amoeba. Depending on the number of nuclear divisions, fission can be either Binary fission (two offsprings from one parent) or Multiple fission (a large number of offsprings from one parent as seen in malarial parasite, Plasmodium.).

- Binary fission can be simple i.e. the cytoplasmic division takes place through any plane, as seen in Amoeba and Bacteria. It can be transverse; when plane of cytoplasmic division coincides with the transverse axis of the organism, as seen in Paramoecium; or it may be longitudinal, when the cytoplasmic division coincides with the longitudinal axis of the individual, as seen in Leishmania (causes Kala-azar).
- Multiple fission, in which repeated divisions of the parent nucleus are followed by cytoplasmic divisions resulting in many daughter cells. For example, malarial parasite Plasmodium.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-108.jpg?height=664&width=1454&top_left_y=794&top_left_x=277)
Fig. Process of Fission

(ii) Fragmentation: Some multicellular organisms are made up of random collection of cells. Such organisms reproduce by breaking of their bodies into small pieces called fragments. Each of these fragments is capable of developing into a complete organism. Examples are flat worms, some echinoderms, sea anemones, filamentous green algae like Spirogyra, mycelial fungi and thalloid bryophytes. It is the simplest method of asexual reproduction in multicellular organisms. This cell-by cell division is not feasible in organisms with specialised tissues and organs.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-108.jpg?height=492&width=544&top_left_y=1704&top_left_x=733)
Fig. Fragmentation in Spirogyra

(iii) Budding: This process involves production of new individual from an outgrowth or small projection, arise from the parent called as bud. It is commonly seen in yeast, some protozoans and certain lower organisms like Hydra and sponges. In yeast, budding takes place in unfavourable conditions. A small process, like a bud, appears at one end of the parent cell. The nucleus enlarges and divides into two daughter nuclei, one of which goes into the bud and the other remains in the parent cell. Gradually, the bud grows and becomes almost the same size as that of parent cell. Finally, a constriction appears at the base of the bud and a separating wall is laid down. Sometimes, a bud may appear on another bud which is still attached to the parent cell and gives a look of a false mycelium or pseudomycelium.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-108.jpg?height=90&width=104&top_left_y=2549&top_left_x=1820)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-109.jpg?height=396&width=1161&top_left_y=374&top_left_x=482)
Fig. Budding in Yeast

(iv) Regeneration: This is the ability of an organism to regenerate its lost part of the body which has been removed as an injury or by autotomy (self amputation as is seen in some lizards which break off their tail). This method is applied by certain lower organisms for sexual reproduction and multiplication e.g. Planaria.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-109.jpg?height=620&width=752&top_left_y=959&top_left_x=685)
Fig. Regeneration in Planaria

(v) Spore Formation: It is a common method of asexual reproduction in fungi and bacteria. The fungal hypha develops an enlarged sporangium. The nucleus in the sporangium divides several times and each nuclei gets surrounded by a bit of cytoplasm which develops into a spore. The spores mature within the sporangium and are liberated by rupturing the sporangial wall. Non-motile and non-flagellated spores are called aplanospores while the motile and flagellated spores are called zoospores. The spores are dispersed and germinate into a new hyphae under favourable conditions after reaching a suitable substratum. e.g. Rhizopus, Mucor, Penicillium, etc.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-109.jpg?height=467&width=602&top_left_y=1868&top_left_x=744)
Fig : Asexual Reproduction- Spore formation in Rhizopus

(vi) Vegetative Propagation in Plants: It is a method of asexual reproduction in which parts of plants other than seeds are used as propagule-a structural unit for multiplication. There are two methods for vegetative propagation which are classified as natural and artificial methods.

## Methods of natural vegetative propagation

- By root tuber - The adventitious roots can be grown from nodes of the running stem in sweet potato plant. The roots can be propagated vegetatively when planted in soil.
- By underground modified stems - The node or the eye of potato tuber is used as a propagule. Rhizome of ginger, corm of banana and bulb of garlic and onion are also used as propagules.
- By runner - The auxillary buds on the lowest leaf of the plant gives rise to modified branches known as runners. They produce roots at the nodes. These segments can be cut and used as propagules e.g. lawn grass.
- By leaf - Plants like Bryophyllum develop adventitious buds on their leaves which develop into new plants.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-110.jpg?height=517&width=1216&top_left_y=699&top_left_x=404)
Fig. Vegatative propagation by Natural Methods

Fig. Vegatative propagation by Natural Methods
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-110.jpg?height=149&width=417&top_left_y=1279&top_left_x=203)

## Let us grow a ginger plant by the process of vegetative propagation.

- Break the rhizome of a ginger into pieces such that each piece has a bud.
- Prepare beds of fertile soil and bury the pieces 3-4 cm deep.
- Water them regularly.
- Observe how long will the buds take to sprout?
- Do the same for bulb of onion and garlic.


## Methods of artificial vegetative propagation

Artificial methods of vegetative propagation are man made and are developed by plant growers or horticulturists for commercial production of crops. Some common methods are :

- Cutting - A small piece of stem, root or leaf used for propagation is called cutting. Stem cutting is generally used for propagation in grapes, rose, Bougainvillea, sugarcane etc. Here, a $20-30 \mathrm{~cm}$ long piece of stem with one or more buds is planted in soil. This cutting produces adventitious roots from the lower end and shoots from the lateral buds. The cutting develops into a new plant similar to the parent. Root cuttings are done in tamarind, lemon, etc.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-110.jpg?height=424&width=473&top_left_y=2037&top_left_x=772)
Fig. Vegetative propagation in Sugarcane by cutting

- Layering - In this technique, the roots are artificially induced on the stem branches before they are detatched from the parent plant for propagation e.g. raspberry, blackberry, etc.
Layering is done by bending a stem until the middle part of stem touches the soil. After some time roots arise along the part of the stem that is underground, resulting in the development of new plant.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-111.jpg?height=839&width=631&top_left_y=571&top_left_x=731)
Fig. Vegetative propagation by layering

- Grafting - This is a technique of joining together points of two different plants in such a manner that they grow as one plant. It is done in two closely related dicotyledonous plants having vascular cambia. The rooted plant in which grafting is performed, is called as stock. It should have a good root system. The portion of other plant (bud, branch, etc.) that is grafted on to the stock, is called as scion. It has the desirable quality of fruit, flower, etc. During grafting, 4-12 inches long scion with all the buds intact is placed on the cut end of the stock and tied in such a way that the cambium of the both come in contact with each other. The joint is sealed with wax or clay to prevent drying or entry of any pathogen. This is done in sweet orange, lemon, grape fruit, etc.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-111.jpg?height=405&width=344&top_left_y=1882&top_left_x=303)
