## Think Out of the Box

Q. 1 Can you graft an apple to a pear tree? Explain why or why not?
Q. 2 Why can't sugarcane be grafted to another plant? Explain with a reason?
(vii) Plant Tissue Culture - This technique involves separation of cells or tissues of plants and growing them in artificial medium containing hormones for growth and differentiation. The excised part of tissue called explant grows into a whole plant when they are nurtured with appropriate medium under controlled laboratory conditions. Normally, excised tissue grows into an unorganised mass of cells called callus, which is activated to grow into separate organs and finally developed into plantlets (organogenesis) after adding suitable mixture of cytokinin and auxin. The plantlets are transplanted to separate pots in the nursery and finally into the fields.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-112.jpg?height=640&width=1027&top_left_y=596&top_left_x=495)
Fig. Plant Tissue culture

Plant tissue culture has developed as a separate branch of science after discovering the potentiality of each cell of plant to grow into a separate organism. This potential of a cell is called as totipotency. It has made significant contribution in the field of crop improvement. Some of them are :
(i) Micropropagation
(ii) Production of disease free plants
(iii) Production of haploids
(iv) Embryo culture
(v) Mutation and mutants
(vi) Creation of variations
(vii) Protoplast culture

## Advantages and Disadvantages of Asexual Reproduction :

## Advantages :

- New generations can be created from a single individual.
- Rapid process of producing offsprings, which are exactly identical to the parents.


## Disadvantages :

- Variations are absent.
- Individuals may not be able to adapt to changing environment.
- It results into overcrowding and struggle for existence.

Time to
Check Your Knowledge

1. Why is regeneration not always same as reproduction?
2. Why can't all multi-cellular organisms reproduce by fragmentation?
3. What is the benefit of reproduction via spore formation?

## SOLUTION

1. At times, regeneration is also required to restore a lost part of an individual's body like autotomy in lizards, wound healing, etc. In such cases, organism doesn't actually reproduce. Hence regeneration is not same as reproduction.
2. Multi-cellular organisms have a very complex form of organization (like tissues and organs) which can't be regained by the process of fragmentation.
3. Spores are covered by a hard protective coat of sporopollenin and can survive under unfavourable conditions. Moreover they are small sized; light in weight and can be dispersed easily.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-112.jpg?height=90&width=100&top_left_y=2549&top_left_x=1822)

## Sexual Reproduction

This mode of reproduction involves two individuals to produce a new generation. Hens alone can't produce chicks, nor can bulls alone produce new calves. Both sexes, males and females are needed to produce new generations. Sexual reproduction involves the fusion of male and female gametes to form a zygote, which then develops into an individual.
The various characteristics of this type of reproduction are as follows -

- Two different sexes i.e. male and female take part in this process.
- The male sex organ produces male gametes or sperms and the female sex organ produces the female gametes or ova.
- Gametes are formed by meiosis and are haploid in nature.
- Sperms are motile and move towards ovum for fertilisation and then form a diploid zygote.
- It is a slow process of multiplication.
- Variations appear due to new combination of genes during crossing over.
- The genetic makeup of offsprings is different from that of parent.
- Diploid zygote divides by mitosis and passes through different phases of development to form a multicellular individual.


## Types of Sexual Reproduction

Sexual reproduction is of two types: Syngamy and Conjugation.

## Syngamy

This is a complete and permanent fusion of two haploid gametes to form a diploid zygote. When the two fusing gametes are morphologically and physiologically similar to each other, the process is called isogamy e.g. Monocystis. When two organisms themselves act as gametes, it is called hologamy e.g. Yeast.

## Conjugation

This is a temporary pairing of two parents to exchange male and female gametes through a temporary cytoplasmic bridge e.g. ciliated protozoans like Paramoecium.
In majority of animals, including human beings, male and female individuals are separate. Such organisms are called unisexual, i.e. they have only one sex in one individual. Some plants are also unisexual e.g. papaya, cucurbits, etc. But majority of flowering plants and a few animals (like tapeworm, earthworm, garden snail, star fish, etc.) are bisexual or hermaphrodite, i.e. individuals have both male and female sex organs.

## Learn More

Differences between Asexual and Sexual Reproduction
| S. No. | Asexual Reproduction | Sexual Reproduction |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. | Involves single parent. | Involves both parents i.e., male and female. |
| 2. | Offsprings are produced from the somatic cells. | Offsprings are produced from the sex cells of both parents. |
| 3. | There is no fusion of gametes. | Fusion of gametes occurs. |
| 4. | DNA copying mechanisms are accurate. | DNA copying mechanisms are not accurate. |
| 5. | Variations are absent. | Variations are present. |
| 6. | Genetic makeup of offsprings is identical to each other and to the parents. | Genetic makeup of offsprings is different from each other and also from the parents. |


1. Why are variations significant for evolution?

## SOLUTION

1. The sexual reproduction produces variation in an offspring. This variation helps in adapting to the new environmental changes and gives survival advantage. Therefore, it will be naturally selected resulting in evolution.

## SEXUAL REPRODUCTION IN FLOWERING PLANTS

The reproductive units of sexually reproducing plants are flowers. The flower is a condensed shoot with the nodes present very close to each other. The different parts of the flower are attached to the nodes. All the structures present at one node are collectively called the whorl. The first or the outermost two whorls are called the non-reproductive or accessary whorls. They are calyx and corolla. The inner two whorls are androecium and gynoecium, the reproductive or necessary whorl. If a flower has either androecium or gynoecium then it is called as unisexual. For e.g. sunflower. While, If the flower contains both androecium and gynoecium then, it is called bisexual. For e.g. Hibiscus and Mustard.

## Parts of a flower

A typical flower consists of following parts -
(i) Sepals : These make outer whorl of the flower called calyx. It is usually green in colour but in some flowers it may be coloured to attract the insects. It protects the inner whorls of the flower.
(ii) Petals : These make the second whorl of flower, called corolla and consists of coloured petals. Being coloured in nature they attract various insects and animals for pollination.
(iii) Stamen : It is the male reproductive part of a flower. Each stamen consists of a stalk called filament and a flattened top called the anther. The anthers produce pollens grains which produces male gametes. The pollen grains appear as yellowish powder which stick to our hands. Stamens form the third whorl, called androecium.
(iv) Carpel : Carpels have a swollen ovary at the base, an elongated middle style and a terminal stigma. The ovary contains ovules. Each ovule possess an egg which is the female gamete. Carpel form the fourth whorld, called gynoecium.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-114.jpg?height=652&width=819&top_left_y=1219&top_left_x=591)
Fig : Longitudinal section of flower

1. Binary fission occurs in :
(a) only eukaryotes
(b) only prokaryotes
(c) both prokaryotes and eukaryotes
(d) none of the above
2. Read the following statements and write your answer as true or false.
(a) Sexual reproduction always requires two separate individuals.
(b) An organism can reproduce both sexually and asexually in its life span.
(c) Budding and fragmentation are similiar phenomenon.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-114.jpg?height=90&width=102&top_left_y=2549&top_left_x=1820)

## SOLUTION

1. (c) Binary fission is a kind of asexual reproduction. It is the most common form of reproduction in prokaryotes such as bacteria. It also occurs in some single-celled eukaryotes like Amoeba and Paramoecium.
2. (a) False
(b) True
(c) False

## CONNECTING TOPIC

## Structure of Androecium (Male Reproductive Organ)

Unit of androecium or male reproductive organ is called stamen. A typical stamen is differentiated into three parts - a long, thin structure called filament and a swollen spore bearing structure called anther.

## Structure of Anther

Anther has two lobes and each lobe consists of two theca (dithecous), hence appears tetragonal in a cross sectional view. Each theca has a mass of sporogenous tissue which develops into microsporangium or pollen sac. Microsporangium appears roughly circular in outline. It has four layers - outermost layer epidermis, endothecium, middle layer and innermost layer tapetum. The outer three layers provide protection and help in dehiscence of anther, while tapetum nourishes the developing microspores or pollen grains.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-115.jpg?height=979&width=1093&top_left_y=1199&top_left_x=555)
Fig. (a) A typical stamen (b) Three dimensional cut-section of an anther (c) Transverse section of a mature anther (d) A dehisced anther

## Microsporogenesis

Each cell of the sporogenous tissue acts as pollen mother cell (PMC). It gives rise to a microspore tetrad, containing four haploid microspores by the process of meiosis. This process of formation of spores from PMC is called as microsporogenesis. As anther matures and dehydrates, microspores dissociate from each other and form pollen grains. Several thousands of pollen grains are released after dehiscence of anther.

## Structure of pollen grain

Pollen grains represent the male gametophyte. They are generally spherical in shape and measure about $25-50$ micrometers in diameter. It has a two layered wall. The outer hard layer is called the exine which is made up of a resistant organic material known as sporopollenin. It can withstand high temperatures and strong acids, alkali and enzymes. The exine is discontinuous due to the presence of apertures, called as germ pores, where sporopollenin is absent. Pollen grains have been found as fossils because of the presence of sporopollenin. The inner wall of the pollen grain is called the intine. It is thin and continuous layer made up of cellulose and pectin. The cytoplasm of the pollen grain is surrounded by a plasma membrane.
A mature pollen grain contains two cells-the vegetative cell and generative cell.
The vegetative cell is bigger, has abundant food reserve and a large irregularly shaped nucleus. The generative cell is small and floats in the cytoplasm of the vegetative cell. It is spindle shaped with dense cytoplasm and a small nucleus. In most of the angiosperms, pollen grains are shed at the two celled stage. In some species, the generative nucleus divides mitotically to give rise to two male gametes (3-celled) before the pollen grains are shed.
Once the pollen grains shed, they have to land on the stigma (female reproductive part) before they loose viability. The period of viability of pollen grains varies from 30 minutes to few months.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-116.jpg?height=560&width=899&top_left_y=950&top_left_x=575)
Fig. Stages of Microspore maturation into a Pollen Grain

## Structure of Gynoecium (Female Reproductive Organ)

The unit of gynoecium is called pistil or carpel. It may be monocarpellary (one carpel), bicarpellary (two carpels), tricarpellary (three carpels) or multicarpellary (many carpels). When the number of carpels is more than one, the gynoecium can be syncarpous (all the carpels are fused together) or apocarpous (carpels remain free). Each pistil has three parts; ovary, style and stigma. Placenta is present inside the ovarian cavity (locule) of ovary. It contain ovules, also known as megasporangia. The number of ovules in an ovary may be one as in mango, rice, wheat or many as in orchids, watermelon, papaya, etc.

## Ovule

The ovule arises as a primordium on the placenta. Ovule is attached to the placenta by a short stalk called funiculus or funicle. The body of the ovule fuses with funicle in the region called hilum. Thus hilum represents the junction between ovule and funicle. Each ovule has two protective coverings called integuments. They cover the whole ovule except at the tip, where a small opening called as micropyle is present. Opposite to the micropylar end is chalaza, which is the basal part of the ovule. Enclosed within the integuments is a mass of cells called nucellus. Cells of nucellus are rich in reserve food material. Embedded in the nucellus, is the female gameotophyte called embryo sac.

## Megasporogenesis

The process of formation of megaspore from megaspore mother cell (MMC) is

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-116.jpg?height=392&width=492&top_left_y=1932&top_left_x=1320)
Fig. : Ovule

called megasporogenesis. A single MMC differentiates in the nucleus towards the micropylar region. It is a large cell containing dense cytoplasm and a prominent nucleus. MMC undergoes meiosis and results in production of four megaspores.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-116.jpg?height=95&width=106&top_left_y=2549&top_left_x=1820)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-117.jpg?height=510&width=775&top_left_y=370&top_left_x=715)
Fig. Part of ovule showing Megasporogenesis

## Embryo Sac

Only one of the four megaspore develops into a functional embryo sac or the female gametophyte while the other three degenerate. The functional embryo sac nucleus divides mitotically into two nuclei which move to the opposite poles, forming a 2 -nucleate embryo sac. Two more sequential mitotic divisions in the 2 -nucleate embryo sac result in the formation of the 4 -nucleate and then 8 -nucleate embryo sac. These mitotic divisions are free nuclear i.e. they are not immediately followed by cell wall formation. After it reaches the 8 -nucleate stage, cell walls are laid down to organise into a typical female gametophyte.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-117.jpg?height=506&width=885&top_left_y=1228&top_left_x=653)

There is characteristic distribution of cells within the embryo sac. Three cells are grouped at the micropylar end and constitute the egg apparatus. The egg apparatus consists of two synergids and one egg cell. The synergids have special thickenings at the micropylar tip called filiform apparatus, which play an important role in guiding the pollen tubes into the synergids. The three cells at the chalazal end are called the antipodal cells. There is one large central cell which has two polar nuclei in the centre of cell. Hence, a typical angiospermic embryo sac, at maturity, is $\mathbf{8}$ nucleated but $\mathbf{7}$ celled.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-117.jpg?height=533&width=400&top_left_y=1941&top_left_x=900)
Fig. Diagrammatic representation of a mature Embryo sac.

## Pollination

The transfer of pollen grains from anther of the stamen to the receptive stigma of the carpel is called pollination. Pollination is an important mechanism by which the pollen grains produced inside the pollen sacs of anthers, are transported by various agencies to the stigma of female reproductive organ. It is an essential process that facilitates successful fertilisation in plants. The pollen grains protect male gametes from drying out.
Types of Pollination: It is of two types i.e., Self Pollination and Cross Pollination.
Self Pollination: Self pollination involves the transfer of pollen grains from the anther of a flower to the stigma of the same flower or to the stigma of another flower on the same plant. In bisexual flowers, pollen grains are transfered from the anther to the stigma of the same flower. This is called Autogamy. It is generally not dependent on an external agency i.e. wind, water, insects, birds, etc for pollination, but may result in less vigorous offspring.
Cross Pollination: It is the transfer of the pollen grains from the anther of one flower to the stigma of another flower on another plant. It involves two separate plants and depends on external agencies like wind, water, air or insects and etc.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-118.jpg?height=454&width=778&top_left_y=865&top_left_x=607)
Fig. : Diagrammatic Representation of Self and Cross Pollination

Majority of plants use biotic (animals) agents and only a small portion of plants use abiotic agents (wind and water) for pollination. The contact of pollen grains with stigma depends on a chance factor. So flowers produce large amount of pollen grains to compensate for it.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-118.jpg?height=149&width=423&top_left_y=1480&top_left_x=201)

Observe some flowers in your surroundings (Rose, lemon, Eucalyptus, mustard, Hibiscus, etc). Try to find out which animal visits them and if they could be the pollinators. Also, try to see whether there is any co-relation in the characteristics of a flower to the animal that visits it.

## Fertilisation

Fertilisation involves the fusion of a male gamete with a female gamete. To bring about fertilisation, the pollen grains fall on the stigma and gives rise to the pollen tubes that passes through the style and enter into the ovule via one of the synergids. Here, it releases two gametes; one fuses with the egg cell to form zygote (syngamy) and another fuses with 2 polar nuclei to form a triploid primary endosperm nucleus (triple fusion) inside the embryo sac. Since two types of fusion occur, it is known as double fertilisation.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-118.jpg?height=474&width=295&top_left_y=2046&top_left_x=909)
Fig. Ovary showing the process of fertilisation

## Learn More

There are three paths for the entry of pollen tube inside the embryo sac.

1. Porogamy → Pollen tube enters through the micropyle. It is found in many angiosperms e.g. Capsella
2. Mesogamy → Pollen tube enters through integuments, (e.g. cucurbita) or through funiculus e.g. Pistacia.
3. Chalazogamy → Pollen tube enters through chalaza, e.g. Betula.

## Post Fertilisation Events

This include endosperm and embryo development, maturation of ovules and ovary into seeds and fruit respectively.

## CONNECTING TOPIC

Endosperm: The primary endosperm nucleus undergoes successive nuclear divisions to give rise to free nuclei. This stage of endosperm development is called free-nuclear endosperm. This is followed by cell wall formation and the endosperm becomes cellular. Endosperm is a triploid tissue where cells are filled with reserve food material and is used for the nutrition of the developing embryo. Hence, endosperm development precedes embryo development.
Endosperm may either be completely consumed by the developing embryo (e.g. bean, pea, etc.) before seed maturation called non-endospermic or it may persist in the mature seeds called endospermic seed (e.g. castor, coconut) and it may be used up during seed germination.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-119.jpg?height=349&width=768&top_left_y=1139&top_left_x=717)
Fig. Seed of Castor showing Endosperm

## Embryo

The diploid zygote develops into embryo at the micropylar end. This starts only after formation of a certain amount of endosperm. The zygote gives rise to a proembryo which develops into a globular embryo, is followed by a heart shaped and later into mature embryo.

## Fruit

Fruits develop from the ovary. The formation of seeds and fruits is simultaneous. Wall of the ovary develops into the wall of the fruit called pericarp. In fruits like guava, mango, etc. the fruits are fleshy while in fruits like mustard, groundnut, etc. they are dry. By the time the fruit develops the other floral parts, degenerate and fall off. However, in some fruits such as apple, strawberry, etc. the thalamus contributes to fruit formation. Such fruits are called false fruits. Fruits that develop only from the ovary are called true fruits. Fruits are normally formed as a result of fertilisation but some fruits like banana develop without the act of fertilisation. Such fruits are called parthenocarpic fruits, these fruits are seedless. Such fruits can also be induced by the application of hormones. They have a great commercial value.

Tomato is the world's most popular fruit and is usually termed as a 'vegetable' in most countries.

## SEXUAL REPRODUCTION IN HUMAN BEINGS

The human beings are unisexual with two separate sexes - male and female. Both the sexes (male and female) are visibly different from each other. The individuals of the two sexes show distinctive features called the secondary sexual characteristics. This period of secondary growth of sexual characters achieved during adolescence, called puberty. Some of the male secondary sexual characteristics are facial hair, cracking of voice, etc. and female secondary sexual characteristics include breast development,
broadening of hips, etc. Such distinguishing features are present in all the animals. These characteristics serve to identify and attract sex partners.
The reproductive system comprises of two different parts:
(1) Primary reproductive system
(2) Accessory reproductive system

Primary reproductive system includes gamete producing organs, the testes and the ovaries. Accessory reproductive system includes the glands, passages and other such associated structures.

## Male Reproductive System

The male reproductive system consists of a pair of testes, a pair of sperm ducts (Vas deferens), accessory glands, and a penis.

## Testes

A pair of testis are the primary sex organs and are present inside the scrotum. They are contained within the abdominal cavity in the embryonic stage but descend in scrotum and remain here throughout life. It happens because the testes cannot produce sperms at body temperature. A temperature $2-3$ degrees lower, is ideal for the production of sperms. The sperms produced in testes are carried by the duct called vas deferens. The vas deferens unites with the urethra coming from the urinary bladder. Thus urethra forms a common passage for both urine and sperms. Prostate and seminal vesicle add their secretions into the vas deferens. Thus the sperm flows in the fluid which also provides the required nutrition.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-120.jpg?height=453&width=807&top_left_y=1016&top_left_x=580)
Fig. Diagrammatic sectional view of the Male Reproductive system
CONNECTING TOPIC

## Structure of Testes

Each testes is covered by a thick connective tissue layer. Internally, the testis is lobed. There are 15-20 lobes, each having a network of seminiferous tubules and the interstitial cells between the tubules. The seminiferous tubules produce sperms that are passed from the tubules to the posterior region of testis. The sperms are produced by a process called the spermatogenesis. The interstitial cells are also know as Leydig cells and they secrete testosterone hormone. In addition, they also form a packing tissue between the seminiferous tubules.

## Duct System

From the seminiferous tubules, the sperms are passed into a network of 10-12 ducts called the efferent ducts or the vasa efferentia. They are then passed into a highly coiled tubular part called the epididymis. Epididymis is an organ that extends from the top of the testis along its side to its back. The sperms from the epididymis pass into a distinct tube called the vas deferens, also called the sperm duct. It rises in the

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-120.jpg?height=560&width=588&top_left_y=1663&top_left_x=1194)
Fig.: Diagrammatic view of male testis

abdominal cavity and loops over the ureter of that side. The passage that connects the testicular region with the abdomen is called the inguinal canal. Sometimes, the intestine bulges into the scrotum through this canal due to pressure in the abdomen. This causes hernia. Vas deferens is joined by the duct from the seminal vesicle to form the ejaculatory duct. The duct then passes through the prostate and opens into the urethra. The system of tubules consisting of the vasa efferentia, epididymis, vas deferens, ejaculatory tube and the urethra is the duct system associated with the male reproductive system.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-120.jpg?height=92&width=102&top_left_y=2549&top_left_x=1822)

## Glands

The various glands associated with the male reproductive system are as follows:
Seminal Vesicle: A pair of glands that are present behind the urinary bladder and secrets a fluid into the common ejaculatory duct. This fluid along with the sperms is called the semen, (a milky fluid).
Prostate: It is a bi-lobed gland near the opening of the urethra. The prostate also pours its secretion into the urethra. It is alkaline and mixes with the semen.
Cowper's Gland: They are a pair of small ovoid glands that secrete lubricating fluid into the urethra just before it enters the penis. The secretions of these glands make the sluggish sperms more active and help in the passage of sperms through the duct system and then in the ejaculation.

## Penis

Penis is a muscular organ containing erectile tissue. The tissue is richly supplied with blood vessels. On sexual stimulation, the penis is gorged, with excess of blood which causes it to become erect. During sexual intercourse, the penis is inserted into vagina of the female for the ejaculation of semen.

## Female Reproductive System

The female reproductive system consists of a pair of ovaries, a pair of oviducts, uterus, vagina and vulva. The main functions of the female reproductive system are to produce eggs, receive the sperms, provide the site for fertilisation, implantation of the growing embryo and development of the foetus. It also produces hormones that control the various stages of ovulation and maintenance of pregnancy.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-121.jpg?height=456&width=510&top_left_y=1146&top_left_x=813)
Fig. Diagrammatic sectional view of the Female Reproductive System

## Ovaries

Ovaries are a pair of oval structures that are present one on either side. The ovaries produce eggs one at a time, every alternate month. The eggs are produced by the germinal epithelial cells of the ovary.

## Oviducts / Fallopian tubes

Oviducts / Fallopian tubes are a pair of tubes of about 12 cm in length. They run from the ovaries of each side to the uterus. At the ovarian end the tube is funnel-shaped with the end of the tube thrown into a number of folds. These folds are ciliated which help to sweep the egg produced by the ovary into the fallopian tube. The fallopian tubes are the sites for fertilisation of the egg by the sperm.

## Uterus

Uterus is pear-shaped structure, broader on the upper end and narrower on the lower end. The upper end is called the body of the uterus and the lower end is called the cervix. At the upper end, it receives the oviducts of either side, whereas at the lower end the cervix opens into the vagina which then opens to the outside.
The uterine wall has three layers. They are the innermost endometrium made up of several glands and blood vessels, the middle myometrium madeup of smooth muscles and the outer perimetrium madeup of connective tissue. The inner surface of the uterus provides a site for the implantation of the embryo. The uterine wall plays an important role during childbirth. Cervix is made of sphincter muscles that control the opening and closing of the uterus.

## Vagina

Vagina is a 9 cm long muscular tube that receives the penis during coitus. It is lined with epithelial cells. The vaginal opening in young females is partially covered with a thin mucous membrane called hymen. This often breaks early in females during strenuous work or sports.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-121.jpg?height=100&width=108&top_left_y=2544&top_left_x=141)

## Vulva

Vulva is the external female genitalia and comprises of mons pubis. The vaginal opening has two pair of folds on either side. The outer fold is thicker with hair, sweat glands and sebaceous glands and is called labia majora. The inner folds are thinner and devoid of hair. They are called labia minora. Covered by the upper part of the folds is the female equivalent of the penis, called the clitoris. It is also an erectile and highly sensitive organ. In this region, there are two openings, the upper urethral meatus or opening and the lower vaginal orifice.

## Gametogenesis

Sperms are produced in seminiferous tubules present in testis. This process is known as spermatogenesis. The precursors of the sperm, the diploid spermatogonium divides mitotically to produce numerous diploid spermatocytes. Spermatocytes undergo meiotic cell division to produce 4 haploid spermatids. Spermatids differentiate and develop into sperm which are single celled male gametes with a head, midpiece, and a tail.
Ovum (egg cell), the female gamete is produced in the ovaries of female reproductive system. This process is known as oogenesis. In contrast to males, the initial steps in egg production occur prior to their birth in females. Diploid oogonia and primary oocytes are produced in foetus and by the time she is born, females have about 1-2 million oocytes. In oocytes, the first meiotic division is initiated and then stopped. No further development occurs until the girl attains puberty. After maturity, the primary oocytes recommence their development, usually one at a time and once a month. The primary oocytes grow further and completes the meiosis I, forming a large secondary oocyte and a small polar body. Only after fertilization, meiosis II is completed. After completion of meiosis II, secondary oocytes gets converted into a fertilized egg or zygote.

## Menstruation

The reproductive cycle in the females is called as menstrual cycle. First menstruation begins at puberty and is called as Menarche. Menstrual cycle has an average time period of about 28 to 29 days. One ovum is released during the middle of each menstrual cycle. Cycle starts with the menstrual phase when menstrual flow occurs and it lasts for $3-5$ days. Menstrual flow results due to breakdown of endometrial lining of the uterus and its blood vessels that come out through vagina. Menstruation occurs only if released ovum is not fertilised. Pregnancy can be indicated by lack of menstrual cycle. It may also be caused by stress, poor health etc. Menstrual cycle ceases at about 50 years of age and this is termed as menopause. Cyclic menstruation is an indicator of normal reproductive phase and extends between menarche and menopause.

## Structures of Gametes

Each sperm is a small cell of about $50 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ length and $2.5 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ diameter. It constits of a head, middle piece and a tail region. The head consists of the nucleus and a structure called the acrosome at the tip. The acrosome secretes lytic substance that break down the wall of the egg for fertilisation. The beating of the tail propels the sperms forward at the rate of $1-4 \mathrm{~mm}$ per minute. Each egg or ovum is a spherical cell much bigger than the sperm. It mainly consists of a single nucleus and some reserve food made of lipid droplets.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-122.jpg?height=467&width=552&top_left_y=1733&top_left_x=303)
Fig. Structure of Sperm

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-122.jpg?height=477&width=575&top_left_y=1726&top_left_x=1121)
Fig. Structure of Ovum

## Fertilisation

Fertilisaiton is defined as the fusion of sperm with the egg nucleus to form a diploid cell known as zygote. The fertilisation is internal in the human reproductive sytem. It is achieved by the insertion of the male organ, penis into the vagina of the female. The sperms are deposited in the vagina of the females during a process called as copulation or sexual intercourse. The release of sperms is called ejaculation and at one ejaculation there may be upto 600 million sperms. Of these, only a few from thousand make the journey from the vaginal canal to the oviducts. This may take $4-8$ hours. The life of the sperm is $1-3$ days after ejaculation.

In the fallopian tubes, many sperms, surround an egg. However, only one enters the egg leaving behind the tail. The enzymes of the acrosome digest the several layers of tissue to reach the egg cytoplasm. Once the sperm is reached inside the egg, the male and female nuclei become lighter and are called pro-nuclei. Both the pro-nuclei fuse to form a zygote. Once a sperm enters inside an egg, the thickening of the outer walls of the egg blocks the entry of other sperms.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-123.jpg?height=640&width=901&top_left_y=571&top_left_x=612)
Fig. Process of Fertilisation

## Post-Fertilisation Events

Once the egg is fertilised, ovulation and menstruation do not take place. The fertilised structure, now called the zygote, which is diploid. It starts dividing and forms a blastocyst, the first stage of an embryo. The blastocyst gets implanted on the uterine walls. It then grows into a foetus.
The foetus remains attached to the mother through an umbilical cord which is embedded in a tissue called placenta at one end. The placenta in turn is embedded into the uterine wall and is richly supplied with vessels. The nutrients from the mother's blood pass into the umbilical cord and the waste from the foetus pass into the mother's blood through the placenta.
The foetus is fully grown and ready to come out from the womb in 40 weeks. The uterine walls start muscular contractions called the labour that helps in pushing the foetus to the outside through the vaginal canal. The placenta detaches from the uterine wall once the baby is out. The umbilical cord is cut and tied near the baby's body.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-123.jpg?height=784&width=725&top_left_y=1704&top_left_x=717)
Fig. Implantation of embryo

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-124.jpg?height=451&width=1081&top_left_y=379&top_left_x=468)
Fig. Reproduction in Human beings

## Reproductive Health

Sexual maturation does not necessarily mean that the body or mind is ready for sexual acts or having and bringing up children. Contraception gives individuals a choice to conceive or have children at their own will. Not only this, contraception also helps in controlling population growth. Goverment has come out with various programmes that encourage smaller families to use various contraceptive methods. Contraceptive devices are used to prevent unwanted pregnancy and STDs (Sexually Transmitted Diseases).

## Contraceptive Method

An ideal contraceptive should be user-friendly, easily available, effective and reversible. It should not have any side-effects and should not interfere with sexual drive, desire and sexual act. The contraceptive methods can be of two types.

1. Natural/Traditional methods

It works on the principle of avoiding chances of fusion of ovum and sperms. It includes :
(i) Periodic abstinence: It involves avoiding of coitus from days 10 to 17 of the menstrual cycle, when ovulation could be expected. It is called fertile period and the chances of fertilisation in this period are very high.
(ii) Coitus interruptus (withdrawal): It involves withdrawal of penis from the vagina just before ejaculation, in order to avoid insemination inside vagina.
(iii) Lactational amenorrhea: It involves the prevention of conception by breastfeeding the child. Ovulation and the cycle do not occur during the period of lactation. It has no side effects. However, this is effective up to 6 months following parturition (delivery). A chance of failure of this method is high.
2. Artificial methods
(A) Barrier methods

These method also prevent fusion of sperm and ovum. It is available for both male and female partners.
(i) Condoms : Condoms are made of rubber/latex sheath. They are used to cover the penis in male or vagina and cervix in the female. They are used just before coitus so that the semen does not enter into female reproductive tract.
Condoms are very popular because:

- It protects the user from sexually transmitted diseases.
- It is disposable.
- It can be self-used and thereby gives privacy to the user.
(ii) Diaphragms, cervical caps and vaults:

These are barriers made of rubber that are inserted into the female reproductive tract to cover the cervix during coitus. They prevent conception by blocking the entry of sperms through the cervix. They are reusable.
(iii) Spermicidal creams, jellies and foams are used with the barriers to increase contraceptive efficiency.
(B) Intra Uterine Devices (IUDs)

These devices are used only by females. These are inserted by doctors or expert nurses inside uterus. It makes the uterus unsuitable for implantation and cervix hostile to the sperms. IUDs includes :
(i) Non-medicated IUDs (e.g. Lippes loop)
(ii) Copper releasing IUDs (e.g. CuT, Cu7, Multiload 375)
(iii) Hormone releasing IUDs (e.g. Progestasert, LNG-20)
(C) Oral contraceptives

This method is used only by female. It is used in the form of tablets, hence known as pills. They contain hormoneseither progestogens or progestogen-oestrogen combinations. Pills are taken daily for 21 days starting within the first five days of menstrual cycle. After a gap of 7 days (during which menstruation occurs) it has to be repeated in the
same pattern till the female desires to prevent conception. They inhibit ovulation and implantation as well as alter the quality of cervical mucus to prevent the entry of sperms. Pills are very effective with lesser side effects E.g. Saheli which is a non-steroidal preparation used as an oral contraceptive. It is a 'once a week' pill with very few side effects and high contraceptive value.

## (D) Injectables

It involves usage of hormone, either progestogens alone or in combination with oestrogen by females as injections or implants under skin. Their mode of action is similar to that of pills. It is very effective for longer periods.

## (E) Emergency contraceptives

These are used within 72 hours of coitus, rape or casual unprotected intercourse. It involves administration of progestogens or progestogen-oestrogen combinations and IUDs.

## (F) Surgical methods (also known as sterilization methods)

These are the permanent or terminal methods to prevent pregnancy. This method blocks the transport of gametes, thereby preventing conception. It is very effective but reversibility is poor. It includes:
(i) Vasectomy: It is the sterilization procedure in males. In this, a small part of the vas deferens is removed or tied up through a small incision on the scrotum.
(ii) Tubectomy: It is the sterilization procedure in females. In this, a small part of the fallopian tube is removed or tied up through a small incision in the abdomen or through vagina.

## CONNECTING TOPIC

## Infertility

There are many diseases/disorders which may affect the reproductive system in both males and females. One of them is infertility. It is the inability of a sexually active non-contracepting couple to achieve pregnancy in one year. In females, the common causes may include hormonal imbalances, blocked oviducts, damaged uterus or cervix and many others. In males, it may be due to the absence of sperms, low sperm count, abnormal sperms, auto immunity, impotence etc.
Infertility treatments can be either hormonal or modern like in vitro fertilisation (IVF). In IVF, the sperm and ovum are fused in a test tube to form a zygote, which is later implanted in the uterus of female for further development. These babies are called test tube babies. Other methods include gamete intra-fallopian transfer (GIFT). Here, gametes are transferred to the oviducts and fertilisation takes place inside the body itself.

(d) If Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

1. Assertion: Asexual reproduction is also called blastogenesis.

Reason: Chances of survival are less during unfavourable conditions.

Reason: Urethra carries only urine while sperms are carried by vasa deferentia only.
4. Assertion: In morula stage, cells divide without increase in size.
Reason: Zona pellucida remain undivided till cleavage is complete.

## Passage

1. When the branches of a plant growing in the field are pulled towards the ground and a part of them is covered with moist soil (leaving the tips of the branches exposed above the ground), then after sometime new roots develop from the parts of branches buried in the soil. ## Reasoning Based Questions:

1. Why is it considered better to grow a rose from cutting rather than from seeds?
2. Why does bread mould grow profusely on a moist slice of bread rather than on a dry slice of bread?
3. Why fertilisation cannot take place in flowers if pollination does not occur?
4. Why do mouse parents produce mouse-like offspring ? Give reasons.
5. Why does an exposed moist piece of bread get covered with a cottony growth?
6. Why do green grass plants spring up in dry fields on their own after the rains?
7. Why are budding, fragmentation and regeneration all considered as asexual types of reproduction. With neat diagrams explain the process of regeneration in Planaria.
8. Why is it an advantage for the testes to be situated in the scrotal sac outside the main body cavity? Can you think of one disadvantage?

## HOTS Questions:

1. How are general growth and sexual maturation different from each other?
2. A piece of potato tuber with an eye produces a new plant while another piece without an eye does not. Give reason.
3. A man went to a doctor and the doctor removed his Cowper's gland. How would the removal of cowper's gland affect the man?
4. If you have to go in a village during AIDS prevention programme, what points will you explain to the villagers?

## Case Study Based MCQs

(CBSE Sample Issued 2021)

## Male Reproduction System

1. Read the following and answer any four questions from 1(i) to 1(v).
The male reproductive system consists of portions which produce the germ-cells and other portions that deliver the germ-cells to the site of fertilisation. The formation of germ-cells or sperms takes place in the testes. These are located outside the abdominal cavity in scrotum because sperm formation requires a lower temperature than the normal body temperature. We have discussed the role of the testes in the secretion of the hormone, testosterone, in the previous chapter. In addition to regulating the formation of sperms, testosterone brings about changes in appearance seen in boys at the time of puberty. The sperms formed are delivered through the vas deferens which unites with a tube coming from the urinary bladder. The urethra thus forms a common passage for both the sperms and urine. Along the path of the vas deferens, glands like the prostate and the seminal vesicles add their secretions so that the sperms are now in a fluid which makes their transport easier and this fluid also provides nutrition. The sperms are tiny bodies that consist of mainly genetic material and a long tail that helps them to move towards the female germ-cell.
(i) The seminiferous tubules of the testes are lined by the germinal epithelium consisting of
(a) spermatids
(b) cells of Sertoli
(c) spermatogonium
(d) spermatocytes
(ii) The seminiferous tubules of the testes are lined by the germinal epithelium consisting of
(a) sertoli cells
(b) cells of germinal epithelium
(c) cells of Leydig or interstitial cells
(d) secondary spermatocytes
(iii) Another name for Bulbourethral gland is
(a) Meibomian gland
(b) Prostate gland
(c) Perineal gland
(d) Cowper's gland
(iv) In man, Cryptorchidism is the condition when
(a) testes do not descent into the scrotum
(b) there are two testes in each scrotum
(c) testis degenerates in the scrotum
(d) testis enlarges in the scrotum
(v) Which of these is an accessory reproductive gland in male mammals
(a) Inguinal gland
(b) Prostate gland
(c) Mushroom-shaped gland
(d) Gastric gland

## Text - Book Questions:

1. Why is variation beneficial to the species but not necessarily for the individual ?
2. How will an organism be benefitted if it reproduces through spores?
3. Can you think of reasons why more complex organisms cannot give rise to new individuals through regeneration ?
4. Why is vegetative propagation practised for growing some types of plants?
5. Why is DNA copying an essential part of the process of reproduction?
6. How is the process of pollination different from fertilization?
7. What is the role of the seminal vesicles and the prostate gland?
8. What are the changes seen in girls at the time of puberty ?
9. If a woman is using a copper-T, will it help in protecting her from sexually transmitted diseases?

1. Which of the following is not a part of the female reproductive system in human beings?
(a) Ovary
(b) Uterus
(c) Vas deferens
(d) Fallopian tube
2. The anther contains
(a) sepals
(b) ovules
(c) carpel
(d) pollen grains
3. Why does menstruation occur ?
4. Draw a labelled diagram of the longitudinal section of a flower.
5. How does reproduction help in providing stability to populations of species?
6. What are the functions performed by the testes in human beings?
7. How are the modes of reproduction different in unicellular and multicellular organisms?
8. What could be the reasons for adopting contraceptive methods?

## Exemplar Questions:

1. Which of the following is the correct sequence of events of sexual reproduction in a flower?
(a) Pollination, fertilisation, seedling, embryo
(b) Seedling, embryo, fertilisation, pollination
(c) Pollination, fertilisation, embryo, seedling
(d) Embryo, seedling, pollination, fertilisation
2. The number of chromosomes in parents and offsprings of a particular species remains constant due to
(a) doubling of chromosomes after zygote formation.
(b) halving of chromosomes during gamete formation.
(c) doubling of chromosomes after gamete formation.
(d) halving of chromosomes after gamete formation.
3. Vegetative propagation refers to formation of new plants from
(a) stem, roots and flowers.
(b) stem, roots and leaves.
(c) stem, flowers and fruits.
(d) stem, leaves and flowers.
4. Length of pollen tube depends on the distance between
(a) pollen grain and upper surface of stigma.
(b) pollen grain on upper surface of stigma and ovule.
(c) pollen grain in anther and upper surface of stigma.
(d) upper surface of stigma and lower part of style.
5. Offspring formed as a result of sexual reproduction exhibit more variations because
(a) sexual reproduction is a lengthy process.
(b) genetic material comes from two parents of the same species.
(c) genetic material comes from two parents of different species.
(d) genetic material comes from many parents.
6. During adolescence, several changes occur in the human body. Mark one change associated with sexual maturation in boys
(a) loss of milk teeth
(b) increase in height
(c) cracking of voice
(d) weight gain
7. In human females, an event that reflects onset of reproductive phase is
(a) growth of body.
(b) changes in hair pattern.
(c) change in voice.
(d) menstruation.
8. Which among the following is not a sexually transmitted diseases?
(a) Syphilis
(b) Hepatitis
(c) HIV-AIDS
(d) Gonorrhoea
9. Characters that are transmitted from parents to offspring during reproduction show
(a) only similarities with parents.
(b) only variations with parents.
(c) both similarities and variations with parents.
(d) neither similarities nor variations.
10. The ability of a cell to divide into several cells during reproduction in Plasmodium is called
(a) budding
(b) reduction division
(c) binary fission
(d) multiple fission
11. Characters that are transmitted from parents to offspring
during reproduction show durity similarities with parents.

- (b)
(b) only variations with parents.
(c) both similarities and variations with parents.
(d) neither similarities nor variations. reproduction in Plasmodium is called

11. Where is the zygote located in the flower after fertilization?
12. Would a Planaria cut vertically into two halves regenerate into two individuals? Complete figures D and E by indicating the regenerated regions.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-133.jpg?height=228&width=569&top_left_y=561&top_left_x=370)
13. Can you consider cell division as a type of reproduction in unicellular organism? Give one reason.
14. Colonies of yeast fail to multiply in water, but multiply in sugar solution. Give one reason for this.
15. Give two reasons for the appearance of variations among the progeny formed by sexual reproduction.

## Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs):

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has four choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONLY ONE is correct.

1. Vegetative propagation is possible by
(a) Root
(b) Stem
(c) Leaves
(d) All of these
2. Ovaries produce
(a) oestrogen and progesterone
(b) oestrogen only
(c) progesterone only
(d) testosterone

(a) ovary
(b) uterus
(c) vagina
(d) fallopian tube

(a) fallopian tube
(b) ampulla
(c) oviduct
(d) vagina

(a) Spermatogonia
(b) Glandular epithelium
(c) Sensory epithelium
(d) Germinal epithelium

(a) male mammals
(b) female mammals
(c) male amphibians
(d) female amphibians
7. Progesterone is secreted by
(a) corpus luteum
(b) thyroid
(c) thymus
(d) testes
8. The general method of asexual reproduction, in yeast fungus is -
(a) by spores
(b) by budding
(c) binary fission
(d) gemma
9. Oral-contraceptives prevent the
(a) fertilization
(b) ovulation
(c) implantation
(d) entrance of sperms in vagina
10. The process of development of organism like itself is called
(a) budding
(b) flowering
(c) reproduction
(d) none of the above
11. Which of the following organisms do not depend on reproduction to exchange genetic information?
(a) animals
(b) plants
(c) bacteria
(d) fungi
12. Like animals, plants produce
(a) many more sperm than eggs.
(b) a few more sperm than eggs.
(c) equal numbers of sperm and eggs.
(d) fewer sperm than eggs.
13. The asexual process replaced by the sexual method is known as:
(a) Semigany
(b) Amphimixis
(c) Apospory
(d) Apomixis
14. The cyclic period of sexual activity in non-human female mammals is called
(a) Menstruation
(b) Luteinization
(c) Oogenesis
(d) Oestrous cycle
15. Characters transmitted from parents to offspring are present in
(a) cytoplasm
(b) ribosome
(c) golgi bodies
(d) genes
16. In sweet potato, vegetative propagation takes place by
(a) $\operatorname{Root}($ b)
Stem
(c) Leaves
(d) Fruit

(a) regeneration
(b) reproduction
(c) healing of wounds
(d) growth

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-133.jpg?height=202&width=239&top_left_y=2145&top_left_x=1356)

(a) Seminal fluid
(b) Vaginal fluid
(c) Uterine lining
(d) Fallopian tube

(a) Oxytocin
(b) Oestrogen
(c) Prolactin
(d) Gonadotrophin

(a) Vitelline membrane
(b) Graafian follicles
(c) Stroma
(d) Germinal epithelium
22. In budding,
(a) Outgrowth develops earlier than nuclear division
(b) Nucleus divides earlier than development of outgrowth
(c) Both occur simultaneously
(d) There is no fixed sequence

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-134.jpg?height=198&width=424&top_left_y=1153&top_left_x=383)
(a) Size of daughter cell to be formed is not appropriate.
(b) Constriction has appeared before nuclear division.
(c) Constriction is not equally deep on the either side.
(d) None of the above

(a) Cytoplasm divides first followed by division of nucleus
(b) Nucleus divides first followed by division of cytoplasm
(c) Both nucleus and cytoplasm divide simultaneously

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-134.jpg?height=113&width=173&top_left_y=1905&top_left_x=264)
(A)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-134.jpg?height=108&width=158&top_left_y=1905&top_left_x=452)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-134.jpg?height=113&width=154&top_left_y=1900&top_left_x=635)

(a) A, B, C, D
(b) A, C, A, B
(c) $\mathrm{B}, \mathrm{D}, \mathrm{A}, \mathrm{C}$
(d) C, A, D, B
26. (A): Vigrous contraction of the uterus at the end of pregnancy causes expulsion.
(B): The stimulatory reflex between the uterine contraction and oxytocin results in weakening contractions.
(a) Both the statements (A) and (B) are correct
(b) Statement (A) is correct but (B) is incorrect.
(c) Both the statements (A) and (B) are incorrect
(d) Statement(B) is correct but (A) is incorrect.
27. (A): The middle piece is called as power house of the sperm.
(B): The numerous mitochondria coiled around axial filament produce energy for the movement of the tail.
(a) Both the statements (A) and (B) are correct
(b) Statement (A) is correct but (B) is incorrect.
(c) Both the statements (A) and (B) are incorrect
(d) Statement(B) is correct but (A) is incorrect.
28. Which of the following cells during gametogenesis is normally dipoid?
(a) Spermatid
(b) Spermatogonia
(c) Secondary polar body
(d) Primary polar body
29. In human females, meiosis II is not complete until?
(a) Fertilisation
(b) Uterine implantation
(c) Birth
(d) Puberty
30. The human embryo gets nutrition from the mother blood with the help of a special organ called
[NTSE]
(a) Zygote
(b) Ovary
(c) Oviduct
(d) Placenta
[NTSE]
31. The source of mammalian hormone 'relaxin' is
(a) ovary
(b) stomach
(c) intestine
(d) pancreas
32. Grafting in monocot plants is not possible because they have
[NTSE]
(a) Parallel venation
(b) Have only one cotyledon
(c) Have cambium
(d) Have scattered vascular bundles
33. In the flowering plants sexual reproduction involves several events beginning with the bud and ending in a fruit. These events are arranged in four different combinations. Select the combination that has the correct sequence of events.
[NTSE]
(a) Embryo, zygote, gametes, fertilization.
(b) Gametes, fertilization, zygote, embryo.
(c) Fertilization, zygote, gametes, embryo.
(d) Gametes, zygote, embryo, fertilization.
34. "Double fertilization" is a complex mechanism of flowering plants that is also unique to angiosperms. Choose the most appropriate statement from the options listed below that explains this phenomenon.
[NTSE]
(a) Fertilization in two flowers of the same plant forming endosperms.
(b) Two male gametes fertilize two eggs inside the ovule as a result the ovary gives rise to bigger fruits.
(c) Two fertilizations occur in a flower-one fertilization results in the formation of a diploid zygote and the second fertilization results in the formation of a triploid endosperm.
(d) Two pollen grains sending two pollen tubes inside the ovary, resulting in the formation of two seeds inside the fruit.
35. During oogenesis in mammals, the second meiotic division occurs
[KVPY]
(a) before fertilisation
(b) after implantation
(c) before ovulation
(d) after fertilisation
36. The correct route that sperm follows when it releases from the testis of a mammal:
[NTSE]
(a) Vas deferens → Epididymis → Urethra
(b) Urethra → Epididymis → Vas deferens
(c) Epididymis → Urethra → Vas deferens
(d) Epididymis → Vas deferens → Urethra

## Multiple Choice Questions: (MCQs)

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has four choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONLY ONE is correct.

1. Intine of pollen grain is made of:
(a) Callose
(b) Pectocellulose
(c) Cellulose
(d) Sporopollenin
2. Exine of pollen grain is formed of:
(a) Callose
(b) Pectocellulose
(c) Lignocellulose
(d) Sporopollenin
3. Wall of a pollen sac consists of:
(a) Endothecium and tapetum
(b) Tapetum and middle layers
(c) Endothecium, middle layers and tapetum
(d) Epidermis, endothecium, middle layers and tapetum
4. Germ pore/germinal furrow present on the surface of pollen grain represents
(a) an area where exine is thin or absent.
(b) specialised thickening of exine.
(c) specialised thickening of intine.
(d) area where tapetum is absent.
5. Female gametophyte of angiosperms is represented by:
(a) Ovule
(b) Megaspore mother cell
(c) Embryo sac
(d) Nucellus
6. The stalk of ovule is:
(a) Pedicel
(b) Funiculus
(c) Petiole
(d) Rachiole
7. Identify those that are associated with the gynoecium.
(a) Stigma, ovule, embryo sac, placenta
(b) Thalamus, pistil, style, ovule
(c) Ovule, ovary, embryo sac, tapetum
(d) Ovule, stamen, ovary, embryo sac
8. Egg apparatus consists of:
(a) Egg cell and antipodal cells
(b) Egg cell and central cell
(c) Egg cell and two synergids
(d) Egg cell and one synergid
9. Embryo sac of flowering plants develops from:
(a) Zygote
(b) Megaspore
(c) Nucellus
(d) Embryo
10. Which one is the female gamete in embryo sac?
(a) Synergid
(b) Antipodal cell
(c) Egg
(d) Central cell
11. Secondary nucleus in the middle of an embryo sac of angiosperms is:
(a) Diploid
(b) Triploid
(c) Tetraploid
(d) Haploid
12. Central cell of embryo sac contains
(a) a single haploid nucleus.
(b) two haploid polar nuclei.
(c) one diploid fusion or secondary nucleus.
(d) Either (b) or (c)
13. The endosperm in angiosperm develops from:
(a) Zygote
(b) Primary endosperm nucleus
(c) Chalazal polar nucleus
(d) Micropylar polar nucleus
14. Anther is typically:
(a) Tetrasporangiate
(b) Bisporangiate
(c) Trisporangiate
(d) Monosporangiate
15. Tapetum present in the microsporangial wall occurs between:
(a) Epidermis and endothecium
(b) Endothecium and middle layers
(c) Epidermis and middle layers
(d) Middle layers and sporogenous tissue
16. Meiosis of megaspore mother cell generally produces:
(a) Linear tetrad
(b) Tetrahedral tetrad
(c) Decussate tetrad
(d) Isobilateral tetrad
17. Function of synergids is to:
(a) attract pollen tube and bear its shock.
(b) fuse with extra male gametes and form endosperm.
(c) produce additional embryo.
(d) protect egg from pathogens.
18. In angiosperms, pollen tubes liberate their male gametes into the:
(a) Central cell
(b) Antipodal cell
(c) Egg cell
(d) Synergids
19. Test-tube baby means a baby born when
(a) it develops from a non-fertilised egg.
(b) it developed in a test-tube.
(c) it is developed through tissue culture method.
(d) the ovum is fertilised externally and thereafter implanted in the uterus.
20. Microspore in angiosperms contain:
(a) Two prothallial cells
(b) One parietal cell
(c) 3 parietal cells
(d) None of these
21. Microsporogenesis occurs:
(a) On margins of leaves
(b) Inside ovule
(c) Inside anther
(d) In essential floral organs
22. To form embryo in vitro, the male gamete is transferred into female gamete directly. Such technique is called $\_\_\_\_$ .
(a) IUI
(b) IUT
(c) ICSI
(d) GIFT
23. Filiform apparatus is characteristic feature of:
[JSTSE]
(a) Nucellar embryo
(b) Aleurone cell
(c) Synergids
(d) Generative cell
24. A childless couple can be assisted to have a child through a technique called GIFT. The full form of this technique is:
(a) Gamete intrafallopian transfer
(b) Gamete internal fertilisation and transfer
(c) Germ cell internal fallopian transfer
(d) Gamete inseminated fallopian transfer
25. The hilum is a scar on the:
(a) Fruit, where it was attached to pedicel.
(b) Fruit, where style was present.
(c) Seed, where micropyle was present.
(d) Seed, where funicle was attached.
26. The hilum is a scar on the:
(a) Fruit, where it was attached to pedicel.
(b) Fruit, where style was present.
(c) Seed, where micropyle was present.
(d) Seed, where funicle was attached.
27. Which of the following statements is not correct?
[JSTSE]
(a) Pollen grains of many species can germinate on the stigma of a flower, but only one pollen tube of the same species grows into the style.
(b) Insects that consume pollen or nectar without bringing about pollination are called pollen/nectar robbers.
(c) Pollen germination and pollen tube growth are regulated by chemical components of pollen interacting with those of the pistil.
(d) Some reptiles have also been reported as pollinators in some plant species.
28. Which one of the following statements is not true?
[JSTSE]
(a) Tapetum helps in the dehiscence of anther.
(b) Exine of pollen grains is made up of sporopollenin.
(c) Pollen grains of many species cause severe allergies.
(d) Stored pollen in liquid nitrogen can be used in the crop breeding programmes.
29. Fertilisation in humans is practically feasible only if:
[JSTSE]
(a) the sperms are transported into vagina just after the release of ovum in fallopian tube.
(b) the ovum and sperms are transported simultaneously to ampullary-isthmic junction of the fallopian tube.
(c) the ovum and sperms are transported simultaneously to ampullary-isthmic junction of the cervix.
(d) the sperms are transported into cervix within 48 hrs of release of ovum in uterus.
30. Capacitation occurs in:
[JSTSE]
(a) Epididymis
(b) Vas deferens
(c) Female reproductive tract
(d) Rete testis
31. Functional megaspore in an angiosperm develops into:
[JSTSE]
(a) Endosperm
(b) Embryo sac
(c) Embryo
(d) Ovule
32. Double fertilisation is:
[JSTSE]
(a) fusion of two male gametes of a pollen tube with two different eggs.
(b) fusion of one male gamete with two polar nuclei.
(c) syngamy and triple fusion.
(d) fusion of two male gametes with one egg.
33. In some plants, the female gamete develops into embryo without fertilisation. This phenomenon is known as:
[JSTSE]
(a) Autogamy
(b) Parthenocarpy
(c) Syngamy
(d) Parthenogenesis
34. Which one of the following statements regarding post-fertilisation development in flowering plants is incorrect?
[JSTSE]
(a) Ovary develops into fruit.
(b) Zygote develops into embryo.
(c) Central cell develops into endosperm.
(d) Ovules develop into embryo sac.
35. Select the correct sequence for transport of sperm cells in male reproductive system:
[JSTSE]
(a) Testis → Epididymis → Vasa efferentia → Rete testis → Inguinal canal → Urethra
(b) Seminiferoustubules → Rete testis → Vasa efferentia → Epididymis → Vas deferens → Ejaculatory duct → Urethra → Urethral meatus
(c) Seminiferous tubules → Vasa efferentia $\rightarrow$ Epididymis → Inguinal canal → Urethra
(d) Testis → Epididymis → Vasa efferentia → Vas deferens → Ejaculatory duct → Inguinal canal → Urethra → Urethral meatus

[JSTSE]
(a) High concentration of Progesterone
(b) Low concentration of LH
(c) Low concentration of FSH
(d) High concentration of Estrogen
37. Meiotic division of the secondary oocyte is completed
[JSTSE]
(a) At the time of copulation
(b) After zygote formation
(c) At the time of fusion of a sperm with an ovum
(d) Prior to ovulation
38. Which of the following hormone levels will cause release of ovum (ovulation) from the graffian follicle?
(a) High concentration of Progesterone
[JSTSE]
(b) Low concentration of LH
(c) Low concentration of FSH
(d) High concentration of Estrogen

[JSTSE]

|  | Column-I |  |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (A) Placenta | Column-II |  |
| (B) Zona pellucida | Androgens |  |
|  | (ii) Human Chorionic |  |
| (C) Bulbo-urethral glands | (iii) Layer of the ovum |  |
| (D) Leydig cells | (iv) Lubrication of the Penis |  |


|  | (A) | (B) | (C) | (D) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (a) | (i) | (iv) | (ii) | (iii) |
| (b) | (iii) | (ii) | (iv) | (i) |

## Very Short Answer Questions:

1. Clone is an individual or group of individuals which is genetically identical to another.
2. Fragmentation is the breaking of an individual into two or more parts each of which grows into a new individual, e.g., Spirogyra.
3. Self-pollination or autogamy is transfer of pollen grains from the anther to the stigma of either the same flower or genetically similar flower.
4. Adolescence is a transitional stage of physical and psychological development that generally occurs during the period from puberty to adulthood.
5. The endometrium is the tissue lining the cavity of the uterus (or womb).
6. Embryonic villi are finger-like outgrowths that form the placenta part from the outer covering of foetus which pass into endometrium for absorption of materials and elimination of waste products.
7. Thalamus is the broad base of the flower which is formed by condensation of internodes and is specialised to bear floral organs close together.
8. Tissue culture is a technique of raising new plants from small pieces of plant tissues over a synthetic nutrient or culture medium under aseptic conditions.
9. Grafting is a horticultural technique of plant propagation in which a small aerial part or scion is inserted into large basal part or stock of another plant in order to form a composite individual.
10. Layering is a horticultural technique of vegetative multiplication where a part of basal shoot is defoliated, injured and pegged down in soil to stimulate root formation.
11. Sporulation is the process of formation of minute unicellular reproductive structures or spores that can undergo dispersal.
12. Multiple fission is a mode of asexual reproduction in unicellular organism where a single unicellular individual divides and splits internally to form a number of daughter individuals, e.g., Plasmodium.
13. Pollination is the process by which the pollen from anther reaches to the stigma of the flower of the same species. Fertilization is the process in which the male gamete fuses with the egg (female gamete) to form zygote.
14. Earthworm.
15. Zygote is formed when male gamete (sperm) fuses with female gamete (ovum).
16. When the menstrual flow stops in females at the age of 50 , that stage is called menopause.
17. The female reproductive system consists of ovaries, fallopian tubes or oviducts, uterus and vagina.
18. Functions of human ovary :
(i) It produces female gamete (ovum).
(ii) It secretes female hormones (oestrogen and progesterone).
19. The two sexually transmitted diseases are gonorrhoea and syphilis.
20. The production of sperms by testis requires temperature slightly lower than the normal body temperature. This temperature is provided by scrotal sac. So, if testis fails to descend in scrotal sacs in human males, then there is a difficulty in the enough sperm formation.
21. Protection of flower in the bud condition and support to other floral organs in open flower, are the main functions of sepals.
22. Semen is a fluid in which sperms travel during ejaculation. Two advantages provided by semen are ease of transportation and nutrition.
23. (i) Oestrogen, (ii) Progesterone
24. (i) Multiplication phase,
(ii) Growth phase, and
(iii) Maturation phase.
25. (i) Amoeba (ii) Hydra
26. Accesory whorls : (i) Calyx (ii) Corolla

Essential whorls. (i) Androecium (ii) Gynoecium
27. (i) Frog (ii) Fishes
28. (i) Cuttings, (ii) Grafting, and (iii) Layering.
29. Insects, wind, water, birds and animals are the different agents of pollination.
30. (i) STD-Sexually Transmitted Disease.
(ii) AIDS - Acquired Immuno Deficiency Syndrome.
(iii) IUCD-Intra Uterine Contraceptive Device.
31. Formation of sperms and to secrete testosterone hormone.
32. Leaf notches.
33. Spirogyra under favourable condition, reproduces by a process known as fragmentation, which is an asexual mode of reproduction.

## Short Answer Questions:

1. Budding is an asexual method of reproduction in Hydra. It takes place during favourable conditions. It usually occurs during summer.
Budding in Hydra involves a small bud which is developed from its parent hydra through the repeated mitotic division of its cells. The small bud then receives its nutrition from the parent and grows healthy. Growth starts by developing small tentacles and the mouth. Finally, the small newly produced Hydra gets separate from its parent Hydra.
2. Unisexual refers to the plant whose flowers contain either stamens (male reproductive organ) or carpels (female reproductive organ) but not both.
Example : Papaya, watermelon.
Bisexual refers to the plant whose flowers contain both stamens (male reproductive organ) and carpels (female reproductive organ). Example : Hibiscus, Mustard.
3. The time period taken from the fertilization till the birth of the child is called gestation period. This period varies from species to species. Like in rat it is of 20 days while in man it is of about 280 days and in elephant it is of about 640 days.
4. The fertility period of males in human beings remains throughout the life and they can produce sperms throughout their life.
In human females, process of ovulation stops at the age of $45-50$ and now this phase is called as menopause. So fertility period in human females is from puberty to 45 50 years. In this way, in a female about 400 eggs reach the uterus in her total fertility period.
5. Difference between asexual and sexual reproduction are as follows-

| S.No. | Asexual Reproduction | Sexual Reproduction |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) | Involves only a single parent. | Involves two parents. |
| (ii) | Faster mode of reproduction. | Much slower mode of reproduction. |
| (iii) | No variations are produced. | Variations are produced as the zygote has genetic material of both the parents. |
| (iv) | Occurs only by mitotic divisions. | First gametes are produced by meiotic divisions and then zygote multiplies by mitotic divisions. |
| (v) | It occurs by fission, budding or fragmentation. | It occurs by the formation of haploid gametes which fuse to form a diploid zygote. |
| (vi) | Individuals formed are genetically identical to the parents. | Individuals formed are not genetically identical and only resemble to both the parents. |

6. The differences between external and internal fertilization are as follows:

| S. No. | Internal Fertilization | External Fertilization |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) | In this case, sperms are injected inside the body of the female. Thus fertilization of the ovum takes place inside the body of the female. Example. Mammals, insects, birds, etc. | In this case, both male and female separate shedding their gametes in the medium (water). Thus the ovum is fertilized outside the body of the female. Example. Frog, toads, etc. |
| (ii) | Internal fertilization takes place in terrestrial animals. | External fertilization takes place in aquatic animals. |
| (iii) | The sperms are released by copulatory organ in the vagina of female. | Water is essential for external fertilization. |

7. Regeneration is the capacity of an organism to grow its lost parts. Vegetative propagation is to grow new plants from parts of a plant using this capacity of regeneration.
8. (i) The two main types of reproduction in living organisms are - Asexual reproduction and Sexual reproduction.
(ii) Asexual reproduction : Amoeba, Yeast Sexual reproduction : Frog, Earthworm.
9. Advantages of Vegetative Propagation :
(i) Plants which produce a few seeds can be grown vegetatively in a short time.
(ii) When the seeds have prolonged dormancy, plants can be raised vegetatively.
(iii) Desirable characteristics can be maintained as plants are genetically identical.
(iv) It is rapid, easier and less expensive method of growing plants.
10. (i) Rhizopus.
(ii) Reproductive part is sporangia Non-reproductive part is hyphae
11. A. Anther : It produces pollen granis.
B. Style : It provides the path through which the pollen tube grows and reaches the ovary.
C. Ovary: It contains ovules and each ovule has an egg cell/female gamete. It develops into fruit after fertilization.
12. Three methods of contraception -
(i) Barrier method or mechanical method/Condom/ Diaphragm, to prevent the meeting of sperms and ova.
(ii) Chemical method / Oral pills - change the hormonal balance of the female partner so that the eggs are not released.
(iii) Surgical method - to block the vas deferens in males /vasectomy or the fallopian tube (oviduct) in females/ tubectomy, to prevent the transfer of sperms or egg and hence no fertilization takes place.
(iv) IUDs/Loop or the copper-T placed in the uterus, to prevent pregnancy.
13. (i) When zygote is implanted in the uterus of mother, the inner lining of the uterus thickens with richly supply blood vessels to provide nourishment to the growing embryo.
(ii) When egg is not fertillised, it survives for one day. Since, the thickened uterus lining is not required; It slowly breaks down and is released out of the vagina as blood and mucous. This is known as menstruation, which lasts for about two to eight days.
14. The difference between asexual and sexual reproduction is that in asexual reproduction only one parent is involved, whereas in the sexual mode of reproduction two parents are involved. An offspring is exactly similar to its parent and in the case of asexual reproduction a single parent gives rise to the offspring.
The organism reproducing sexually have a better chances of survival than asexually because it incorporates variations and promote diversity of characters in an offspring due to combinations of genes and thus the offspring are better adapted to the environment and they survive for a longer period of time.

## Long Answer Questions:

1. (a) (i) The ovaries have two main reproductive functions in the body. They produce oocytes (eggs) for fertilization and they produce the reproductive hormones, oestrogen and progesterone.
(ii) Oviduct (fallopian tube) help transmission the ova from the ovary to the uterus and providing the necessary environment for fertilization and for initial development of the egg or zygote.
(iii) Uterus help in nurturing the fertilized ovum that develops into the foetus and holding it till the baby is mature enough for birth.
(b) The embryo gets nutrition from the mother's blood with the help of a special tissue called placenta. This is a disc which is embedded in the uterine wall. It contains villi on the embryo's side of the tissue. On the mother's side there are blood spaces, which surround the villi. This provides a large surface area for glucose and oxygen to pass from the mother to the embryo. The developing embryo will also generate waste substances which can be removed by transferring them into the mother's blood through the placenta. The development of the child inside the mother's body takes approximately nine months. The child is born as a result of rhythmic contractions of the muscles in the uterus.
2. (i) The organ that produces sperms and secretes male hormones is testis. The hormone secreted by testis is testosterone.

## Function of testosterone include:

(a) Stimulation of sperm production.
(b) Stimulation of the development of secondary sexual characters in males.
(c) Development, maturation and functioning of accessory sex organs like vas deferens and seminal vesicles.
(ii) Fallopian tubes
(iii) The embryo gets nourishment from the mother's blood with the help of a special tissue called placenta. This tissue is embedded in the uterine wall, containing villi on the embryo's side of the tissue. On the mother's side blood spaces are present which surround the villi. This provides a large surface area for glucose and oxygen to pass from the mother to the embryo.
3. Pollination is the transfer of pollen grains from the anther of one flower to the stigma of the same or another flower. It is said to be the first process of sexual fertilization in flowering plants. Pollen grains contain the male gamete and are present in the anthers of the flower.
Types of Pollination- There are two types of pollination:

- Self pollination: It takes place when the pollen of one flower are pollinated to the same flower or other flowers of the same plant.
- Cross pollination: It is also known as allogamy. Cross pollination occurs when pollen grains are transferred from the flower of one plant to the flower of different plant.
Agents of pollination: Wind, water or animals.
Pollination leading to fertilisation: After the pollen lands on a suitable stigma, the male gametes present in it has to reach the female germ cells present in the ovary. For this, a tube grows out of the pollen grain and travels through the style to reach the ovary. Once pollen tube reaches the ovary, it enters inside and bursts to release male gametes. This is followed by union of male and female gametes to produce a zygote. This process is known as fertilisation. After fertilisation, the zygote divides several times to form an embryo within the ovule. This is how a suitable pollination leads to fertilisation.


## OR

(a) The given diagram is of female reproductive system. In the given diagram, 1 is labelled as oviduct or Fallopian tube, 2 as Ovary, 3 as uterus (cavity), 4 as Cervix and 5 as Vagina.
