(b) Contraception (birth control) is an artificial method or technique to prevents pregnancy by interfering with the normal process of ovulation, fertilisation, and implantation. There are different kinds of birth control that act at different points in the process.
Three advantages of adopting contraceptive measures are as follows:
(i) It reduces the risk of developing certain reproductive cancers.
(ii) It helps in controlling the population.
(iii) It helps in preventing the unintended pregnancy.

4 (a) Germination of pollen on stigma of a flower:-
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-140.jpg?height=618&width=510&top_left_y=906&top_left_x=1181)
(b) A pollen tube is a tubular structure produced by the male gametophyte of seed plants when it germinates. Pollen tubes elongation is an integral stage in the plant life cycle. The pollen tube acts helps in the transportation of the male gamete cells from the pollen grain-either from the stigma (in flowering plants) to the ovules at the base of the pistil or directly through ovule tissue in some gymnosperms. In maize, this single cell can grow longer than 30 cm to transverse the length of the pistil.
(c) (i) Seed - Ovules
(ii) Fruit - ovary

## Reasoning Based Questions:

1. To grow plants by cutting rather than from seeds is a method of artificial asexual reproduction. It is considered better to grow a rose from cutting rather than from seeds due to following reasons:
(i) It is the only method of reproduction in seedless plants.
(ii) We get a fully matured plant in a very short time.
(iii) It is a short cut method for rapid multiplication.
(iv) It helps to preserve the type of character that a plant breeder desires.
(v) It is a very easy and economical method for the multiplication of ornamental plants.
2. Hyphae for digestion of bread nutrients secrete enzymes. For their working and absorption of digested food, hyphae require moisture. Moistened bread slice offers both moisture and nutrients to the bread mould, hence, it grows profusely. Dry slice of bread offers nutrients but not moisture hence, hyphae fail to grow.
3. In a flower, fertilisation requires both male and female gametes.
If pollination does not occur, male gamete is not available, hence, fertilisation would take place.
4. Mouse parents produce mouse-like offspring because their reproductive cells contain chromosomes with correctly coded nucleic acid, i.e., reproduction is specific.
5. The moist piece of bread is an ideal substratum for growth of Rhizopus or bread mould. The spores of this fungus are nearly always present in the air. Some of them settle over the bread, germinate and form mycelia which appear as cottony growths. Later on a number of black spots develop. They represent sporangia.
6. The fields have dry stems of the old grass plants all over. These dry stems have buds in an inactive state. By getting rainwater, the bud present on the dry grass stem gets activated and grow to produce new grass plants.
7. Budding, fragmentation and regeneration are considered as asexual types of reproduction because:
(i) They involve only one parent,
(ii) Gametes are not involved in reproduction.
(iii) Meiotic division does not take place at any stage of this type of reproduction.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-141.jpg?height=545&width=501&top_left_y=1473&top_left_x=402)
Fig. Regeneration in Planaria

8. The testes to be situated in the scrotal sac outside the main body cavity because the formation of sperms require a low temperature than the normal body temperature. Its disadvantage of being outside the body cavity is that it is more prone to injury.

## HOTS Questions:

1. General growth refers to different types of developmental process in the body like increase in height, weight gain, changes in shape and size of the body but sexual maturation is specific changes that reflected at puberty like cracking of voice, new hair patterns, development of breast in female, etc.
2. A piece of potato tuber having an eye possesses bud which sprouts to form a new plant. Piece of tuber without an eye does not form a new plant due to absence of bud.
3. Cowper's gland secrete an alkaline fluid which helps to neutralise the acidic environment of the male urethra and female reproductive tract. Removal of cowper's gland thus would inactivate and kill sperms in male urethra and female reproductive tract.
4. A few points for prevention of AIDS are:
(i) Use condoms while having sex.
(ii) Pregnant ladies should be checked.
(iii) Use disposable syringe.
(iv) Blood transfusion should be done carefully.

# Case Study Based MCQs <br> (CBSE Sample Issued 2021) <br> Male Reproduction System

## Text - Book Questions:

1. Variations are differences among individuals produced as a result of errors caused during DNA copying at the time of cell division. If a reproducing organism is suitable to a particular niche and niche drastically changed then the population could be wiped out and got adversely affected. The variants may have the ability to survive under adverse condition. The surviving organism may reproduce and develop a kind of population which is suited for their niche. This shows that the species is able to survive but the individual lacking suitable features failed to do so. Thus, variation is beneficial to the species but not necessarily for the individual.
2. Spore formation is a method of asexual reproduction which is found in non - flowering plants like fungi (Rhizopus) and bacteria, in which the parent plant produces hundreds of tiny spores which turn into a new plant.
An organism can be benefitted in following ways if it produces through spores:
(i) Spores are thick walled which protect the organism from unfavourable condition and remain dormant till favourable conditions become available.
(ii) Since it is a form of asexual reproduction, so the new organism won't lose its parental characters.
(iii) At a time, several numbers of spores are produced so cannot extinct very easily. Hence, numerous plants can be grown and does not require any medium for dispersal.
(iv) Spores are light weighted so they can be easily transfered from one plant to other for germination.
3. Regeneration is the process carried out by specialised cells which involves the formation of new individual from its body parts. These cells proliferate and make large numbers of cells. From this mass of cells, different
cells undergo changes to become various cell types and tissues. Since the simple organisms comprise of only one type of cells which assist that organism to grow and develop in Hydra and Planaria.
Complex organism have organ - system level of organisation in which all the organs of organ system work as an interconnected unit. They can regenerate their body parts such as skin, muscles etc. but cannot give rise to new individual through regeneration.
4. Vegetative propagation is practiced for growing certain types of plants because:
(i) It is the only method of reproduction in those plants which have lost their capacity to produce seeds like banana, orange, rose, jasmine etc.
(ii) Plants raised by vegetative propagation can bear flowers and fruits earlier than those produced from seeds.
(iii) Plants produced by this method are genetically similar enough to the parent plant to have all its characteristics.
(iv) Vegetative propagation is used in methods such as layering or grafting to grow many plants like sugarcane, roses, or grapes for agricultural purposes.
5. DNA copying is the biological process of producing two identical copies of DNA from one original DNA molecule. DNA are the carriers of genetic information which transfers the information or characters from parents to offspring. It also helps to generates variations during sexual reproduction and leads to evolution. This explains how DNA copying is an essential part of the process of replication.
6. Difference between pollination and fertilisation are as follows-

| S.No. | Pollination | Fertilisation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) | Pollination is the process of transferring pollen grains from the male anther to female stigma. | Fertilisation is fusion of male and female gametes to form a new organism of the same species. |
| (ii) | It occurs in the early stages of sexual reproduction in all flowering plants. | It is preceded by the pollination in all flowering plants. |
| (iii) | It requires external factors like wind, birds, insects, animals etc. | It does not require any external factor and can be achieved by the growth of the pollen tube. |
| (iv) | In pollination, there is no formation of pollen tube occur. | In fertilisation, pollen tube is formed for transferring male gametes into an egg cell. |
| (v) | Pollination occurs only in plants. | Fertilisation occurs in almost in every living organism like plants, humans, animals, insects etc. |

7. Seminal vesicles are one pair which lies at the base of urinary bladder behind its neck. They secrete a viscous fluid for nourishment of sperms in human male.
Prostate Gland : It is single in number which surrounds the first part of urethra. Its secretion is poured into urethra by many ducts.
It secretes alkaline fluid which is discharged into urethra and neutralize any acidity caused due to residual urine. Both the parts add their secreations so that the sperms mix with the fluid which makes their transport easier.
8. The changes seen in girls at the time of puberty are as follows:

- Pelvic region and the overall fat content of the body increases.
- The waist narrows due to more deposition of fat on the hips under the influence of hormones.
- Mammary gland starts developing with dark nipples at the tip of breast.
- Pubic hairs appear in the genital area, underarms, face, hands, and legs.
- At the time of puberty, menstrual cycle begins.
- The size of uterus and ovary increases.
- More secretion of oil from the skin, which results in the appearance of pimples.

9.     - No, copper-T will not help a woman to protect her from sexually transmitted disease.

- The copper-T is a simple intra-uterine device (IUD) made of a flexible, "T" shaped piece of plastic wrapped with a thin copper containing wire. It only prevents the implantation of the embryo in the uterus. As copper- T does not prevent entry of semen so it is not able to protect a woman from sexually transmitted diseases (STDs).


## Text - Book Exercise:

1. (c) Ovary, uterus and fallopian tube are the part of female reproductive system but vas deferens is a part of male reproductive system in human beings. The sperms are delivered through the vas deferens which unites with a tube coming from the urinary bladder.
2. (d) Stamens are the male reproductive parts of flowers. A stamen consists of an anther (which produces pollen) and a filament.
3. Menstruation, or period, is normal vaginal bleeding that occurs as part of a woman's monthly cycle. Every month, one egg is released by an ovary and if the egg is not fertilised, it lives for about one day. Since the ovary releases one egg every month, the uterus also prepares itself every month to receive a fertilised egg. Thus its lining becomes thick and spongy. The main purpose of this lining is to provide nourishment to the embryo. Now, however, this lining is not needed any longer. So, the lining slowly breaks and comes out through the vagina as blood and mucous which causes menstruation.
4. 

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-143.jpg?height=414&width=533&top_left_y=379&top_left_x=331)
Fig.: Longitudinal section of a flower

5. Reproduction is a life process which enables an organism to reproduce its own offspring. Thus, they continue their species without extinction. Reproduction helps in providing stability to populations of species by considering following points:

- It provides stability to population of species by giving birth to new individuals as the rate of birth must be at par with the rate of death to provide stability to population of a species.
- The consistency of DNA copying during reproduction is also important for the maintenance of body design features that allow the organism to use that particular niche. Therefore, reproduction is linked to the stability of populations of species.

6. Testes is a part of both male reproductive system and endocrine system. They are located behind the penis in a pouch of skin called the scrotum. The functions performed by the testes in human beings are as follows:

- It helps in the production of sperms in male which takes part in sexual reproduction.
- It assists in the secretion of the testosterone hormone, which plays a key role in the development of secondary sexual characters in male.

7. Unicellular organisms are single celled organisms in which all the physiological activities like reproduction, respiration excretion etc. are performed by a single cell. Multicellular organisms are those which consist of more than one cell, in contrast to unicellular organisms. Therefore, modes of reproduction are different in unicellular and multicellular organism as:

- Unicellular organisms always reproduce by asexual methods like budding, regeneration, spore formation, binary fission, multiple fission, etc. Multicellular organism, on the other hand, uses both sexual and asexual method for reproduction.
- In unicellular organism, there is only single cell which perform all the activities so there is no specialised cells and tissues that are meant for reproduction. But in multicellular organism, they have well developed reproductive organ which takes part in reproduction.

8. Contraceptive method is any method, medicine, or device used to prevent pregnancy. It includes mechanical barrier method, hormonal method, surgical method, IUDs, etc.
The reasons for adopting contraceptive methods are as follows:

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-143.jpg?height=186&width=81&top_left_y=1868&top_left_x=1370)
D

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-143.jpg?height=188&width=84&top_left_y=1867&top_left_x=1500)
E

(i) Population control - It helps in controlling the population rate or birth rate.
(ii) Prevents unwanted pregnancies - It helps to maintain a women's health by preventing unwanted pregnancies and by keeping gap between consecutive pregnancies.
(iii) Prevents from STDs - There are few contraceptive methods like condoms which prevents spread of sexually transmitted diseases and lethal diseases like HIV/AIDS.
(iv) It assists in family planning - Family planning/ contraception reduces the need for abortion, especially unsafe abortion.

1. No, we can't graft an apple to a pear tree successfully. Apple and pear belong to the same family, but they do not have the same genus. Grafting requires both stock and scion to be botanically compatible.

## Case Study-2

1. Once a sperm comes in contact with an egg, the acrosome and cortical layers of both undergo several changes to avoid entry of any other sperm.
2. Menstrual bleeding results from the breakdown of thick vascular lining of the uterus which is required during pregnancy. Once a female gets pregnant, this lining doesn't shed and the bleeding doesn't occur.
3. The natural/traditional methods are the healthiest as they have no side effects.
4. At puberty, the sexual maturation occurs and the hormonal changes take place. The primary oocyte completes meiosis I and forms a secondary oocyte. The lining of uterus is thickened and in the absence of pregnancy, it sheds off resulting in menstruation

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-146.jpg?height=1716&width=1314&top_left_y=820&top_left_x=385)

## HEREDITY

Transmission of characters (resemblances as well as variations) from parents to the offspring from one generation to another is called heredity.
Although the offsprings inherit characters (or traits) of the parents and resemble them very closely, but the resemblance is not identical in all respects. The offsprings are never a true copy of the parents. In fact, no two individuals are exactly alike and the members of any one species differ from one another in some traits. The differences in the traits shown by the individuals of a species, and also by the offsprings of the same parents, are referred to as variations.
For example, in human beings, few people have hanging earlobes called free earlobe. In others, the earlobe is closely attached to the side of the head and is called closely attached earlobe. So free earlobes and attached earlobes are the two variations found in humans.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-147.jpg?height=346&width=462&top_left_y=781&top_left_x=831)
Fig. Variation in earlobe

## Accumulation of Variation during Reproduction :

We see different organisms around us. They all look different as species differ from each other. But when you look for differences among individuals of the same species, no two individuals are identical to each other. We acquire a common body design along with subtle changes. For example, a bacterium produces two bacteria by asexual reproduction. One of the offspring bacterium has a variation due to which it has higher tolerance of heat. This characteristic of heat resistance will go on accumulating in the offsprings of successive generations of this bacterium, and this will ultimately give rise to a variant of bacteria which will be highly heat resistant and would be able to survive even at very high temperatures. The advantage of variation to a species is that it increases the chance of its survival in a changing environment.
Only minor differences will be generated due to small inaccuracies in DNA copying mechanism. The diversity is quite high in case of sexually reproducing organisms because of reshuffling of traits during gamete formation and chance fusion of gametes during fertilisation. If the DNA comes from two parents, the differences will be pronounced. These differences will be passed on to the offsprings and would create diversity.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-147.jpg?height=610&width=874&top_left_y=1609&top_left_x=624)

Fig. Creation of diversity over succeeding generations. The original organism at the top will give rise to, say, two individuals, similar in body design, but with subtle differences. Each of them, in turn, will give rise to two individuals in the next generation. Each of the four individuals in the bottom row will be different from each other. While some of these differences will be unique, others will be inherited from their respective parents, who were different from each other. Variations arise either due to an environmental influence or genetic factors like recombination of genes, mutation etc. The environmental factors influence the phenotype of individuals and these are not heritable. Genetic traits affect the genes and are heritable and important from evolutionary perspective.

Few examples of genetic traits in human beings and their variations are-

| S.No. | Genetic Trait | Variations |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Earlobes | Free, Attached |
| 2 | Forelock | White, Not white |
| 3 | Dimples | Dimples, No dimples |
| 4 | Thumb | Straight, curved |
| 5 | Pinky finger | Bent, straight |
| 6 | Mid-digit hair | Mid-digit hair, No mid-digit hair |
| 7 | Toes | 2nd toe longer than big toe, 2nd toe shorter than big toe |
| 8 | Tongue rolling | Rolling up edges, Not rolling |

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-148.jpg?height=147&width=348&top_left_y=849&top_left_x=196)

1. Does random fertilization contribute to genetic variation?
2. Why is genetic variation significant?
3. What is the relationship between meiosis and genetic variation?

## SOLUTION

1. Random fertilization promotes random combination of gametes resulting in genetically unique zygote and hence variation.
2. Genetic variation helps in survival of species to a changing environment or natural selection. It also forms the basis of evolution.
3. Crossing over occurs during meiosis which may result in genetic variation.
4. Observe the eye color of all your friends.
5. Carefully note the eye color of their siblings and parents.
6. Correlate the eye color of your friends and their siblings to their respective parents.
7. Based on this observation, try to suggest an inheritance pattern of eye color in humans.

## Rules for the Inheritance of Traits: Mendel's Contribution

The study of genetics was started by Gregor Johann Mendel, an Austrian Monk, in 1850s. However, his studies were not recognised by the world till after his death. Mendel is known as 'Father of Genetics'.
Before Mendel, although people knew that parents with blue eyes would get blue eyed children, etc., they did not know the exact mechanism of transfer of these traits from the parents to the offspring. They did not know that there is a physical basis of heredity. This was identified by Mendel through his pioneering work in pea plant (Pisum sativum). He blended his knowledge of science and mathematics and was the first one to keep count of individuals exhibiting a particular trait in each generation.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-148.jpg?height=385&width=323&top_left_y=2037&top_left_x=854)
Fig. Gregor Johann Mendel

## Mendel's experimental material.

Mendel picked common garden pea plant for his experiment as they can be grown easily in large numbers and their reproduction can be manipulated. Pea plants have both male and female reproductive organs and they can either self-pollinate themselves or can cross-pollinate with another plant. Mendel was able to selectively cross-pollinate pure bred plants with particular traits and observe the outcome over many generations.

## Learn More

Geneticists today carry out breeding experiments with species that reproduce much more rapidly so that the amount of time and money required is reduced. Fruit flies and bacteria are commonly used for this purpose now. Fruit flies reproduce in about 2 weeks from birth, while bacteria E.coli reproduce in $3-5$ hours.

Through selective cross-breeding of pea over several generations, Mendel discovered that certain traits show up in offsprings without any blending of parent characteristics. Seven traits were observed by Mendel in pea plant and these could occur in one of the two forms. (dominant or recessive)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-149.jpg?height=442&width=597&top_left_y=959&top_left_x=767)
Fig. Pisum Sativum

Fig. Pisum Sativum
| S. No. | Character | Dominant | Recessive |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Stem height | Tall | Dwarf |
| 2 | Flower colour | violet | White |
| 3 | Flower position | Axial | Terminal |
| 4 | Pod shape | Inflated | Constricted |
| 5 | Pod colour | Green | Yellow |
| 6 | Seed shape | Round | Wrinkled |
| 7 | Seed colour | Yellow | Green |


Mendel proposed that something is passed on, unchanged, from parents to offsprings over successive generations, and called it factors. These factors are now known as genes, which are units of inheritance and contain information that is required to express a particular trait. Genes which code for a pair of contrasting traits are called alleles (alternative form of a gene that is located at a specific position on a specific chromosome). When the alleles of a pair are same, they are called homozygous. When the alleles of a pair are different; one masks the expression of other, is termed as heterozygous. The allele which expresses itself even in the presence of another allele is called dominant, and is represented by the first capital letter of the trait. For example; in case of a tall pea plant, dominant allele is represented by $\mathbf{T}$. On the other hand, an allele that fails to express itself in the presence of a dominant allele is called recessive. It is represented by the small letter of the corresponding dominant trait. For example, the dwarf pea plant is represented by $\mathbf{t}$.
Punnett square (devised by R.C. Punnett) is used to predict the outcome of a breeding experiment. It gives the probability for genotypes (genetic makeup) and not the phenotypes (actual expressed traits or morphological characteristics).

## Mendel's Monohybrid cross

Mendel crossed tall and dwarf pea plants (Parental or P generation) to study the inheritance of one gene/single trait. He collected seeds as a result of this cross and grew them to generate plants of first hybrid generation called Filial progeny or F1 generation. Mendel found that all the F1 progeny plants were tall; they all resembled to one of their parents and none were dwarf. He then self-pollinated the F1 progeny and found that some of the plants were dwarf. To his surprise, the trait which was absent in F1 generation reappeared in $\mathbf{F 2}$ generation. The proportion of tall plants was $3 / 4$ and the dwarf plants were 1/4. All plants were either tall or dwarf and none in between; there was no blending of characteristics in the progeny. The ratios of tall to dwarf plants for F2 generation were 3:1 (phenotypic ratio) and 1:2:1 (genotypic ratio). Mendel found similar results for other traits of the pea plants.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-150.jpg?height=659&width=499&top_left_y=376&top_left_x=774)

## Mendel's Dihybrid cross

Mendel also crossed two plants that differed in two traits. For example, he crossed seeds with round shaped and yellow color (RRYY) to the seeds with wrinkled shape and green color (rryy). Yellow color and round shape are dominant traits while green color and wrinkled shape were recessive characters. In F1 generation, both yellow and green seeds as well as round and wrinkled seeds were present in 3:1 ratio. When F1 generation was self-pollinated, the phenotypes round, yellow; wrinkled, yellow; round, green and wrinkled, green appeared in the ratio $\mathbf{9 : 3 : 3 : 1}$. Such a ratio was also observed for several other pairs of characters that Mendel studied.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-150.jpg?height=958&width=924&top_left_y=1414&top_left_x=550)
Fig. Dihybrid cross

## CASE STUDY-1 : Dihybrid Cross

|  | RY | Ry | rY | ry | Round/Yellow: <br> Round/green: <br> Wrinkled/yellow: <br> Wrinkled/green: <br> 9:3:3:1 phenotypic ratio <br> 9 <br> 3 <br> 3 <br> 1 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| RY | RRYY | RRYy | RrYY | RrYy |  |
| Ry | RRYy | RRyy | RrYy | Rryy |  |
| rY | RrYY | RrYy | rrYY | rrYy |  |
| ry | RrYy | Rryy | rrYy | rryy |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |

CASE - I : In a certain plant, red fruit $(R)$ is dominant over yellow fruit $(r)$ and tallness $(T)$ is dominant over shortness (t). If a plant with RRTt is crossed with a plant rrtt genotype, what will be the percentage of tall plants with red fruit in the progeny?

If a plant with RRTt is crossed with a plant rrtt genotype is done then,

| Parent: | RRTt | x | rrtt |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Gametes: | $\mathrm{RT} \quad \mathrm{Rt}$ |  | rt |
| Offspring: | RrTt |  | Rrtt |
|  | (Tall with |  | (Dwarf with |
|  | red fruit) |  | red fruit) |

Ratio $=1: 1$
So the percentage of tall plants with reds fruit in the progeny is $50 \%$.

## Think Out of the Box

$\mathrm{F}_{2}$ generation is 7520 . What will be the actual number of the following plants?
(ii) Heterozygous plants with flowers in axial position.

CASE - II : In a particular plant, two genes control leaf shape and color. Round leaves (R) are dominant over jagged leaves (r). Yellow fruits ( $Y$ ) are dominant over white fruits (y). A true bred yellow-fruited plant with rounded leaves is crossed with true bred white fruited plant with jagged leaves. What are the genotypes of the plants involved in this cross?

A true bred plant is the one that produces offspring with the same traits, when self-fertilised. True-breeding organisms have identical alleles for specified traits with the parents of opposite phenotypes. So, the genotypes involved in this type of a cross will be RRYY x rryy.

CASE - III : In a dihybrid cross between pod color (yellow and green) and shape (inflated and constricted), what will be the proportion of the progeny will have yellow color and constricted shape of a pod?

The phenotypic ratio of a dihybrid cross is 9:3:3:1. So, the proportion of the progeny having yellow color and constricted shape of a pod will be $1 / 16$.
Q. 1 If 400 plants are produced in $\mathrm{F}_{2}$ generation of Mendel's dihybrid cross, what will be the number of non-parental phenotypes among them?
Q. 2 Scientist crossed the $\mathrm{F}_{1}$ generation of true bred parents for flower color (violet and white) and position (axial and terminal). The number of offsprings obtained in
(i) Plants with white flowers in axial position.

## CONNECTING TOPIC

## Mendel's Laws of Inheritance

Mendel's observations of his experiments led to the principles or laws:

1. Law of Dominance - It states that dominant alleles are always repressed by recessive alleles. Hence, the cross between homozygous dominant and homozygous recessive plants will always result in dominant phenotype with heterozygous genotype.
2. Law of Segregation: According to this law, for any particular trait, the pair of alleles of each parent separate and only one allele passes from each parent to an offspring. Paternal allele is inherited as a matter of chance (we now know that segregation of alleles occurs during the process of meiosis).
3. Law of Independent Assortment: This is also known as 'Inheritance law'. According to this law, different pairs of alleles are passed to offsprings independently of each other. Today, we know this is due to the fact that the genes for independently assorted traits are located on different chromosomes.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-152.jpg?height=83&width=104&top_left_y=918&top_left_x=968)

## Learn More

Back cross - A cross of F1 hybrid with one of the parents.
Test cross - It is a type of back cross where F1 hybrid is crossed with homozygous recessive parents. It is called a 'test cross' because it is used to make sure whether the dominant character is due to homozygous or heterozygous genotype.
A test cross conducted for a single pair of contrasting traits results in a ratio of $1: 1$.

CROSS: Heterozygous tall (male) Homozygous dwarf (female)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-152.jpg?height=134&width=940&top_left_y=1235&top_left_x=534)

F1 generation:
(Punette square)

|  | T | t |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| t | Tt | tt |
| t | Tt | tt |

Genotype: Tt : $\mathrm{tt}=1: 1$
Phenotype: Tall ( Tt ) : Dwarf $(\mathrm{tt})=1: 1$
A test cross conducted for two pairs of contrasting traits results in the ratio of $1: 1: 1: 1$

| Phenotypes | Round Yellow |  | × | Wrinkled Green |  |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Genotypes | RrYy |  | rryy |  |  |
| Gametes | RY, Ry, rY, ry |  | ry |  |  |
| Genotypes |  | RY | Ry | rY | ry |
|  | □ <br> ry | RrYy | Rryy | rrYy | rryy |


| Phenotypes | Round <br> Yellow | Round <br> Green | Wrinkled <br> Yellow | Wrinkled <br> Green |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Proportions | $1 / 4$ or $25 \%$ | $1 / 4$ or $25 \%$ | $1 / 4$ or $25 \%$ | $1 / 4$ or $25 \%$ |

## How are Characteristics (or traits) Transmitted to Progeny

Genes are responsibe for the traits of organisms. The characteristics or traits of parents are transmitted to their progeny through genes present on their chromosomes during the process of sexual reproduction. Deoxyribose Nucleic Acid (DNA) carries the genetc information.

## Learn More

- Mendelian trait is controlled by a single locus and shows simple Mendelian inheritance pattern. In such a case, a mutation in a single gene can cause a disease that is inherited according to Mendel's law. e.g. sickle cell anemia.
- According to the latest findings there are nearly 30,000 genes in humans, collectively called the genome.
- Genes are located on chromosomes and they determine the hereditary characteristics in organisms.


## CONNECTING TOPIC

## Deoxyribonucleic Acid (DNA)

Structure of DNA was determined by X-ray diffraction studies. A double helix model of DNA was proposed by Watson and Crick in 1953. They suggested that:
(i) Each DNA molecule consists of two polynucleotide chains.
(ii) The chains are helically coiled around a common axis.
(iii) Each chain is complementary to another one.
(iv) There are 10 bases per turn of helix and one complete turn of helix is of $3.4 \AA$.
(v) The diameter of the DNA molecule is $20 \AA$.

The DNA molecule is a polymer consisting of several thousand pairs of nucleotide monomers. Each nucleotide consists of the pentose sugar deoxyribose a phosphate group and a nitrogenous base which may be either purine or pyrimidine. The two purines are Adenine (A) and guanine (G). The two pyrimidines are thymine (T) and Cytosine (C).
Deoxyribose and a nitrogeneous base together form a nucleoside. A nucleoside and a phosphate together form a nucleotide.

$$
\left[\begin{array}{l}
\text { Nucleoside }=\text { Deoxyribose }+ \text { Nitrogenous base } \\
\text { Nucleotide }=\text { Deoxyribose }+ \text { Nitrogenous base }+ \text { Phosphate }
\end{array}\right]
$$

Deoxyribose is a five carbon sugar molecule, which forms the phosphate backbone of the DNA molecule.
Genes are arranged linearly along long chains of DNA sequence, called chromosomes. The DNA of the chromosome is associated with proteins that organise, compact and control the access to the DNA, forming a material called chromatin. In eukaryotes, chromatin is composed of nucleosomes - segments of DNA wound around histone protein. The complete set of hereditary material in an organism i.e., the combined DNA sequences of all chromosomes is called genome.
Haploid organisms have only one copy of each chromosome while diploid organisms (most animals and many plants) contain two of each chromosome i.e., they have two copies of every gene. The two alleles for a gene are located on identical loci of sister chromatid, each allele inherited from a different parent. Thus each gamete or germ cell has one chromosome from each pair which may be either maternal or paternal. When the two germ cells combine, the normal number of chromosomes is restored in the progeny. This ensures the stability of chromosome number and DNA of a species.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-153.jpg?height=531&width=595&top_left_y=1959&top_left_x=753)
Fig. Structure of Chromosome

## How do genes control traits?

Genes correspond to regions with DNA, a molecule composed of 4 types of nucleotides. The sequence of these nucleotides contains the informations that organisms inherit. DNA is a double stranded structure, each strand acts as a template for creating a new complementary strand.
The sequence of nucleotides in a gene is translated by cells to produce a chain of amino acids, creating proteins. The order of amino acids in a protein corresponds to the order of nucleotides in the gene. This relationship between nucleotide sequence and amino acid sequence is known as genetic code. The amino acids in a protein determine how it folds into a three-dimensional shape. This structure is, in turn, responsible for the protein's function. Proteins carry out almost all the functions needed for cells to live. A change in the DNA can change amino acid it codes and thus, changing its shape and function. This can have a serious effect in the cell and on the organism as a whole e.g., sickle-cell anaemia, which is caused due to change in a single nucleotide.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-154.jpg?height=718&width=330&top_left_y=767&top_left_x=792)
Fig. DNA

## Sex Determination

## Sex determination is a biological system that determines the development of sexual characters in an organism.

Most sexual organisms have two sexes: Males and females. Genetically, both the sexes have different alleles or even different genes that specify their sexual morphology. In humans, the total chromosome complement is $46 ; 44$ of which are autosomes while 2 distinct chromosomes are the sex chromosomes, which determine the sex of an organism and various sex-linked characteristics.
In humans, sex is predetermined in the sperm gamete. The egg gamete mother cell is said to be homogametic because all its cells possess the XX sex chromosomes, sperm gametes are said to be heterogametic because around half of them contain the X -chromosome and others possess the Y -chromosome to compliment the first X -chromosome.
There are two possibilities that can occur during fertilisation between male and female gametes, ( XX and XY ).
All offsprings will inherit an X chromosome from their mother regardless of whether they are boys or girls. Thus, the sex of the children will be determined by what they inherit from their father.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-154.jpg?height=449&width=824&top_left_y=2051&top_left_x=593)
Fig. Sex determination in human beings by Checker Board Method

Many sex-determination systems exist. In a number of insects including grasshoppers, cockroaches and crickets, there is a variation where the females have two copies of the sex chromosome (XX), but males have only one (XO). The 'O' denotes the absence of the second sex chromosome. In some species of reptiles like turtles, the sex is determined by the temperature at which the egg is incubated. In tropical clown fish, the dominant individual in a group becomes female while the others are male. Some species have no sex determination system. Certain species of snails and common earthworm are hermaphrodite. A few species of insects, fishes and reptiles reproduce by parthenogenesis and are females only.

## CONNECTING TOPIC

## Karyotype

It is the entire chromosome complement of an individual or cell. A karyotype includes the detailed morphological structure of each chromosome of the set. In man, 23 pairs of homologous chromosomes are present.
A diagram depicting the arrangement of homologous chromosomes in the decreasing order of their size is called idiogram. Or a pictorial depiction of karyotype is called idiogram.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-155.jpg?height=833&width=711&top_left_y=735&top_left_x=1160)

## EVOLUTION

Evolution is the product of two opposing forces- Processes that constantly introduce variation in traits, and processes that make particular variants become more common or rare. The main cause of variation is mutation, gene flow or reshuffling of genes in sexual reproduction. Natural selection occurs and only few individuals in each generation will survive, as resources are limited and organisms produce more offsprings than their environment can support. Over many generations, mutations produce small random changes in traits which are filtered by Natural Selection and the beneficial changes are retained. This causes adjustment of traits so that they become suited to an organism's environment. These adjustments are called adaptations. However, not every trait is an adaptation.
Evolution: A gradual development of more complex species from pre-existing simpler forms.

## Organic Evolution

The word evolution has been derived from the Latin word evoluere which means 'to unroll'. It can be defined as the gradual unfolding of organisms from pre existing ones through change. The origin of life is inseparably associated with evolution. In the following section, you will study about evolution of the living organisms called organic evolution.

## CONNECTING TOPIC

## Lamarckism

Jean Baptist Lamarck (1744-1829) tried to explain the principle behind the process of evolution in the book "Philosophie Zoologique". Lamarck proposed the "Theory of inheritance of acquired characters". According to this theory-
(a) Environment keeps on changing and thus, influence the organism by creating new needs. The new needs are met with by producing new structures. The changed environment put certain old organs out of use.
(b) A constant use of an organ results in its better devlopment whereas disuse leads to its ultimate degeneration and loss.
(c) The modifications produced during the whole life of an organism are transferred to its offspring. As the process is continued, after several generations, a new form (species) will be formed.

## Darwin's Theory of Evolution

Charles Darwin (1809-1882) gave this theory in his book entitled. 'The origin of Species'. Darwin's theory presumes "descent with modification" i.e., complex creatures evolve from more simplistic ancestors over time. Genetic mutations occur within an organism's genetic code; the beneficial mutations are preserved because they help in survival, a process known as "Natural Selection". These beneficial mutations are passed on to the next generation. Over the time, beneficial mutations accumulate and the result is an entirely different organism.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-156.jpg?height=474&width=364&top_left_y=1137&top_left_x=831)
Fig. Charles Robert Darwin

Darwin found that some fossils resembled present day forms while others were different, though related. Plants and animals of different places were different, but they had the same type of climate. In Galapagos islands, he fould different species of finches (sparrow-like birds) having varied forms of beaks, size and food habits. All of them were related to finches of mainland. Darwin was fascinated by the creation of large number of breeds of animals by human efforts. He, therefore concluded that variations occur in organisms. Out of these variations, the ones suited to organisms were selected by nature and are inherited. On accumulation, they produce new species. These conclusions were publised in 1859 in his book "Origin of species by Means of Natural Selection. The Preservation of Races in the struggle of Life". These books popularly called 'Origin of Species'.
Theory of Natural Selection (Darwin, 1859): This theory states that organic evolution occurs through natural selection and accumulation of inheritable variations which provide structural and functional superiority to some individuals over others in their survival and reproduction.
The important features of this theory are as follows :
(i) Reproduction. Organisms have a very high reproductive potential, producing a very large number of offsprings.
(ii) Limited food and space. There is a limitation of food and space an earth. Therefore, populations of different species cannot increase beyond a certain limit.
(iii) Struggle for existence. There is a competition amongst organisms to obtain optimum resources. The competition is maximum amongst members of the same species, i.e., intraspecific competition.
(iv) Variations. A large number of variations occur in size, structure, physiology and behaviour of individuals of a species. The variations are of three types-useful, neutral and harmful.
(v) Natural Selection (Survival of the Fittest). In the struggle for existence, only those individuals survive and reproduce which have the most useful variations.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-156.jpg?height=92&width=102&top_left_y=2549&top_left_x=1822)
(vi) Inheritance of useful Variations. Individuals with useful variations reproduce and transfer the variations to the next generation. Next generation repeats the process of formation of new variations and natural selection, a process called a continuous selection.
(vii) Formation of new species. Accumulation of variations produces a completely new species.

|  | Acquired Traits | Inherited Traits |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. | Development: The traits develop during lifetime of an individual. | The traits are obtained from the parents. |
| 2. | Nature: They are somatic variations. | They are genetic variations. |
| 3. | Cause: Acquired traits develop due to direct effect of an environment. | The traits develop due to mutations and reshuffling of genetic material. |
| 4. | They die with the death of the individual Ex. Muscular body of a wrestler. | They are passed on to the next generation. Ex. Fused and free earlobes. |

Micro-evolution : Refers to small but significant changes that alter the common characteristics of an organism in the same species.

## How does Evolution Occur ?

Darwin's Theory of Natural Selection has been divided into five parts to explain clearly how evolution happens in nature.

1. Organisms produce a large number of offsprings.
2. Every individual must struggle to survive as there are not enough resources.
3. There are variations within a species.
4. Some variations allow members of a species to survive and reproduce better than others.
5. Organisms that survive and reproduce pass their traits to their offsprings and therefore the helpful traits gradually appear in more and more of the population.
This example of how brown hares in polar region became white hares will demonstrate each of the above points.
(a) There were brown hares living in cold, snowy polar region. Many of them were killed and eaten by other animals very few survived.
(b) Hares were competing for life, trying to avoid predators to survive.
(c) As a result, a few albino (white) hares were born due to mutation in the genes.
(d) This variation helped albino hares to hide. Predators had a hard time finding the albino hares in the snow and hence they were able to survive and reproduce.
(e) In the following generations, more white hares survived and reproduced resulting in an entire population of white hare. The albino hares have adapted to fit better in their environment than the brown hares. This is called Natural Selection. Natural selection is the phenomenon where nature selects traits favourable to the species in its environment.
Now, if we examine the fifth point in the example, survival of more albino hare in the population changed the colour characteristic from normal brown to white, over a period of time. This change took place only in a small population of hare living in cold and snowy regions i.e., there is an accidental change or mutation in the genes in a small population, even if this change gives no survival advantage to the possessor. This phenomenon is called Genetic Drift and it is a random genetic change in a population. The elimination of the genes of certain traits from the small population, when a section of the species population dies of natural reasons or imigrates to other region, is called Genetic drift. It alters the gene frequency of the remaining population.

## Speciation

The origin of new species from the existing one due to reproductive isolation of a part of its population is called Speciation. The geographical isolation make sub-populations become more and more different from each other due to geographical barriers. Ultimately, reproductive isolation occurs between individuals of these two groups i.e., they will no longer be able to exchange genetic material between them. At this point, natural selection works on the genetic variation within that population and results in formation of few species.

## CONNECTING TOPIC

## Four types of speciation occur in nature. They are:

1. Allopatric speciation: Population splits into two geographically isolated regions and each population undergoes genotypic and/or phenotypic divergence. e.g., Darwin's finches
2. Peripatric speciation: New species are formed in isolated small peripheral populations that are prevented from exchanging genes with the main population. Genetic drift plays an important role here e.g., underground mosquito is a variant of Culex pipiens.
3. Parapatric speciation: Two populations are partially seperated geographically, so individuals of each species may come in contact with the other from time to time, but reduced fitness of the heterozygote prevents breeding between the two species. e.g., Ensoltina salamanders.
4. Sympatric speciation refers to the formation of two or more descendant species from a single ancestral species all occupying the same geographical location. e.g., cichlids in East Africa.

|  | Allopatric | Peripatric | Parapatric | Sympatric |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Original Population | ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-158.jpg?height=100&width=127&top_left_y=996&top_left_x=788) | ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-158.jpg?height=100&width=122&top_left_y=996&top_left_x=1005) | ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-158.jpg?height=98&width=122&top_left_y=996&top_left_x=1215) | ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-158.jpg?height=98&width=118&top_left_y=996&top_left_x=1431) |
| Initial step of speciation | ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-158.jpg?height=109&width=151&top_left_y=1138&top_left_x=786) | ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-158.jpg?height=122&width=149&top_left_y=1122&top_left_x=1000) | ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-158.jpg?height=117&width=169&top_left_y=1127&top_left_x=1203) | ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-158.jpg?height=100&width=120&top_left_y=1140&top_left_x=1435) |

Fig. : Types of speciation

## Artificial Selection

Artificial selection is the process by which man selects traits which are useful to him for improving the qualities of domesticated plants and animals. New species have been created by domesticated Animal Husbandry e.g., reproductive isolation leads to differences in the species of fruit flies after eight generations using different food types.
Hybridisation between two different species sometimes leads to a distinct phenotype. For e.g. Mariana mallard.
The individual having the desired traits are selected and allowed to interbreed among themselves. This process of artificial selection when repeated for many generations produces a new breed. For example many useful plants like wheat, rice, pulses and vegetables are produced through this method. The plants like cabbage, cauliflower, broccoli kholrabi and kale are all formed from a wild cabbage species.

| S. No. | Natural Selection | Artificial Selection |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. | It is a natural phenomenon | It is an artificial process. |
| 2. | It occurs in nature on a vast scale. | It is controlled by man on a limited scale in a specific environment. |
| 3. | Traits selected for evolution are beneficial to the species. | Traits selected for improvement are beneficial to man. |
| 4. | Results are achieved over a long period of time. | Results are achieved in a short period of time. |

Table: Comparison between Natural and Artificial selection.

CASE - I : Some plants of the grass species (Anthoxanthum odoratum) which live in contaminated soil are tolerant of heavy metals, while others that inhabit the non-polluted soil are not. Which type of speciation has occurred in this species of grass? This is a case of parapatric speciation as two different populations have formed due to an extreme change in their habitat.

CASE-II : Two separate species of squirrel inhabit north and south rims of Arizona's Grand Canyon. This is an example of which type of speciation?

It is an example of Allopatric speciation as in this case, geographical barrier is the driving force for speciation.

CASE - III : Polar bear has evolved from the brown bear. Explain how?

Based on the DNA evidences, it has been observed that polar bears had undergone peripatric speciation. They had evolved from their extinct ancestors i.e. brown bears, during ice age.

## CASE - IV : Why is sympatric speciation common in bacteria?

Sympatric speciation can result in two different groups that eventually become so genetically different from one another that they can no longer breed with each other. Since, bacteria has the ability to exchange genetic material with organisms other than parents or offspring, they can undergo sympatric speciation.

## Evidences for Evolution

Different organisms show common features which provides evidence in favour of evolution i.e., they can be considered to have been evolved from common ancestor. The more the number of features the organisms/species have in common, the more closely they are related. The more closely they are related, the more recently they would have had a common ancestor. The following sources provide evidence for evolution :
(i) Homologous Organs : These are organs having same structure, but may be similar or different in functions. Example, forelimbs of man (arm or hand), whale (flipper or paddle), bird (wings), lizard and frog. All have the same selection but modified for various functions. This indicates that they all have inherited a common plan from a common ancestor.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-160.jpg?height=613&width=190&top_left_y=701&top_left_x=571)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-160.jpg?height=476&width=266&top_left_y=758&top_left_x=760)

Wing of Bird

Fore Limb of House Lizard
(ii) Analogous organs : These are organs of different origin and basic plan, but similar in functions. Example, wings bat and wings of bird. The former are basically skin folds stretched between elongated fingers and the latter are a feathery covering all along the arm. So the designs of two wings are very different and hence analogous.

Fig. : Homologous Organs
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-160.jpg?height=282&width=357&top_left_y=1507&top_left_x=829)

Fig. Analogous Organs
|  | Homologous Organs | Analogous Organs |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Same structure but different functions. | Same function but different underlying structure. |
| 2 | Examples: Wing of a bat, lateral fin of a whale, and the human arm; they all have similar structures, but they carry out different functions. | Examples: Bat's wing and fly's wing; they both are used to help the creature fly, but they have different patterns in bone structure. |
| 3 | Homologous structures mean they had come from a common ancestor, supports divergent evolution. | The similarity of the function also reflects that they are adapted to a similar environment, supports convergent evolution. |


![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-160.jpg?height=182&width=113&top_left_y=2206&top_left_x=406)

In man, there are about 180 vestigial organs, out of which more than 90 are visible.
RKnow?

## (iii) Vestigial Organs

These organs occurr as reduced and functionless structures in body. It is considered that vestigial organs were functional in remote ancestors, but became vestigial and functionless due to their disuse in course of evolution.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-161.jpg?height=415&width=997&top_left_y=360&top_left_x=564)
Fig. Some vestigial structures in Human beings

(iv) Connecting Links : These are animals possessing characters of two different groups. Echidna and Platypus are connecting links between reptiles and mammals. They indicate that mammals have evolved from reptiles. Likewise, Archeopteryx the connecting link between reptile and birds.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-161.jpg?height=314&width=339&top_left_y=922&top_left_x=902)
Fig. Archaeopteryx

(v) Embryology : The embryology of different vertebrates provides very strong evidences favouring organic evolution. The early embryos of different vertebrates show striking similarities. This indicates common origin and ancestory of the different vertebrates. On this basis, Ernest Haeckel proposed the law named "Biogenetic Law". According to this law the embryonic development of any organism repeats complete evolutionary history

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-161.jpg?height=1057&width=703&top_left_y=1443&top_left_x=712)
Fig. Comparative Embryology (Early development stages of fish, bird and human)

Molecular phylogeny: Molecular phylogeny states that distantly related organisms accumulate greater number of differences in their DNA. Studies on these differences help in tracing out the evolutionary relationships among the organisms. This relationship among different organisms shown by molecular phylogeny matches the recent classification systems.
Evolution through stages: Evolution is a complex process and hence cannot take place by a single DNA change. It occurs in many stages and over many years of time. During the course of evolution, even the intermediate stages can be useful to some extent. For example the evolution of complex organ 'eyes' took place in stages. One of the intermediate stages; the rudimentary eyes; was also useful for some organisms. The adaptation at different stages was very popular in this case. Eyes are found in insects, octopus as well as vertebrates but vary greatly in structure.

1. How does migration affect natural selection?
2. Why 'biogenetic law' is also referred as 'recapitulation theory'?
3. What are the main drawbacks of Lamarckism?
4. True and False-
(a) Long neck of giraffe is an example of acquired inheritance.
(b) All organs which are not in use either degenerate or become vestigial with time.
(c) Natural selection is the only factor responsible for evolution.
(d) Darwin's theory of evolution is based on genetic basis of inheritance.

## SOLUTION

1. Migration is the movement of individuals from one place to another. This results in addition and removal of alleles from a particular population of species which will affect the phenomenon of natural selection.
2. Biogenetic law is also referred as recapitulation theory as according to this law, ontogeny repeats phylogeny. It means that the embryonic development of an organism traces the evolutionary development of the species.
3. The main drawbacks of Lamarckism are -
(a) Lack of experimental proof
(b) Not all acquired characters are inherited. For example, ear piercing in women.
(c) Organs don't form or degenerate on the basis of their use and disuse.
4. (a) (True)
(b) (False)
(c) (False)
(d) (False)

## Fossils

Fossils are organic remains of plants and animals that existed long ago. An entire organism or a part of its body was buried in the sediments of earth resulting in the formation of fossils. The study of fossils indicates the structural features of organisms that are not alive and their comparison with the existing forms which gives an indication as to how evolution has taken place. Fossils are found deeply embedded in sedimentary rocks. Most recent rock layers are on top, the oldest fossils are found in the deepest rocks. In many places, old rocks have become exposed due to erosion and weathering.
Sedimentary rocks are not the only source of fossils. Insects have been found in amber, while the entire woolly mammoths have been found to be preserved in ice.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-163.jpg?height=659&width=942&top_left_y=360&top_left_x=589)
Fig. Fossil formation

By arranging fossils in the order in which they have appeared on earth many fossil series have been established. Such series demonstrate how various forms of life have changed gradually over a period of millions of years. As a result, the evolution of a number of animals such as horse, camel and elephant, has been clearly understood. Plenty of fossil evidence is now available so as to establish human evolution too.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-163.jpg?height=76&width=111&top_left_y=1233&top_left_x=1025)
Learn More

Dating of fossils is the process of finding out the age of fossil. The most effective way of dating is to measure the amount of radioactive material in the rock or soil. Half-life periods are calculated on the basis of the ratio of the radio active material and its products of decay. The most commonly used radioactive materials are uranium and carbon -14 .

## Human Evolution

The evolutionary sequence from earliest known human ancestors is not a straight line but is a 'tree' with many dead ends. As the fossil evidences are limited, the scientists are unable to tell about number of human species that have existed and which was ancestor of the others. The 'tree' of human evolution provides a simple guide to relationship but does not clearly indicate ancestry.

- Paranthropus
- Australopithecus
- Homo habilus
- Homoerectus
- Homo neanderthals
- Homo sapiens

Paranthropus was strongly built "man-apes" that lived in southern and eastern Africa between three and one million years ago. Australopithecus are thought to be the earliest hominids i.e., human-like people. They had a small brain and projecting jaw. They stood upright and walked on two feet. This is known from their leg bones and backbone, and also from the footprints found in Tanzania ( 3.7 million years old).
Homo habilus or the "handyman" is the earliest known species of Homo (genes to which the modern day human belongs). It lived in the woodlands and Savannahs of Africa. It had a brain size of 650-800 ml. It made and used simple stone tools, and was a successful forager and scavenger.
Homo erectus was the first human to leave Africa and move to Europe and Asia. It had a sloping forehead, flattish face and brain size between $850-1100 \mathrm{ml}$. These humans exploited more habitats than their ancestors. They were the first ones to use fire. Homo neanderthals were the first humans to have adopted to life in cold climates of Europe and Asia. They had strong physique and large brain. They wore clothes, made a range of tools and used fire to keep warm. They were the first humans to bury their dead. Homo sapiens or the "modern human" first evolved in Africa. They had a large brain, were considerably intelligent and had developed the ability to use language. Modern man took control of their surroundings as they developed agriculture, societies and technology.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-164.jpg?height=599&width=1326&top_left_y=386&top_left_x=342)
Fig. Human Evolutionary Tree

## Origin of life on Earth

Darwin's theory of evolution tells us how life evolved from simple to more complex forms and Mendel's experiments give us the mechanism for the inheritance of traits from one generation to the next. But neither tells us anything about how life began on earth in the first place.
Haldane's Theory - J.B.S. Haldane, a British scientist (who became a citizen of India later), suggested in 1929 that life must have developed from the simple inorganic molecules which were present on earth soon after it was formed. He speculated that the conditions on earth at that time, which were far from the conditions we see today, could have given rise to more complex organic molecules that were necessary for life. The first primitive organisms would arise from further chemical synthesis.
How did these organic molecules arise?
Miller and Urey's Experiment - An answer was suggested by the experiment conducted by Stanley L. Miller and Harold C. Urey in 1953. They assembled an atmosphere similar to that thought to exist on early earth (this had molecules like ammonia, methane and hydrogen sulphide, but no oxygen) over water. This was maintained at a temperature just below $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and sparks were passed through the mixture of gases to simulate lightning. At the end of a week, $15 \%$ of the carbon (from methane) had been converted to simple compounds of carbon including amino acids which make up protein molecules.

## Evolution Vs Progress

Is evolution of species a progress to higher, more complex and intelligent forms, as often thought?
If one sees the tree of human evolution, one can see that there are multiple branches at each and every stage of evolution. Speciation does not intend to convey the dying out of an inferior species giving way to superior ones. Species adapt to the conditions available in their environment. Some older and simpler species live on quite effectively. Scorpion is one of the most primitive invertebrate which has lived successfully for millions of years; it can withstand heat and nuclear radiation levels that would easily kill man.
The process of evolution in nature has resulted in every organism reaching a certain stage by various processes. The same process has brought man to the stage of a human being and considers him as a specific kind of species distinct from other ones. In fact, there is no real progress in the idea of evolution. It simply implies the generation and shaping of diverse life forms by environmental selection. The only progressive trend in evolution seems to be that more and more complex body designs have emerged over time.

## Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) :

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has 4 choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONLY ONE is correct.

1. A pair of contrasting characters is called
(a) phenotype
(b) genotype
(c) allele
(d) gene
2. An allele is said to be dominant if
(a) it is expressed only in heterozygous combination.
(b) it is expressed only in homozygous combination.
(c) it is expressed in both homozygous and heterozygous condition.
(d) it is expressed only in second generation.
3. Which of the following is a test cross?
(a) $\mathrm{TT} \times \mathrm{tt}$
(b) $\mathrm{Tt} \times \mathrm{tt}^{3}$
(c) $\mathrm{Tt} \times \mathrm{TT}$
(d) $\mathrm{tt} \times \mathrm{tt}$
4. Which is the example of homologous organs?
(a) Forelimbs of man and wings of bird
(b) Wings of birds and wings of insects
(c) Vermiform appendix and nictitating membrane
(d) Archaeopteryx and Balanoglossus
5. Which of the following features do humans lack that other primates have ?
(a) Forward-facing eyes
(b) Short snouts
(c) Flexible shoulder and elbow joints
(d) Opposable big toes
6. A pure tall plant can be differentiated from a hybrid tall plant:
(a) By measuring length of plant
(b) By spraying gibberellins
(c) If all plants are tall after self-pollination
(d) If all plants are dwarf after self-pollination
7. In fruit flies wild-type colour is red and is dominant to white eye colour. Because this eye colour is located on the:
(a) Y chromosome only.
(b) autosome only.
(c) X and Y chromosomes
(d) X chromosome only.
8. The diagram below represents a portion of a nucleic acid molecule.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-165.jpg?height=324&width=179&top_left_y=2224&top_left_x=539)

The part indicated by arrow $X$ could be
(a) adenine
(b) ribose
(c) deoxyribose
(d) phosphate
9. Mendel's concept of segregation implies that the two members of an allelic pair of genes -
(a) are distributed to separate gametes
(b) may contaminate one another
(c) are segregated in pairs
(d) are linked
10.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-165.jpg?height=282&width=309&top_left_y=890&top_left_x=1231)

The figure above shows :
(a) A bird sitting on a tree
(b) Connection between Birds and Reptiles
(c) Connection between Pisces and Aves
(d) All of the above
11. If a homozygous red-flowered plant is crossed with a homozygous white-flowered plant, the offspring would be:
(a) Half red-flowered
(b) Half white-flowered
(c) All red-flowered
(d) Half pink-flowered
12. Which of the following would stop evolution by natural selection from occurring?
(a) If humans became extinct because of a disease epidemic.
(b) If a thermonuclear war killed most living organisms and changed the environment drastically.
(c) If ozone depletion led to increased ultraviolet radiation, which caused many new mutations.
(d) If all individuals in a population were genetically identical, and there was no genetic recombination, sexual reproduction, or mutation.
13. If the fossil of an organism is found in the deeper layers of earth, then we can predict that:
(a) The extinction of organism has occurred recently.
(b) The extinction of organism has occurred thousands of year ago.
(c) The fossil position in the layers of earth is not related to its time of extinction.
(d) Time of extinction cannot be determined.
14. A Mendelian experiment consisted of breeding tall pea plants bearing violet flowers with short pea plants bearing white flowers. The progeny all bore violet flowers, but almost half of them were short. This suggests that the genetic make-up of the tall parent can be depicted as:
(a) TTWW
(b) TTww
(c) TtWW
(d) TtWw
15. Normal maize has starchy seed which remain smooth when dry. A mutant form has sugary seed which go crinkled when dry. When a mutant was crossed with a normal plant, an $F_{1}$ was produced which had smooth seeds. What would be the relative ratios of the different seed types, if the $F_{1}$ was allowed to self
(a) 1 smooth : 3 sugary
(b) 3 smooth : 1 sugary
(c) 1 smooth : 1 sugary
(d) All sugary

## Assertion \& Reason:

DIRECTIONS : Each of these questions contains an Assertion followed by reason. Read them carefully and answer the question on the basis of following options. You have to select the one that best describes the two statements.
(a) If both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
(b) If both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
(c) If Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
(d) If Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

1. Assertion: Chromosomes are known as hereditary vehicles.
Reason: The chromosomes are capable of selfreproduction and maintaining morphological and physiological properties through successive generations.
2. Assertion: Ear muscles of external ear in man are poorly developed.
Reason: These muscles are useful which move external ear freely to detect sound efficiently.
3. Assertion: Although living organism always arise from other living organism, life should certainly have had a beginning.
Reason: The study of the conditions and the mechanisms involved in the creation of most primitive living structures on earth is actually the problem of origin of life.
4. Assertion: The establishment of reproductive isolations in an event of biological significance.
Reason: In the absence of reproductive isolation species can merge back into single population.
5. Assertion: DNA fingerprinting is a method in which polymerase chain reaction followed by DNA probe is used.
Reason: A DNA fingerprint is inherited and therefore, resembles that of parents.
6. Assertion: The birds have large, light spongy bones with air sacs.
Reason : These adaptations help them during flight.
7. Assertion: We have lost all the direct evidence of origin of life.
Reason: The persons responsible for protecting evidences were not skilled.
8. Assertion: Among the primates, chimpanzee is the closest relative of the present day humans.
Reason: The banding pattern in the autosome numbers 3 and 6 of man and chimpanzee is remarkably similar.
9. Assertion: Human ancestors never used their tails and so the tail expressing gene has disappeared in them.
Reason: Lamarck's theory of evolution is popularly called theory of continuity of germplasm.
10. Assertion: The genetic complement of an organism is called genotype.
Reason: Genotype is the type of hereditary properties of an organism.

14. Attached ear lobe is recessive trait.
15. Charles Darwin discovered the law of independent assortment.

## Reasoning Based Questions:

1. Why are the traits acquired during the lifetime of an individual not inherited?
2. Why are human beings who look so different from each other in terms of size, colour and looks said to belong to the same species?
3. Name one organ analogous to the wing of bird. Why are they both analogous ? Can you include the wing of bat also with them under the same category ? Give reason.
4. Give reasons for the appearance of new combination of characters in the $\mathrm{F}_{2}$ progeny.
5. A black mouse mates with a brown mouse, and all the offsprings are black.
(a) Why are no brown offsprings produced?
(b) If two of the black offsprings mate with each other, what kind of offspring would you expect and in what proportions? Give reason for your answer.
6. Pure-breed pea plants A are crossed with pure-breed pea plants B . It is found that the plants which look like A do not appear in $F_{1}$ generation but re-emerge in $F_{2}$ generation. Which of the plants A and B ar: (i) tall and (ii) dwarf? Give reason for your answer.
7. "Only variations that confer an advantage to an individual organism will survive in a population". Do you agree with this statement? Give reason for your answer.

## HOTS Questions:

1. Why is recessive trait found in fewer individuals?
2. How does creation of variations in a species promote survival?
3. A plant has two varieties, one with red petals and the other with white petals. When these two varieties are cross-pollinated, all the offsprings have red petals?
(a) Which gene is dominant?
(b) Choose suitable letters to represent the two genes.
4. (a) E is the gene for brown eye colour and e is the gene for blue eye colour. Which gene is (i) recessive, and (ii) dominant?
(b) Both father and mother have the genes Ee in their cells. What colour are their eyes?
(c) Which combination of genes in the zygote will produce children with blue eyes?
(d) Which combinations of genes in the zygote will produce children with brown eyes?

## Case Study Based MCQs

(CBSE Sample Issued 2021)

## Sex Determination

1. Read the following and attempt any four questions from 1(i) to $1(\mathrm{v})$.
Most human chromosomes have a maternal and a paternal copy, and we have 22 such pairs. But one pair, called the sex chromosomes, is odd in not always being a perfect pair. Women have a perfect pair of sex chromosomes, both called X . But men have a mismatched pair in which one is a normal-sized X while the other is a short one called Y . So women are XX , while men are XY .
(i) If a normal cell of human body contains 46 pairs of chromosomes then the numbers of chromosomes in a sex cell of a human being is most likely to be:
(a) 60
(b) 23
(c) 22
(d) 40
(ii) Which of the following determines the sex of a child?
(a) The length of the mother's pregnancy
(b) The length of time between ovulation and copulation
(c) The presence of an X chromosome in an ovum
(d) The presence of a Y chromosome in a sperm
(iii) In human males, all the chromosomes are paired perfectly except one. These unpaired chromosomes are:
(a) Large chromosome
(b) Small chromosome
(c) Y chromosome
(d) X chromosome
(iv) The process where characteristics are transmitted from parent to offsprings is called:
(a) Variation
(b) Heredity
(c) Gene
(d) Allele
(e) None of the above
(v) Who have a perfect pair of sex chromosomes?
(a) Girls only
(b) Boys only
(c) Both girls and boys
(d) It depends on many other factors

## Exercise 2

Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar)

## Text - Book Questions:

1. If a trait A exists in $10 \%$ of a population of an asexually reproducting species and a trait B exists in $60 \%$ of the same population, which trait is likely to have arisen earlier?
2. How does the creation of variations in a species promote survival?
3. How do Mendel's experiments show that traits may be dominant or recessive?
4. A man with blood group A marries a woman with blood group O and their daughter has blood group O . Is this information enough to tell you which of the traits - blood group A or O - is dominant? Why or why not?
5. How is the sex of the child determined in human beings ?
6. What are the different ways in which individuals with a particular trait may increase in a population?
7. Why are the small numbers of surviving tigers a cause of worry is from the point of view of genetics?
8. Will geographical isolation be a major factor in the speciation of an organism that reproduces asexually? Why or why not?
9. Give an example of characteristics being used to determine how close two species are in evolutionary terms.
10. Can the wing of a butterfly and the wing of a bat be considered homologous organs? Why of why not?
11. Why are human beings who look so different from each other in terms of size, colour and looks said to belong to the same species?

## Text - Book Exercise:

1. A Mendelian experiment consisted of breeding tall pea plants bearing violet flowers with short pea plants bearing white flowers. The progeny all bore violet flowers, but almost half of them were short. This suggests that the genetic make-up of the tall parent can be depicted as
(a) TTWW
(b) TTww
(c) TtWW
(d) TtWw
2. An example of homologous organs is
(a) our teeth and elepahant's tusks
(b) our arm and a dog's fore-leg
(c) potato and runners of grass.
(d) all of the above.
3. A study found that children with light-coloured eyes are likely to have parents with light-coloured eyes. On this basis, can we say anything about whether the light eye colour trait is dominant or recessive? Why or Why not?
4. How are the areas of study - evolution and classification - interlinked?
5. Explain the terms analogous and homologous organs with examples.
6. Outline a project which aims to find the dominant coat colour in dogs.
7. What evidence do we have for the origin of life from inanimate matter?
8. Explain how sexual reproduction gives rise to more viable variations than asexual reproduction. How does this affect the evolution of those organisms that reproduce sexually?
9. Only variations that confer an advantage to an individual organism will survive in a population. Do you agree with this statement? Why or Why not?

## Exemplar Questions:

1. A basket of vegetables contains carrot, potato, radish and tomato. Which of them represent the correct homologous structures?
(a) Carrot and potato
(b) Carrot and tomato
(c) Radish and carrot
(d) Radish and potato
2. According to the evolutionary theory, formation of a new species is generally due to:
(a) Sudden creation by nature
(b) Accumulation of variations over several generations
(c) Clones formed during asexual reproduction
(d) Movement of individuals from one habitat to another
3. From the list given below, select the character which can be acquired but not inherited:
(a) Colour of eye
(b) Colour of skin
(c) Size of body
(d) Nature of hair
4. Select the group which shares maximum number of common characters.
(a) Two individuals of a species
(b) Two species of a genus
(c) Two genera of a family
(d) Two genera of two families
5. Why do all the gametes formed in human females have an X chromosome?
6. Do genetic combination of mothers play a significant role in determining the sex of a new born?
7. In human beings, the statistical probability of getting either a male or female child is $50: 50$. Give a suitable explanation.
8. A very small population of a species faces a greater threat of extinction than a larger population. Provide a suitable genetic explanation.
9. A woman has only daughters. Analyse the situation genetically and provide a suitable explanation.
10. All the human races like Africans, Asians, Europeans, Americans and others might have evolved from a common ancestor. Provide a few evidences in support of this view.
