## Learn More

Biome - It is defined as the total assemblage of plant and animal species interacting within specific conditions. In simpler terms, it is basically a set of ecosystems sharing similar characteristics and occupying a major habitat. For example, forest, grassland, etc.
Biosphere - It includes all the living components of the earth. It consists of all plants, animals and micro organisms inhabiting the earth and their interactions with the environment.
Biogeochemical cycles - The cyclic movements of chemical elements of the biosphere between the organism and the environment are referred as biogeochemical cycles. The cycle starts by absorbing the chemical elements by the organism and is returned to the air, water and soil through decomposition. For example, gaseous (the main reservoirs of nutrients are atmosphere and ocean) and sedimentary cycles (reservoirs of nutrients are soil and rocks).

## IMPACT OF HUMAN ACTIVITIES

Human beings are an important part of environment. Our activities change the environment around us. And the change in the environment affects us. Let us see how?

## Pollution

Pollution is derived from a Latin word 'polluere' which means contamination. Pollution is defined as the introduction of substances (contaminants) into the natural environment that causes adverse changes. These substances that cause pollution are called as pollutants. Pollution can be broadly classified into following categories -
Air pollution - The contamination of the earth's atmosphere by the release of various harmful substances like chemicals, harmful gases or fumes etc. is called air pollution. The various sources of air pollution are burning of fossils fuels, release of harmful fumes/chemicals from the industries/factories and mining operations. It can cause various respiratory, cardiovascular and skin diseases in humans.
Water pollution - The contamination of water bodies by water pollutants (contaminants) is termed as water pollution. The various sources of water pollution are human and animal wastes, untreated industrial sewage, agricultural runoff containing pesticides and fertilizers etc. Sometimes it can be natural as in case of eutrophication (nutrient enrichment of water body resulting in oxygen depletion).
Soil Pollution - The contamination or degradation of soil by the presence of chemicals or manmade substances is termed as soil pollution. The various sources include acid rain, industrial waste disposal, agrochemicals (pesticides and fertilizers), oil spills etc. Noise Pollution - It is usually man made and is caused by the excessive amount of noise thereby disrupting natural balance. The various sources are social events, household noises, heavy machines in industries, noises from construction sites etc.

## CONNECTING TOPIC

## Global Warming

Scientist have determined that a number of human activities are contributing to global warming by adding excessive amounts of greenhouse gases to the atmosphere $\left(\mathrm{CO}_{2}, \mathrm{CH}_{4}, \mathrm{CFCs}, \mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)$. Carbon dioxide accumulate in the atmosphere and trap heat that normally would exit into outer space.
While many greenhouse gases occur naturally and are needed to create the greenhouse effect. They keep the earth warm enough to support life. The human use of fossil fuels is the main source of greenhouse gas emission. By driving cars, using electricity from coal-fired power plants, or heating our homes with oil or natural gas, we release carbon dioxide and other heat trapping gases into the atmosphere. Deforestation is another significant source of greenhouse gases as fewer trees convert less carbon
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-195.jpg?height=355&width=524&top_left_y=1740&top_left_x=1347)
dioxide to oxygen.
During the 150 years of the industrial age, the atmospheric concentration of carbon dioxide has increased by 31 percent. Over the same period, the level of atmospheric methane has risen by 151 percent, mostly from agricultural activities such as raising cattle and growing rice.
As the concentration of greenhouse gases increases, more heat is trapped in the atmosphere and less escapes back into space. This increase in trapped heat changes the climate and alters weather patterns. This may affect species extinction, the length of seasons, and can cause natural disasters (floods, storms etc.).

## Few actions one can take to Reduce Global Warming

1. Reduce, Reuse, Recycle - Reduce waste by choosing reusable products instead of disposables. Recycle paper, plastic, newspaper, glass and aluminium cans. By recycling half of your household waste, you can save 2,400 pounds of carbon dioxide.
2. Use less heat and air conditioning - Proper insulation of the room can lower the heating or airconditioning cost by more than 25 percent. Setting the thermostat just 2 degrees lower in winter and higher in summer could save about 2,000 pounds of carbon dioxide each year.
3. Change a light bulb - Replace regular light bulbs with Compact Fluorescent Light (CFL) bulbs. CFLs last 10 times longer than incandescent bulbs, use two-third less energy, and give off 70 percent less heat.
4. Drive less and Drive smart - Lesser driving means fewer emissions. Explore the community mass transit system and check out options for car-pooling to work or school.
When you do drive, make sure your car is running efficiently. Every gallon of gas you save not only helps your budget but also keeps 20 pounds of carbon dioxide out of the atmosphere.
5. Buy energy-efficient products - Buy appliances which are energy efficient. Avoid products that come with excess packaging, especially plastic molded and other packaging that can't be recycled.
6. Use less hot water - Set the water heater at 120 degrees to save energy and make sure that it is well insulated. Wash clothes in warm or cold water to reduce the use of hot water and the energy required to produce it. This change alone can save atleast 500 pounds of carbon dioxide annually in most households. Use energy saving settings in your dishwasher and let the dishes air-dry.
7. Use the 'Off' switch - Save electricity and reduce global warming by turning off lights when you leave a room, and using only as much light as you need. Remember to turn off the television, music system and computer when you are not using them.
It is good idea to turn off water when you are not using it especially, while brushing your teeth, washing your vehicle, etc. In this way you'll reduce the water bill and also save energy needed to pump water to your house.
8. Plant a tree - Trees absorb carbon dioxide and give off oxygen during photosynthesis. Trees are an integral part of the natural atmospheric cycle, but very few are left to counter the increase in carbon dioxide caused by human activities. A single tree can absorb about one ton of carbon dioxide during its lifetime.
These steps will take you a long way towards reducing energy use and also your monthly budget. And less energy use means less dependence on the fossil fuels that create greenhouse gases and contribute to global warming.

## OZONE DEPLETION

About $90 \%$ of earth's ozone is concentrated in the stratosphere. Stratospheric ozone protects the earth from potentially damaging doses of ultraviolet radiations. Its concentration is maximum at $23-25 \mathrm{~km}$ above equator and $11-18 \mathrm{~km}$ above poles. This rich zone of ozone in the stratosphere is called ozone layer or ozonosphere. It is also called ozone shield because this region intercepts the ultraviolet radiations.

## Ozone layer

In the stratosphere, ozone is being photodissociated and generated simultaneously by the absorption of harmful ultraviolet radiations coming from the sun.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-196.jpg?height=145&width=560&top_left_y=1672&top_left_x=724)

These two reactions are in equilibrium, hence maintaining a steady concentration of ozone in the stratosphere.
Thinning of ozone layer is called ozone depletion. With the technological advancement in the past century various man made substances have caused depletion of ozone from the stratosphere. Thinning of ozone was noticed in 1985 above the Antartica, by Farman and coworkers. In 1987, the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) succeeded in reaching an agreement control CFC production. The widely known substances which cause ozone depletion are :

- Refrigerants (CFCs-chloroflurocarbons and HCFs- hydrofluorocarbons)
- Insulating foams
- Solvents
- Aerosols

Primary health issues caused by ozone depletion are because of the easy penetration of ultraviolet rays from the sun. It has harmful effects on man, animals and plants:

- Sunburn
- Skin Aging
- Various forms of skin cancer.
- Eye problems like cataract, cornea damages, etc.
- Attacks immune system and makes humans prone to infections/diseases.

This is a global issue and environmentalists are working towards developing an effective strategy to check the thinning of ozone layer. The Helsinki Declaration in 1989 had majority of the nations pledge to phase out CFC by the year 2000. Efforts are still on and each one of us has to contribute to this.

1. Do we expect an increase in ground-level ultraviolet radiation with depletion of ozone layer?
2. Which of the following statements are correct-?
(a) The ecological pyramid of numbers is always upright.
(b) The food chain includes saprophytes.
(c) Ozone layer is present roughly 7-25 miles above the earth's surface, a layer known as stratosphere.
(d) Recently, the change in weather patterns has limited the depletion of ozone layer.
3. How are CFC's harmful for the environment and living beings?

## SOLUTION

1. Yes, we do expect an increase in ground level ultraviolet radiation as the ozone depletion prevents the absorbance of ultraviolet radiation from the sun.
2. (a) False, (b) False, (c) True and (d) True.
3. CFC's contain both chlorine and fluorine. They are not degraded by any natural processes near the earth. In stratosphere, they are dissociated by UV light to release free chlorine atoms which catalyse the breakdown of ozone molecules ( $\mathrm{O}_{3}$ ) into oxygen ( $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ ). This thinning of ozone layer leads to penetration of UV rays into the Earth's atmosphere causing blindness, skin cancers and mutations.

## WASTE

Accumulation of wastes due to its improper disposal is a major problem in a country. Population in our country has been growing at a rapid rate. With this increase, there has been an increase in the amount of wastes being produced, especially in the cities. Every person on an average generates about 400 to 500 grams of wastes per day. At this rate, in a city of about 10 lakh people, around 500 tones of wastes is being produced per day.
In order to understand the problem and work towards a solution, one must know the types of wastes being generated. Waste can be classified into two categories :
(1) Biodegradable Wastes : These are the wastes which are broken down and disposed off naturally by saprophytes or decomposers. Common saprophytes are microscopic organisms or microbes like bacteria and fungi. They secrete enzymes which bring about the solubilisation of wastes. Biodegradable wastes are generally organic wastes, e.g., garbage, sewage, livestock waste, used tea leaves, waste paper, waste cotton, wool, leather, wood and jute articles, food leftovers, etc. Besides natural degradation by microbes, they can also be disposed off through waste treatment plants or converted into an agricultural resource of manure and compost. Biodegradable wastes should not be allowed to pile up anywhere as they attract wildlife.
(2) Non-biodegradable Wastes : These are the wastes which cannot be broken down by decomposers because they do not have enzymes for the same. We cannot eat coal to obtain energy because we do not possess enzymes for the same. Most of the non-biodegradable wastes are human made, e.g., plastic, crockery, metallic cans, glass articles, polythene bags, synthetic fibres, silver foil, many pesticides etc. Industries also produce a number of non-biodegradable wastes consisting of heavy metals, synthetic chemicals, toxins, etc., Some of these wastes can be broken down by heat, pressure and oxidation under specific conditions.

Differences between Biodegradable and Non-biodegradable Waste
| Biodegradable Waste | Non-biodegradable Waste |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1. They decompose into the soil. <br> 2. They are degraded by microorganisms such as bacteria and fungi. <br> 3. They don't undergo biomagnification. <br> 4. They are converted into soil or some useful end product. <br> Examples : Sewage, household garbage, etc. | 1. Take long time or may never decompose. <br> 2. They are not degraded by microorganisms. They can be broken down into smaller elements by few natural processes. <br> 3. They enter the food chain and can undergo biomagnification. <br> 4. These require recycling depending on the material. <br> Examples : Plastic, glass, pesticides, heavy metals, radioactive elements, etc. |


（1）Take a piece of paper，a piece of old cotton cloth and a plastic bag．Dig these three items 15 cm deep in the ground， separately．Cover with soil and leave it for about a month．Then dig out the buried material and observe them．
We will find that the piece of paper and the piece of cloth have been partially decomposed but the plastic bag has remained unaffected．This means the paper and cloth have been decomposed by the micro－organisms present in the soil i．e．they are bio－degradable．The plastic bag has not been decomposed by micro－organisms present in the soil，therefore，plastic is non－biodegradable．
（2）Dig pit of 40－50 cm depth in one corner of your school garden or any other vacant area．Place some domestic waste articles in it．Moisten them and cover the articles with atleast 15 cm thick soil．Moisten the soil at intervals of 2－3 days．Observe at 15 days interval by removing the soil temporarily．
We will see that some articles have degenerated after 30－45 days into an amorphous mass．The latter disappears within 2－3 months．They are biodegradable articles．For e．g．，staple food，food left－overs，fruit and vegetable peels， used tea leaves，waste paper，cartons，etc．Others remain identifiable and intact even after six months．They are non－biodegradable wastes．For e．g．，polythene bags，milk pouches，bottles，strips，etc．

## Sources of Waste

Domestic waste includes wastes generated in houses e．g．paper，plastic，vegetable waste，glass，textile，etc：
Commercial waste includes waste generated in commercial establishments like shops，printers，offices，godowns，etc．It includes packing material，polythene，paper，spoiled goods，vegetable and meat remnants，etc．Ashes come from burning solid fossil fuels like coal，wood and coke．Many houses and road side eateries use these fuels．
Animal wastes include dung of stray．
Biomedical wastes are wastes generated from hospitals which include expired drugs，plastic syringes，surgical dressings，etc． They can be infectious．
Construction waste is contributed by the construction activity going on in the urban areas．This includes metal rods，cement， concrete，etc．This type of waste is also generated by departments like electricity，drainage，telephone，etc．
Sewer removed from the sewerage during cleaning is left on the roadside which poses several health hazards to the public．
Hazardous wastes are those which catch fire easily，react explosively with air or water，corrode other materials and are toxic or radioactive．
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-198.jpg?height=99&width=106&top_left_y=1594&top_left_x=968)
More

## E－waste

Discarded electronic devices like television，personal computers，floppies，audio－video CDs，batteries，electric switches， telephones，cellphones，refrigerators，washing machines，air conditioners，kitchen gadgets，aircrafts，etc．contribute to the e－waste．These products contain components that have toxic substances like lead，cadmium，mercury，barium and carcinogens like carbon black and heavy metals．This deadly mix can cause severe health problems for those handling the waste．It can even prove fatal．

## Harmful Effects of Wastes

In the absence of proper waste management，the waste lies on the streets，road corners and improperly disposed off on vacant land．It has some serious health hazards．They attract wildlife like flies，mosquitoes，etc．，scavangers such as stray dogs，pigs， rats which can spread diseases．It also generates bad odour and causes pollution．
Some non－biodegradable substances such as industrial chemicals，pesticides，radioactive substances，heavy metals are very harmful to the living beings．These substances enter the food chain at a lower trophic level and their concentration increases from one trophic level to the next．As human beings occupy the top most level in the food chain，maximum concentration of these elements get accumulated in our bodies and produce harmful effects．This increase in concentration of harmful chemicals from one tophic level to the next is termed as biomagnification．Excessive use of pesticides and fertilizers may reduce the soil fertility and crop yield．

## Bioplastics or Organic Plastics

It is a form of plastic derived from renewable biomass sources such as vegetable oil，corn starch，pea starch，or microbiota rather than fossil－fuel plastics which are derived from petroleum．Some，but not all，are designed to biodegrade．
These plastics are used for organic waste bags where they can be composted along with the food or green waste．Some trays for fruits，vegetables，eggs and meat，bottles for soft drink and daily products are manufactured from bioplastics．

## WASTE MANAGEMENT

Improvement in our life- style has resulted in greater amount of waste material generation. Waste management has become a serious issue in urban areas. Disposing of garbage at the outskirts of a city is a common sight. Empty cans, food wrappers, etc which are non- biodegradable are littered in crowded places and tourist centres.
The disposal of the waste we generate is causing serious environmental problems. There are a number of concepts about waste management, which vary in their usage between countries or regions. The waste hierarchy refers to the ' 3 Rs' reduce, reuse and recycle, which classify waste management strategies according to their desirability in terms of waste minimisation.
For leading a healthy life, society has to efficiently manage the waste produced by it. Waste management is concerned

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-199.jpg?height=527&width=878&top_left_y=664&top_left_x=993)
Least favoured option

Fig. Waste Hierarchy
with the activity of collecting, transporting, processing, recycling, disposing and monitoring the waste dumped by the people in a society and around the world as a whole.

## Methods of Waste Management

1. Preventing and reducing the amount of waste being disposed of : - People should be made aware of their requirements for waste management, and efforts should be made to reduce the amount of waste. They should be encouraged to use recycled products.
2. Recovering energy from waste material:- Various kinds of machines have been developed to transform waste materials into some form of energy which can be used for cooking, heating and for providing power to various other machines.
3. Biological reprocessing: - Biodegradable waste can be converted into campost by burying in a pit dug into ground and used as manures.
4. Sanitary Landfill : - The disposal of waste by dumping it in low- lying areas of ground and covering it with earth is called landfill. This requires proper planning, design and good amount of man power. Land once filled can be reused, although it asks for maintenance.
5. Incineration:- 'Incineration' means 'reducing to ashes' i.e. burning of substance at very high temperature (above $1000^{\circ}$ C) to form ash. This reduces the volume of the waste. Incineration is carried out in a furnace called incinerator. All organic matter present in the waste is removed as carbon dioxide and water. The ash left behind is removed from time to time and used in landfill.
6. Sewage treatment:- The ultimate return of used water to the environment is done by treating sewage. Sewage treatment involves three stages-primary, secondary and tertiary. The solids are first separated from the waste water. The dissolved biological matter is slowly converted into a solid mass by using water-borne bacteria. Finally, the biological solids are neutralized and then disposed off or reduced. The treated water is disinfected chemically. The final effluent is either discharged into a stream or water body or used for irrigation.
7. Bioremediation:- This is a rapidly developing clean up technology. Cleaning the environment with biological options such as microbes and plants is called bioremediation. Some naturally occuring bacteria and other micro- organisms have the capability to degrade or absorb or detoxify the wastes such as heavy metals.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-199.jpg?height=152&width=421&top_left_y=2213&top_left_x=260)
8. Find out how much waste is generated at your home on daily basis?
9. Categorize and calculate the amount of non-biodegradable waste?
10. Check with the local bodies, like Municipal Corporation for the various mechanisms being implemented for the treatment of both biodegradable and non-biodegradable waste separately.

## Assertion \& Reason:

DIRECTIONS : Each of these questions contains an assertion followed by reason. Read them carefully and answer the question on the basis of following options. You have to select the one that best describes the two statements.
(a) If both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
(b) If both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
(c) If Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
(d) If Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

1. Assertion: In an ecosystem, the function of producers is to convert organic compounds into inorganic compounds.
Reason: Green plants, the producers, transduce solar energy.
2. Assertion: Ecology is study of relationship between living organisms and their environment.
Reason: The biotic community and non-living environment of an area function together to form an ecosystem.
3. Assertion: Animals adopt different strategies to survive in hostile environment.
Reason: Praying mantis is green in colour which merges with plant foliage.
4. Assertion: Abiotic component of an ecosystem involves cycling of material and flow of energy.
Reason: This is essential to keep biotic factors alive.
5. Assertion: The crown fires are most destructive as they burn the tree top.
Reason: Due to crown fire, the temperature of that area may rise upto $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
6. Assertion: Trophic levels are formed by only plants.

Reason: Food chains and webs are formed due to linked organisms on the basis of their nutrition.
7. Assertion: A network of food chains existing together in an ecosystem is known as food web.
Reason: An animal like kite cannot be a part of a food web.
8. Assertion: Supersonic jets cause pollution as they thin out ozone.
Reason: Depletion of ozone cause green house effect.
9. Assertion: Tropical rain forests are disappearing fast from developing countries such as India.
Reason: No value is attached to these forests because these are poor in biodiversity.

## DIRECTIONS : Read the following statements and write your answer as true or false.

4. Biodegradable wastes should be separated and kept in blue colour bins for garbage collectors.
5. Blue green algae are producers.
6. The reproduction and other activities of living organisms are affected by the abiotic components of ecosystem.
7. Specific enzymes are needed for the break-down of a particular substance.
8. The materials like plastics are not acted upon by physical process.
9. Secondary consumers in a food chain are always carnivores.
10. Carbon dioxide causes depletion of ozone layer thereby allowing more UV-radiations to reach the earth.
11. Organisms can make organic compounds from inorganic substances by using the radiant energy of the sun in the presence of chlorophyll.
12. Ecology is the scientific study of the interaction of organisms with each other and the environment.
13. The abiotic components of the environment are the living factors.
14. The amount of usable energy remains constant as it is passed from one trophic level to another.
15. The energy within an ecosystem is fixed and never changes.
16. Human population and technology are having a destructive impact on the biosphere.

## Passage Based Questions:

DIRECTIONS : Study the given paragraph ( $s$ ) and answer the following questions.

## Passage

1. Some hunters are roaming in the plush green forest of Africa. They spot a deer and kill it. They decide to roast the deer there are then and eat it. When the hunters had just finished enjoying the feast of roasted deer, a lion attacks them. The lion kills one of the hunters and eats his flesh.
(a) Write a food chain which provides food to lion in this case.
(b) Which animal (other than deer) the lion could look for food if he did not get the hunter as prey?
(c) Which other animal in the forest could have been in place of lion?
(d) How does the above food chain differ from the food chain such as: plants ⟶ goat ⟶ man?
2. The surface of water in a lake appears green due to a layer of tiny free-floating organisms X on its surface. The lake water also contains organisms like water beetle, fish and tadpole. The sun shines over the lake water and provides energy for the functioning of this lake ecosystem.
(a) What could organisms X be?
(b) Write a food chain comprising of all the four organisms mentioned.
(c) What is the general name of the food chains like the one written above?
(d) Name (i) secondary consumer (ii) producer (iii) tertiary consumer, and (iv) primary consumer, in the above food chain.
(e) If the tertiary consumer gets 0.2 J of energy from the secondary consumer, then how much energy was radiated by the sun to the producer?

Case Study Based MCQs
(CBSE Sample Issued 2021)

## Ozone Depletion

1. Read the following and answer any four questions from 1 (i) to $1(\mathrm{v})$.
Ozone ( $\mathrm{O}_{3}$ ) is a molecule formed by three atoms of oxygen. While $\mathrm{O}_{2}$, which we normally refer to as oxygen,
is essential for all arobic forms of life. Ozone is a deadly poison. However, at the higher levels of the atomsphere, ozone performs an essential function. It sheilds the surface of the earth from ultraviolet (UV) radiation from the Sun. This radiation is highly damaging to organisms, for example , it is known to cause skin cancer in human beings. Ozone at the higher levels of the atmosphere is a product of UV radiation acting on oxgen ( $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ ) molecule. The higher energy UV radiations split apart some moleculer oxygen ( $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ ) into free oxygen ( O ) atoms. The amount of ozone in the atmoshphere began to drop sharply in the 1980s. This decrease has been linked to synthetic chemicals like chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) which are used as refrigerants and in fire extinguishers. In 1987, the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) succeeded in forging an agreemennt to freeze CFC production at 1986 levels. It is now mandatory for all the manufacturing companies to make CFC-free referigerators throughout the world.
(i) Ozone molecule is made up of
(a) 3 oxygen atoms
(b) 2 oxygen atoms
(c) 4 oxygen atoms
(d) 1 oxygen atoms
(ii) Ozone shields the surface of the earth from which radiation?
(a) IR
(b) U.V.
(c) Microwave
(d) X-ray
(iii) The amount of ozone in the atmosphere began to drop sharply in which decade of the twentieth century?
(a) 1979
(b) 1981
(c) 1980
(d) None of these
(iv) The United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) succeeded in forging an agreement to freeze CFC production at which levels?
(a) 1987
(b) 1986
(c) 1980
(d) 1985
(v) UV radiations are known to cause $\_\_\_\_$ in humans.
(a) Skin cancer
(b) Lung cancer
(c) Asthama
(d) Eye defect

## Text - Book Exercise:

1. Which of the following groups contain only biodegradable items ?
(a) Grass, flowers and leather
(b) Grass, wood and plastic
(c) Fruit-peels, cake and lime-juice
(d) Cake, wood and grass
2. Which of the following constitute a food-chain?
(a) Grass, wheat and mango
(b) Grass, goat and human
(c) Goat, cow and elephant
(d) Grass, fish and goat
3. Which of the following are environment-friendly practices?
(a) Carrying cloth-bags to put purchases in while shopping.
(b) Switching off unnecessary lights and fans.
(c) Walking to school instead of getting your mother to drop you on her scooter.
(d) All of the above.
4. What will happen if we kill all the organisms in one trophic level?
5. Will the impact of removing all the organisms in a trophic level be different for different trophic levels? Can the organisms of any trophic level be removed without causing any damage to the ecosystem?
6. What is biological magnification? Will the levels of this magnification be different at different levels of the ecosystem?
7. What are the problems caused by the non-biodegradable wastes that we generate?
8. If all the waste we generate is biodegradable, will this have no impact on the environment?

(a) decomposers
(b) producers
(c) herbivores
(d) carnivores

(a) All green plants and blue-green algae are producers.
(b) Green plants get their food from organic compounds.
(c) Producers prepare their own food from inorganic compounds.
(d) Plants convert solar energy into chemical energy.
3. Which of the following limits the number of trophic levels in a food chain?
(a) Decrease in energy at higher trophic levels.
(b) Deficient food supply.
(c) Polluted air.
(d) Water.

(a) $\mathrm{T}_{4}$
(b) $\mathrm{T}_{2}$
(c) $\mathrm{T}_{1}$
(d) $\mathrm{T}_{3}$

Grass → Deer → Tiger
(a) The population of tiger increases.
(b) The population of grass decreases.
(c) Tiger will start eating grass.
(d) The population of tiger decreases and the population of grass increases.

## Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs):

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has four choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONLY ONE is correct.

1. Each step in a food chain is called a
(a) trophic level.
(b) consumer level.
(c) food web.
(d) producer.
2. As a biologist, if you become very interested in the study of the interaction of organisms with each other and the environment your subspeciality would be
(a) Zoology
(b) Ecology
(c) Botany
(d) Herpetology
3. The last chain of food is
(a) producers
(b) decomposers
(c) parasites
(d) none of the above
4. Trophic levels are formed by -
(a) only plants
(b) only animals
(c) only carnivores
(d) organisms linked in food chain
5. The part of earth comprising water is called an
(a) atmosphere
(b) hydrosphere
(c) lithosphere
(d) none of the above
6. The maximum energy is stored at following tropical level in any ecosystem
(a) Producers
(b) Herbivores
(c) Carnivores
(d) Top carnivores
7. Pyramids of energy are
(a) always upright
(b) always inverted
(c) mostly upright
(d) mostly inverted
8. Individuals of any species at a place form
(a) biotic community
(b) ecosystem
(c) population
(d) biome
9. City garbage can be used to produce
(a) sewage sludge
(b) useful articles
(c) biogas and manure
(d) All the above.
10. Acid rain is the downpour of
(a) carbon dioxide in rain
(b) dust in rain
(c) sulphur dioxide in rain
(d) oxygen in rain
11. Select the most dangerous pollutant
(a) CO
(b) $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$
(c) $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$
(d) $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
12. Ozone layer is essential because it absorbs most of the
(a) infrared radiations
(b) heat
(c) solar radiation
(d) ultraviolet-radiation
13. The biotic and abiotic components interacting with each other in a pond form
(a) a community
(b) a population
(c) an ecosystem
(d) a biome
14. Environment consists of
(a) land, air, water
(b) light, temperature and rainfall
(c) plants, animals and microbes
(d) All the above.
15. Carnivores represent
(a) primary consumers
(b) secondary and tertiary consumers
(c) reducers
(d) zooplankton.
16. World environment day is celebrated on
(a) $15^{\text {th }}$ March
(b) $15{ }^{\text {th }}$ April
(c) $4^{\text {th }}$ May
(d) $5^{\text {th }}$ June
17. Flow of energy in an ecosystem is always
(a) unidirectional
(b) bidirectional
(c) multi-directional
(d) no specific direction

(b) Aluminium cans
(c) DDT
(d) Cattle dung

(a) Exposed surface of metal
(b) Moisture
(c) Air
(d) All these

(a) Infra-red radiation
(b) Visible light
(c) Ultra-violet
(d) All these
21. In an ecosystem green plants are known as
(a) primary consumers
(b) secondary consumers
(c) producers
(d) tertiary consumers
22. Carbon monoxide is a pollutant because
(a) It reacts with $\mathrm{O}_{2}$
(b) It inhibits glycolysis
(c) Reacts with haemoglobin
(d) Makes nervous system inactive

(b) $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$
(c) Pb particles
(d) Radioactive disintegrations

(c) NO
(d) $\mathrm{CFCl}_{3}$

(b) Carnivores
(c) Producers

(c) $8 \%$
(d) $10 \%$

(a) Haemoglobin of blood

(b) (i) and (ii)

## Column - II

I. An organism that eats meat.
II. An organism that eats plants.
III. Anorganismthatmakes its food from light or chemical energy.
IV. An organism that gets its energy by eating other organisms.
V. The sequence of organisms of who eats whom in a biological community.
VI. The network of all the interrelatedfoodchains in a biological community.
(a) A - V; B - VI; C - IV; D - III; E - I; F - II
(b) A-VI; B-IV; C-III; D-I; E-II; F-V
(c) $\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{III} ; \mathrm{B}-\mathrm{I} ; \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{II} ; \mathrm{D}-\mathrm{V} ; \mathrm{E}-\mathrm{VI} ; \mathrm{F}-\mathrm{IV}$
(d) $\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{II}, \mathrm{B}-\mathrm{V}$; $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{VI}$; $\mathrm{D}-\mathrm{IV}$; $\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{III}$; $\mathrm{F}-\mathrm{I}$

(b) Statement (A) is correct but (B) is incorrect.
(c) Statement (A) and (B) both are incorrect.
(d) Statement (A) is incorrect but (B) is correct.

(b) Pyramid of biomass in pond ecosystem

## Column - I

A. Phosphorus
B. Carbon
C. Goat
D. Grasses

## Column - II

I. Atmosphere
II. Producers
III. Rock
IV. $\mathrm{T}_{2}$

[NTSE]
(a) petroleum refinery

[NTSE]

[KVPY]
(a) Grass since it grows in the contaminated soil
(b) Goat since it eats the grass
(c) Tiger since it feeds on the goat which feeds on the grass
(d) Vulture since it eats the tiger, which in turn eats the goat, which eats the grass

(b) Increase in carbon dioxide
(c) The green-house effect will be most significance at the poles
(d) Energy is lost at each trophic level of a food chain

(b) Radiation fails to escape from the glass house completely

(d) Plants produce heat inside the glass house

(d) II, III and IV

(b) Fishes
(c) Spirogyra
(d) Zooplanktons

[NTSE]

(b) Chemical amplification
(c) Biomagnification
(d) Chemical magnification

## Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs):

DIRECTIONS (Qs. 1-21) : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has four choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONLY ONE is correct.

1. In an ecosystem the function of the producers is:
(a) To convert organic compound into inorganic form
(b) To utilise chemical energy
(c) To trap solar energy and convert into chemical energy
(d) To release energy
2. Which of the following food chain produces a greater availablity of food to next trophic level?
(a) Diatoms → Fish → Large fish → Birds → Man
(b) Green plant → Rabbit → Man
(c) Green plant → Grasshopper → Frog → Snake → Peacock
(d) Green plant → Rabbit → wolf → Man
3. If a big fish eats small fish which eats Hydra which in turn eats water fleas. Water fleas in turn eat phytoplanktons. In this chain, water fleas will be:
(a) Producers
(b) Primary consumers
(c) Secondary consumers
(d) Top consumer
4. Which of the following pyramids represents the variation in biomass at different trophic levels in pond ecosystem?

5. Which of the following statement is incorrect about energy flow in an ecosystem?
(a) Pyramid of energy is always straight.
(b) Energy flow is unidirectional.
(c) Energy flow is cyclic.
(d) Ecological efficiency is generally $10 \%$.
6. Whale is:
(a) Primary producer
(b) Carnivorous, secondary consumer
(c) A decomposer
(d) Herbivorous
7. Which of the following strategy is an incorrect approach to reduce global warming?
(a) Reducing the greenhouse gas emission by limiting the use of fossil fuels.
(b) Increase the vegetation cover particularly the forest for photosynthetic utilisation of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$.
(c) Minimising the use of nitrogen fertilisers in agriculture for reducing $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ emission.
(d) Increasing the use of air conditioners, refrigeration unit and production of plastic foams and propellants in aerosal spray cans.
8. Today the concentration of greenhouse gases is very high because of:
(a) Use of refrigerator
(b) Increased combustion of oils and coal
(c) Deforestation
(d) All of the above
9. Which of the following would be most likely to help to slow down the greenhouse effect?
(a) Ensuring that all excess paper packaging is burned to ashes.
(b) Promoting the use of private rather than public transport.
(c) Converting tropical forests into grazing land for cattle.
(d) Re-designing land-fill dumps to allow methane to be collected.
10. Global warming can be controlled by:
(a) reducing reforestation, increasing the use of fossil fuel.
(b) increasing deforestation, slowing down the growth of human population.
(c) increasing deforestation, reducing efficiency of energy usage.
(d) reducing deforestation, cutting down use of fossil fuel.
11. During ecological succession:
[JSTSE]
(a) the establishment of a new biotic community is very fast in its primary phase.
(b) the numbers and types of animals remain constant.
(c) the changes lead to a community that is in near equilibrium with the environment and is called pioneer community.
(d) the gradual and predictable change in species composition occurs in a given area.
12. An association of individuals of different species living in the same habitat and having functional interactions is:
[JSTSE]
(a) Biotic community
(b) Ecosystem
(c) Population
(d) Ecological niche
13. Increase in concentration of the toxicant at successive trophic levels is known as:
[JSTSE]
(a) Biodeterioration
(b) Biotransformation
(c) Biogeochemical
(d) Biomagnification
14. Which of the following would appear as the pioneer organisms on bare rocks?
[JSTSE]
(a) Lichens
(b) Liverworts
(c) Mosses
(d) Green algae
15. The term ecosystem was coined by:
[JSTSE]
(a) E.P. Odum
(b) A.G. Tansley
(c) E.Haeckel
(d) E. Warming
16. Which of the following is a characteristic feature of cropland ecosystem?
[JSTSE]
(a) Absence of soil organisms
(b) Least genetic diversity
(c) Absence of weeds
(d) Ecological succession
17. Which ecosystem has the maximum biomass?
[JSTSE]
(a) Grassland ecosystem
(b) Pond ecosystem
(c) Lake ecosystem
(d) Forest ecosystem
18. What type of ecological pyramid would be obtained with the following data?
[JSTSE]
Secondary consumer : 120 g
Primary consumer : 60 g
Primary producer : 10 g
(a) Inverted pyramid of biomass
(b) Pyramid of energy
(c) Upright pyramid of biomass
(d) Upright pyramid of numbers
19. Which one of the following substance is non-biodegradable?
[JSTSE]
(a) Paper
(b) Manure
(c) Cotton cloth
(d) DDT
20. Which of the following ecological pyramids is generally inverted?
[JSTSE]
(a) Pyramid of numbers in grassland
(b) Pyramid of energy
(c) Pyramid of biomass in a forest
(d) Pyramid of biomass in a sea
21. Which of the following pairs of gases is mainly responsible for green house effect?
[JSTSE]
(a) Ozone and Ammonia
(b) Oxygen and Nitrogen
(c) Nitrogen and Sulphur dioxide
(d) Carbon dioxide and Methane
22. Montreal protocol was signed in 1987 for control of
[JSTSE]
(a) Emission of ozone depleting substances
(b) Release of Green House gases
(c) Disposal of e-wastes
(d) Transport of Genetically modified organisms from one country to another
23. Snow-blindness in Antarctic region is due to
[JSTSE]
(a) Inflammation of cornea due to high dose of UV-B radiation
(b) High reflection of light from snow
(c) Damage to retina caused by infra-red rays
(d) Freezing of fluids in the eye by low temperature
24. In water hyacinth and water lily, pollination takes place by :
[JSTSE]
(a) Water currents only
(b) Wind and water
(c) Insects and water
(d) Insects or wind

## Very Short Answer Questions:

1. A food web is the natural interconnection of food chains and a graphical representation (usually an image) of what-eats-what) in an ecological community.
2. Zooplanktons are free floating small animals which usually feed on phytoplanktons, e.g., Daphnia, Paramaecium.
3. The trophic level of an organism is the position it occupies in a food chain.
4. Biomass is amount of organic matter present in organisms.
5. It is the reduction in the concentration of ozone in the ozone layer or ozonosphere.
6. Ten percent law is an enunciation given by Raymond Lindeman (1942) which states that only $10 \%$ of energy contained in a lower trophic level is trapped by next higher tropic level, the remaining $90 \%$ being lost in transfer and respiration of the latter.
7. Ecological pyramid is a graphic representation of an ecological parameter (like biomass) sequence wise with producers at the base, herbivores above them followed by carnivores, secondary carnivores, etc. with the tip occupied by top carnivores.
8. Primary carnivores are called secondary consumers because they feed on first order or primary consumers (herbivores).
9. Biological magnification (= biomagnification) is the increase in concentration of a chemical per unit weight of the organisms with successive rise in trophic level.
10. Ozone is a molecule made of three atoms of oxygen, O3. Ozone layer is located at a height of $11-16 \mathrm{~km}$ above poles and $23-25 \mathrm{~km}$ above 'equator in stratosphere.
11. Plastics and pesticides.
12. Natural ecosystem: Forests

Man-made ecosystem: Gardens
13. Producers are those living organisms which can produce their own food by the process of photosynthesis (green plants).
14. Micro-organisms, animals and plants.
15. Those organisms which cannot manufacture their food by the process of photosynthesis and depend either on plants or other organisms are known as consumers.
16. Soil, water/air.
17. The two components of ecosystem are:
(i) Biotic components (all living organisms).
(ii) Abiotic components all (non-living components).
18. Hydrofluorocarbon (HFC) and hydrochlorofluorocarbon (HCFC).
19. The names of aquatic organisms in order to form food chain is given below. Algae → insect → small fish → big fish.
20. UNEP: United Nations Environment Programme.

CFC: Chlorofluorocarbons.
21. Due to incomplete combustion of fossil fuels carbon monoxide is produced in the air.
22. Biotic Components: Producers, consumers and decomposers.
Abiotic Components. Climatic factors (light, temperature, humidity, rainfall, air currents), edaphic factors (soil), topographic factors (surface of earth), inorganic and organic substances.
23. UV-radiations are very harmful as it causes skin cancer in human beings.
24. In biogeochemical cycles, essential nutrients like nitrogen, carbon, oxygen and water are changed from one form to another.
25. Two natural source of air pollution are forest fire and volcanic eruptions.
26. Ecosystems are sustained by a continuous flow of energy from sunlight and a constant recycling of nutrients.
27. Decomposers are essential because they recycle nutrients from dead organisms to the physical environment, where the nutrients may again be used by another organism.
28. The two main sources of water pollution are :
(i) Industries (ii) Sewage
29. Plants are called transducers or converters because they convert light energy into chemical energy.
30. When tertiary consumers are destroyed in a food chain then increase in number of secondary consumers is observed.
31. UNEP convention of 1987 succeeded in forging agreement for reduction in the production of chlorofluorocarbons to half the level of 1986.
32. Carbon monoxide (CO).
33. The green plants prepare food by photosynthesis by using solar energy.
34. 10000 J

## Short Answer Questions:

1. 

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-211.jpg?height=365&width=449&top_left_y=1059&top_left_x=1231)
Fig. A simple food web found around us

2. Disappearance of ozone layer will allow the passage of UV radiations to earth causing blindness in animals, skin cancers and cataract in humans, reduced photosynthesis and death of many organisms in larval stages.
3. Undesirable-activities of man eliminate the growth of organisms belonging to one or more trophic levels in a food chain. Thus, the food chain gets shortened, e.g. hunting tigers for their skin, etc.
It causes imbalance in the functioning of the ecosystem and biosphere. If organisms of one trophic level are eliminated, the organisms prior to that trophic level will flourish and increase in number and also the organisms of the subsequent trophic level will sharply decrease, thereby creating an imbalance.
4. (a) The loss of energy at each step of food chain is so great that very little usable energy remains after four trophic levels. Hence, food chains generally consist of only three or four steps.
(b) $10 \%$
5. (i) If the lions are removed herbivores will increase and then less amount of grass would lead to soil erosion.
(ii) If the deers are removed then food available for lions would be less. Amount of grassland will increase.
6. (i) In a food chain, the energy always moves progressively through the various trophic levels and is no longer available to the organisms of the previous trophic level/energy captured by the autotrophs does not go back to the solar input.
(ii) Pesticides are used in agriculture for crop protection. When washed away/down into the soil/ water bodies absorbed by plants/producers.
(iii) Upon consumption, they enter into our food chain and being non - biodegradable, these chemicals get progressively accumulated in our body.
7. (i) The existence of decomposers is in biosphere because they break very crucial complex organic substances into simple inorganic substances that can be absorbed by the plants
(a) It helps to build up the soil naturally.
(b) It helps in extracting the biodegradable waste.
(ii) In a food chain, the energy always moves progressively through the various trophic levels, it is no longer available to the previous trophic level (autotrophs) and the energy captured by the autotrophs does not revert back to the solar input. Hence, the flow of energy is unidirectional.
8. (i) Ozone layer helps in shielding the Earth from the lethal UV radiation coming from sun. If ozone layer gets depleted, UV radiation will directly reach the earth's surface and seriously affect the life on earth.
(ii) Ozone layer can be protected by: (any two)
(a) Restriction in release of chlorofluorocarbons.
(b) Eliminating the pollutant nitrogen monoxide and carbon monoxide.
(c) Less usage of air conditioners.
9. Waste disposable is the term used for collecting, processing or recycling waste materials.
We can help in reducing the problem of waste disposal by adopting the following methods:
(i) Minimising the use of non-biodegradable material.
(ii) Recycling the non-biodegradable wastes like plastic.
(iii) Production of compost, biogas from bio-degradable wastes.

An ecosystem is a biological community of interacting organisms and their physical environment. It provides the food chain through which energy flows and the biological cycles that recycle essential nutrients and wastes. The given diagram showing the flow of energy in an ecosystem.

## Long Answer Questions:

1. A food chain in a grassland ecosystem may consist of grasses and other plants, grasshoppers, frogs, snakes and hawks. A food chain always begins with producers. Herbivores (plant-eaters) come next in the chain. They are consumed by carnivores (flesh-eaters).
(I) Producers: The primary producers of food are the grasses. If herbs and shrubs are present, they also contribute to the primary production of food.
(II) Consumers: The consumers in a grassland ecosystem are of three levels.
(a) Primary consumers: These feed directly from the grasses (grazing) and include herbivores such as Cows, Buffaloes, Goats, Rabbits, Mouse etc. and also insects, termites, centipede, millipedes etc.
(b) Secondary consumers: These consumers are the carnivorous animals such as snakes, lizard, jackal, foxes, frogs etc. which feed on the primary consumers.
(c) Tertiary consumers: Hawk, Eagles and vultures constitute the tertiary consumer in the grassland ecosystem which preys upon the secondary and primary consumer.
(III) Decomposers: The organic matter of the grassland is decomposed by the microbes like actinomycetes, fungi (Mucor, Aspergillus, Rhizopus, Penicillium, and Cladosporium), aerobic and anaerobic soil bacteria etc. They release the minerals back into the soil thus making the soil fertile.
2. (i) Soil - Fertilizer added to soil not only changes the chemistry of the soil but also kills many useful microbes.
(ii) Ground Water - A part of fertilizer always leaches down into soil and reaches ground water. It raises the salt content of ground water.
(iii) Eutrophication - Run-off from fields sprayed with fertilizer reaches water bodies. It results in their eutrophication.
(iv) Pesticides - Pesticides sprayed over crops reach water bodies killing the biota. Persistent pesticides undergo biomagnification and prove highly harmful to higher organisms.
(vi) Ground Water - Continued use of ground water in agriculture has resulted in lowering of water table at most of the places.
(vii) Irrigation - It causes water-logging and salivation of soils.
(viii) Genetic Erosion - Use of only selected high yielding varieties has resulted in genetic erosion of the crop plants.
(ix) Damage to Nature - Natural ecosystems and habitats have been damaged during clearing land for agriculture.
3. Fertilizer industries produce mainly two types of wastes
(a) Gaseous (b) Effluents. Additionally they may release heat and fuel waste if coal is being used as a source of energy.
(a) Gaseous wastes - They are passed through wet scrubbers to dissolve the pollutant gases.
(b) Effluents - The effluents of the industry are tested for chemicals present in them. The same can be
retrieved and made available to the market. Hot effluents are cooled. Acidic or alkaline nature can be corrected. Heavy metals and toxins can be separated. Only treated and harmless effluents are allowed to be discharged into surrounding environment.
4. (i) Green plants in a terrestrial ecosystem capture about $1 \%$ of the energy of sunlight that falls on their leaves.
(ii) When herbivore (primary consumers) eat plants a great deal of energy is lost as heat to the environment, some amount is used for various life processes. An average of $10 \%$ of the food eaten is turned into its own body and made available for the next level of consumers.
(iii) The loss of energy at each step is so great that very little usable energy remains after four trophic levels.
(iv) In a food chain the greatest number of individuals are at lower trophic level of an ecosystem. The greatest number is of the producers.

## Reasoning Based Questions:

1. It's better to use paper bags than plastic bags because paper is recyclable whereas plastic is not. Plastic cannot be degraded and they pollute the environment; whereas paper is biodegradable and does not cause any pollution. Moreover, even if burned, paper will not cause much pollution but plastics on burning will release toxic chemicals into the environment thus polluting the environment.
2. Community interactions influence population size and the interacting populations with communities act on one another as agents of natural selection. Thus, community interactions also shape the bodies and behaviors of the interacting populations.
3. Kulhdas or clay cups are made from the soil or clay that is used for agriculture and is very fertile. So if these cups are made in large scales then the fertility of the soil decreases and renders the land unfertile and unfit for cultivation.
4. There are rarely more than five links (five organisms) in a food chain because there is a decrease in energy with every transfer to next trophic level and energy availability will be so small that animals will not be able to survive with small amount of energy.
5. An ecosystem does not have its own source of energy. It receives the same from sun. Green plants or producers trap the solar energy and change it into chemical form during synthesis of food. Herbivores obtain energy from the food they take. A lot of energy dissipates during transfer and utilization of food energy by herbivores
( $10 \%$ law). From herbivores the food energy passes to primary carnivores. However, only about $10 \%$ of herbivore energy is passed into body mass of primary carnivores. The rest is dissipated. From primary carnivores the energy passes into secondary carnivores ( $10 \%$ ), etc. It is ultimately lost as heat.
6. An aquarium is an artificial system which is also incomplete due to absence of producers, food chains and decomposers. There is no recycling and self-cleaning. However, a pond or a lake is a self-sustained, natural and complete ecosystem where there is perfect recycling of nutrients.
7. Pesticides are non- biodegradable chemicals, so they get accumulated in each tropic. Since human occupy the top position in each tropic level, so the maximum amount of harmful chemical pesticides get accumulated in our bodies, which damages our health gradually.

## HOTS Questions:

1. Elimination of decomposers will cause (i) Piling up of excretions, litter and dead bodies of plants and animals. Soon there will be no free space for new generations of organisms. (ii) Non-cycling of biogenetic nutrients. As a result all the available biogenetic nutrients will be tied up in organic matter. Further synthesis of organic matter will stop resulting in destruction of all life.
2. A good amount of garbage is generated each day in the cities which may consist of glass bottles, crockery, plastic containers, polythene and other packing materials, automobile spares, machines, cycle parts, construction wastes, sludge, dead animal skeletons, crop residues, etc. These clog drains, do not look aesthetic, may be eaten mistakenly by animals, and pile up at public places and obstruct daily life. Heavy metals from garbage may leach into the soil and cause ground water pollution. Garbage may stink giving bad odour and fumes all around the area. Most of the garbage is non-biodegradable and causes immense harm to the environment.
3. Cause of Concern: Ozone layer present in the stratosphere has thinned out by about $8 \%$ over the equator and more so over the antarctica where a big ozone hole appears every year. This has increased the level of UV-B radiations reaching the earth by 15 $20 \%$. These radiations are causing increased number of skin cancers, cataracts and reduced immunity in human beings. There is increased incidence of blinding of animals, death of young ones, reduced photosynthesis, higher number of mutations and damage to organisms.

## Steps to Limit Damage :

(i) Ban on production and use of halons.
(ii) Ban on production and use of chlorofluorocarbons.
4. An improper disposal of wastes means addition of pollutants into environment-air, water, soil. They will harm living beings, human assets and human beings. For example, passage of sewage into water body will cause europhication, stink development of sludge, killing of animals and source of water borne pathogens.
5. Some examples of actions that are good for the global environment: Reduce the quantity of nonfood items purchased; reuse items until they are no longer usable; buy used items rather than getting everything new. Recycle paper, plastic, glass, and metal. Bring reusable cloth bags when shopping. Plant trees and other native plants, especially those that help feed the native wildlife. Reduce water use by not leaving water running when brushing teeth, by adjusting the water level of washing machines to match the size of the load, and by using water-saving fixtures. Reduce fossil fuel use by choosing a gas-efficient car and by using household heating and air conditioning only as needed. Use compact fluorescent light bulbs, and turn off lights that are not in use. Support organic farmers by purchasing organically grown food.
Some examples of actions that are harmful for the global environment: Use non-recyclable products and products that are not from recycled sources. Drive alone rather than carpooling or taking public transportation. Use a gas or electric-powered lawn mower. Make excessive purchases of non-essential items (gadgets, many sets of clothes, extra cars, vacation homes, etc.). Drive a lowmileage vehicle, such as a sport utility vehicle or truck, out of choice, not necessity.
6. Abiotic Components - Light, Soil Biotic Components - Deer, Tiger, Tree, Grass.
7. CFCs are widely used as coolants in refrigerators and air-conditioners, in fire extinguishers, aerosol spray and as propellants. Once released they enter the atmosphere and cause depletion of ozone layer. Depletion of ozone layer leads to the passage of more UV-radiations through it. This. may cause skin cancer, cataract and damage to our immune system.

Case Study Based MCQs
(CBSE Sample Issued 2021)

## Ozone Depletion

1. (i) (a) Three oxygen utoms
(ii) (b) ultraviolet (UV) radiation
(iii) (c) 1980 s
(iv) (d) 1986
(v) (a) Skin cancer

## Text - Book Questions:

1. The micro-organisms like bacteria and other decomposer organisms (called saprophytes) present in our environment are 'specific' in their action. They break down the natural materials or products made from natural materials (say, paper) but do not break down man-made materials such as plastics. So, it is due to the property of decomposer organisms of being specific in their action that some waste materials are biodegradable whereas others are non-biodegradable.
2. The two ways in which biodegradable substances would affect the environment are:
(i) Biodegradable wastes usually do not pollute the environment. It pollutes the environment only when their amount is large and cannot be degraded (or decomposed) into harmless substances in nature at the right time.
(ii) The biodegradable substance mainly contains carbon. These substances after decomposition release that carbon back to the atmosphere.
3. The two ways in which non-biodegradable substances affect the environment are:
(i) Non-biodegradable wastes are the major pollutants of the environment. For example, the discarded plastic articles, glass articles and metal objects are the non-biodegradable waste materials which cause a lot of pollution in our surroundings.
(ii) Use of non-biodegradable substances such as polythene results in blocking of drainage system and also harms animals when they are eaten by animals.
4. The various steps in a food chain at which the transfer of food (or energy) takes place are called trophic levels. The producers form the first trophic level as they manufacture food. The primary consumers form the second trophic level, the secondary consumers form the third, and the tertiary consumers form the fourth trophic level.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-215.jpg?height=179&width=712&top_left_y=2019&top_left_x=319)

## Fig.: Food chain showing different trophic levels

In the above figure, grass is the producer (first trophic level) which is eaten by the grasshopper known as primary consumer (second trophic level). Then the grasshopper is eaten by shrew, secondary consumer (third trophic level) and lastly shrew eaten up by owl, tertiary consumer (fourth trophic level).
5. The role of decomposers in the ecosystem are as follows:
(i) The decomposers help in decomposing the dead bodies of plants and animals, and hence act as cleansing agents of environment.
(ii) Decomposer organisms help in recycling the materials in the ecosystem so that the process of life may go on and on like an unending chain.
(iii) They breakdown complex organic matter into its basic components (carbon, nitrogen etc.) and release them into the environment.
(iv) They feed on dead and decaying organisms and thus prevent the foul odour that spreads from the decaying organic matter.

## Text - Book Exercise:

1. (c) and (d) The term biodegradable is used to describe materials that decompose through the actions of bacteria, fungi, and other living organisms. Temperature and sunlight may also play roles in the decomposition of biodegradable plastics and other substances.
Example: human and animal excreta, plant products like rubber, paper, wood, leaves, cotton, and wool, dead remains of living organisms, kitchen waste, agricultural waste.
2. (b) Food chain is a linear sequence of organisms which starts from producer organism and ends with decomposer species. In the given group, grass is a producer, goat is a herbivore and human is top carnivore.
3. (d) Eco-friendly products promote green living that helps to conserve energy and also prevent air, water and noise pollution. They prove to be a boon for the environment and also prevent human health from deterioration.
4. If we kill all the organisms in one trophic level, then it will disturb the entire ecosystem. The transfer of food energy to the next trophic level will stop. This will cause a break in the food chain. As a result, the organisms of the higher trophic level will also die, while the individuals of the lower trophic level will exhibit enormous growth in their population.
5. Yes, removing all the organisms in a tropic level is different for different tropic levels. If the organisms of any trophic level be removed it will certainly damage the ecosystem. For example, if all the producers are killed, it will cause death or migration of primary consumers in an ecosystem. But if primary consumers are removed, it will lead to the cause of death of higher trophic level, while those are of lower level (producers) will show an exponential growth.

The survival of organism of one trophic level depends upon the existence of the member of second trophic level. All the categories of organisms are linked with food chain, so removal of the organisms in a trophic level will disturb the whole ecosystem.
6. The increase in concentration of harmful chemical substances like pesticides in the body of living organisms at each trophic level of a food chain is called biological magnification.
Yes, magnification will be different at different trophic levels. The level of these harmful substances will go on increasing from one trophic level to the next because when certain harmful substances enter the food chain at the level of primary producers, they get concentrated many times at each subsequent trophic level.
7. The problems caused by the non-biodegradable wastes that we generate are as follows:
(i) Non-biodegradable wastes persist in the environment for a long time and cause greater harm to the various members of the ecosystem by causing biological magnification.
(ii) Some of the non-biodegradable waste (like metals, DDT, plastics) enter the food chain of the human and damage their health for long run.
(iii) Non-biodegradable waste such as fertilisers, pesticides, weedicides, etc., changes the soil chemistry which affects the fertility of soil and subsequently reduces the crop yield.
(iv) Burning non-biodegradable wastes can lead to produce harmful gases in the environment causing air pollution.
(v) It leads to the destruction of balance of ecological environment.
8. If all the waste we generate is biodegradable then it will have an adverse effect on the environment. It affects the environment in the following ways:
(i) Too much of biodegradable waste will not be broken down into simpler substances by decomposers at the right time, which will go on accumulating in the environment and act as a pollutant.
(ii) Slow decomposition of biodegradable waste leads to the release of foul smell and harmful gases. When these gases inhaled by human beings, they may cause nausea, irritation, etc.
(iii) Increase in the population of micro-organism in an aquatic medium, leads to the deficiency of oxygen in water bodies.
(iv) Blockage of drains due to the accumulation of large amount of bio-degradable waste also causes unhygienic conditions and multiplication of mosquitoes, which spread diseases like malaria and dengue.

## Exemplar Questions:

1. (b) Organisms which synthesise carbohydrates from inorganic compounds using radiant energy are called producers e.g., all green plants. The producers make the energy from sunlight available to the rest of the ecosystem.
Organisms which consume the food produced, either directly from producers or indirectly by feeding on other consumers are the consumers. Microorganisms which break-down the complex organic substances into simple inorganic substances used by plants are called decomposers. Carnivores and herbivores are types of consumers.
2. (b) Producers capture the solar energy and convert it into chemical energy. All green plants and certain blue-green algae which can produce food by photosynthesis come under this category and are called the producer.
3. (a) There is a loss of energy as we go from one trophic level to the next, this limits the number of trophic levels in a food-chain.
4. (c) 5. (d)
5. Following are the four activities which are eco-friendly?
(i) Separation of biodegradable and non-biodegradable substances.
(ii) Gardening
(iii) Use of gunny bags/paper bags in place of polythene/plastic bags.
(iv) Use of compost and vermicompost in place of fertilisers
(v) Harvesting rain water.
6. Wastes
(i) Vegetable and fruit peels and skin, stale food, food leftovers, used tea leaves.
(ii) Milk pouches, polythene bags, empty cartons.
(iii) Waste paper (newspaper, bags, envelopes), packing paper, empty bottles, torn cloth pieces, etc.
(iv) Dust and other sweepings.

Disposal :
(i) Separation into biodegradable and nonbiodegradable, recyclable and non-recyclable wastes.
(ii) Recyclable wastes (waste paper, cloth, polythene or plastic bags, cartouns, bottles, cans, etc.) can be given to rag pickers for recycling.
(iii) Preparation of compost or vermicompost from kitchen wastes for home garden (kitchen garden).
(iv) In the absence of kitchen garden, the household garbage and other wastes can be given to waste collectors for disposal.
8.

| S.No. | Food cain | Food web |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) | Food chain is a series of organisms feeding on one another. | Food web consists of a number of interlinked food chains. |
| (ii) | Members of higher trophic level feed upon a single type of organism of the lower trophic level. | Members of higher trophic level can feed upon organisms of the lower trophic levels of other food chain. |

9. Cloth bags are
(i) capable of carrying more things
(ii) made of biodegradable material
(iii) do not pollute our environment
(iv) can be reused.
10. Artificial ecosystems are those ecosystems which are modified and managed by human beings for their own benefit.
Crop fields are not like wild forest area, the piece of land which has to converted into crop field is manged. The soil is prepared for sowing seeds, then irrigated and further also kept under observation for getting good yield of crops. Thus, crop field is an artificial ecosystem.

20. (d) Ecological pyramid is a graphical representation that illustrates the flow of energy through different forms of life in an ecosystem. It is generally inverted in case of pyramid of biomass in a sea because the biomass of the fishes is more than the phytoplanktons (producers).
Features of pyramid of biomass in a sea:

- Food chain in ocean - Phytoplankton → zooplankton → predatory fish
- The biomass of consumers (zooplankton) is greater than phytoplankton (the producer) because of their small size and low weight. The predatory fish is much larger than zooplankton.
- The producers of ocean or aquatic ecosystem are phytoplankton and they have short life span and turn over and have lower mass than zooplankton.
- The biomass of tropic level is depends on the longevity of member.

21. (d) Gases, responsible for Green house effect are carbon dioxide and methane.
A green house gas is a gas that absorbs and emits radiant energy within the thermal infrared range. Greenhouse gases cause the greenhouse effect. The primary greenhouse gases in Earth's atmosphere are water vapour, carbon dioxide, methane, nitrous oxide and ozone. Without greenhouse gases, the average temperature of Earth's surface would be about " $18{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\left(0^{\circ} \mathrm{F}\right)$, rather than the present average of $15^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\left(59^{\circ} \mathrm{F}\right)$.

The main uses of ozone depleting substances include:

- CFCs and HCFCs in refrigerators and air conditioners,
- HCFCs and halons in fire extinguishers,
- CFCs and HCFCs in foam,
- CFCs and HCFCs as aerosol propellants, and
- Methyl bromide for fumigation of soil, structures and goods to be imported or exported.
