## Case Study-1

1. In an ecosystem, energy can follow more than one path as most organisms can be a part of many overlapping food chains. Hence, food web is more accurate representation of an ecosystem as compared to food chain.
2. If predators are removed from the food web, the prey population explodes as both predator-prey balances each other.
3. Decomposers break down dead animals or plants, and return nutrients to the soil.

## Case Study-2

1. The total energy at level 4 is 60 kcal , as $10 \%$ of the energy is lost at each level.
2. Since, $10 \%$ of the energy is lost at each trophic level, therefore the top levels have very low amount of energy and can't sustain large number of organisms.
3. The ecological pyramids do not consider the possibility of more than one species at one trophic level; hence it doesn't give a realistic representation of an ecosystem.

## 6. Management of Our Natural Resources

## Foundation

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-220.jpg?height=1571&width=1098&top_left_y=921&top_left_x=527)

[^2]
## NATURAL RESOURCES

Natural resources are obtained from earth and its environment, and are also know as earth resources. Resources can be natural, human or human-made.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-221.jpg?height=723&width=1541&top_left_y=513&top_left_x=294)

On the basis of their distribution, natural resources can be classified into:
(i) Inexhaustible resources are those which are present in unlimited quantity in nature and are not likely to be exhausted by human activity. For example, sunlight, air, rain etc.
(ii) Exhaustible resources are those which are limited and can be exhausted by human activity in the long run. For example, petroleum, natural gas etc.
(a) Renewable resources are those that can be replenished or reproduced easily. Some of them like wind, air, sunlight, etc. are continuously available and their quantity is not affected by man's consumption. Some renewable resources can be depleted by human use, but is also replenished, thus maintaining a flow. Agricultural crops take a short time for renewal; water takes a comparatively longer time, while forest takes even longer time.
(b) Non-renewable resources are those which are formed over very long geological periods. Minerals and fossil fuels are included in this category. As the rate of formation of fossil fuels is very slow, they can't be replenished once they get depleted.

## CONNECTING TOPIC

## Natural Resources can also be classified in three other ways:

1. On the basis of origin:
(i) Biotic resources are those which are obtained from the biosphere such as forests, animals, plants and their products. Mineral fuels such as coal and petroleum are also included in this category because, they are formed from decayed organic matter.
(ii) Abiotic resources include non-living things like land, water, air and mineral ores.
2. On the basis of their stage of development:
(i) Potential resources are those that exist in a region and may be used in the future. For example, petroleum exists in many parts of the country, but it remains a potential resource till the time it is drilled out and put to use.
(ii) Actual resources are those that have been surveyed, their quantity and quality determined and are being used in present times e.g. wood.
3. On the basis of their distribution:
(i) Natural resources are confined to national boundries e.g. minerals, forests, etc.
(ii) Multinational resources are shared by more than one nation e.g. certain rivers, migratory birds, etc.
(iii) International resources are shared uniformly by all nations e.g. sunlight, air, etc.

Why do we need to manage our resources?
Natural resources especially the non-renewable ones are depleting day by day. If proper attention is not given to this, the day is not far when we will be left with no more coal and oil.

- Natural resources like land, forest and water provide livelihood to millions.
- Natural resources help in raising food crops and other natural food items
- Export of minerals helps in economic growth of the country.
- Natural resources like forests help to prevent soil erosion.
- Developing countries depend a lot on the natural resources for their economic development.

As the population grows, the demands for all these resources also increase, but it is upto us to utilise it wisely and not waste it or misuse it.
Renewable resources such as water, air, plants, etc are considered to be plenty and one might even think that it will not perish. But the fact is that they might not perish away but will definitely become scarce. The capacity at which these items renew themselves is at a much slower pace than the demand. For example, we might think that there is plenty of water and we might never go short of it, but the truth is that there is only $3 \%$ of fresh water on earth for human need and the rest $97 \%$ is saline water. If water is not utilised properly there will be scarcity of water in the near future.
These natural resources should be consumed to protect our environment. The management of natural resources is called as Natural Resource Management. It is to improve the quality of life of the present and the future generations.
Mother earth rich with its natural resources should be managed and preserved without which the existence of all life forms would be impossible. Healthy atmosphere would keep us healthy and increase our life span.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-222.jpg?height=188&width=318&top_left_y=1057&top_left_x=201)

According to the U.S. Energy Information on Administration's (EIA) International. Energy Outlook (IEO 2019), the global supply of crude oil, other liquid hydrocarbons, and biofuels is expected to meet the world's demand for liquid fuels only through 2050.

## SAVE NATURE

As we read in chapter V, there are three R's of waste management: Reduce, reuse, and recycle. Practicing all three of these activities every day is important for a healthy environment.

3 R's
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-222.jpg?height=278&width=611&top_left_y=1525&top_left_x=694)

## Reduce

Reduce/Reduction: to make something smaller or use less, resulting in a smaller amount of waste. "Source reduction" is reducing waste before you purchase it, or by purchasing products that are not wasteful in their packaging or use. A key part of waste "reduction" is "conservation" - using natural resources wisely, and using less than usual in order to avoid waste.

## Tips to practice reduction:

1. Avoid single-use containers whenever possible.
2. Purchase less products and in materials/packaging that can be readily recycled.
3. Buy concentrates rather than diluted products- the result is less waste for disposal when it is empty.
4. Refuse store bags.
5. Use durable items rather than disposable items whenever possible.

## Reuse

Reuse materials in their original form instead of throwing them away, or pass those materials to others who could use them too.

## Tips for reuse :

Carry washable cups or travel mugs instead of disposables; a lot of restaurants and convenient stores will be glad to fill or refill your own mug. When you do use disposables like plastic cups, plates, utensils, and plastic food storage bags; don't throw them away.

Bicycles or other durable goods like washers, dryers, etc. are being repaired rather than being replaced when they break. This is another form of "reuse". New is not always better, nor it is always necessary. When you do decide to replace something large and "reusable", be sure to donate the old one to charitable outlets. Use cloth gift bags and stop ripping the paper off gifts. If you remove the wrapping paper carefully, you can use it again, and there's nothing wrong with doing just that. And don't forget to use canvas or cloth bags when shopping so you don't need to make the choice between "paper or plastic." Use washable table napkins instead of paper napkins. Cloth napkins are usually much larger and they absorb more than paper products.

## Recycle

Recycling occurs when you save and take reusable materials to places where they can be remade into either the same product or new products, instead of throwing them.
Making new items from recycled ones also takes fewer energy and other resources than making products from brand new materials. Buy toilet paper, paper towels and stationery made from recycled paper, tile from crushed light bulbs, garden hoses from used tires and yard furniture from recycled plastic bottles.

## CONNECTING TOPIC

## Environmental Laws

One of the fundamental rights, according to the Indian constitution is the 'Right to life' which means right to a healthy environment. Various laws have been enacted by the Government of India at different times. Some of the important ones are given below :

- Forest Act, 1972, 1980
- Wildlife Protection Act 1972 (amended in 1991)
- The Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Cess Act, 1977 (amended in 1991)
- The Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Cess Act, 1981 (amended in 1987)
- The Environment Protection Act, 1986
- Prevention of Cruelty of Animals Act, 1960
- The Motor Vehicle Act, 1938 (amended in 1988)


## FORESTS AND WILDLIFE

The biotic component of our environment on land i.e. plants and animals are found in forests. Forests form a buffer for the earth to protect life forms. It comprises an area of diverse and complex ecosystems consisting mainly of trees, shrubs and wild plants which house an extraordinary variety of communities of living organisms. For example, the Indian forest includes a wide variety of plants and animals. India has over 45,000 plant species, 800 species of mammals, 2000 species of birds, 420 species of reptiles, 2000 species of fish, 5000 species of insects and 4000 species of molluscs etc. Due to the wide range of life forms and genetic diversity of life forms, a good forest is therefore called the 'biodiversity hot spots'.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-223.jpg?height=515&width=586&top_left_y=1820&top_left_x=769)

## Forest

In last chapter, we studied the ecological web of life, how all life forms are interconnected and also how the well being of one organism is dependent on the other. One of the main aims of conservation is to try and preserve the inherited biodiversity. A loss of diversity would lead to an ecological imbalance. There is a clear and mutual relation between the forests, rainfall concentration, the amount of water in rivers and conservation of wild animals.

## Role of Forest

Forests play a vital role in boosting the welfare of people and our environment. They produce many valuable things besides providing ecological balance of the land area. Some important products of the forests are :

1. Wood is needed as timber used for making furniture and houses, wooden crates for packing, paper, sports goods, boats, railway sleepers, as fire wood in developing countries, etc.
2. Food and spices like dry fruits (almond, walnut, cashew, etc.) and spices (cloves, cinnamon, cardamon, etc.).
3. Tannins, Gums, Resins and Dyes. Tannins are used in making ink and leather polish, glues are used in making adhesives, resins are used in paints, dyes like hematoxylin are used in staining tissues in anatomy studies.
4. Drugs e.g. quinine, aconite, belladona, etc.
5. Other products like rubber, cork, honey, camphor, etc.

## Stakeholders

Modern approaches in forest conservation have found that enforcement of forest laws or policies which do not recognise the legitimate needs of different groups that depend on the forest, often fail in their objectives when we consider the conservation of forests. We need to look at different stakeholders whose dependency on forest resources vary.

1. Local communities that stay next to or in the forests Tribals staying within the forest areas and villages bordering them need the five basic ' $\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{s}}$ ' i.e., Food, Fodder, Fibre, Fuel and Fertilizer for surviving. This is obtained from the forest.
2. The forest department of the Government which owns the land undertakes protection and controls the resources from forests. They earn revenue from the sale of timber to construction and furniture markets. They also earn revenue from commercial mono-plantations grown from trees like popular, eucalyptus, teak, bamboo etc. to access the specific products for industries such as paper, plyboard, etc.
3. Industries that use various forest produce, but are not dependent on the forest produce in any one area. These include timber, paper, resin and gum, lac, natural oils, minor food products, medicines, sports equipment industries. They are neither interested in loss of valuable biodiversity nor in the sustainability of forests in particular.
4. Environment Conservationists and Nature Enthusiasts who are not in any way dependent on the forests. NGOs like the World Wide Fund for Nature (WWF), Centre for Science and Environment (CSE), International Union for Conservation of Nature and Natural Resources (IUCN) often exercise a considerable say in policy and management of forests. Historically, conservationists took up causes of protecting large animals like tigers (Project Tiger), lions, elephants and rhinoceros. They now recognise the need to preserve biodiversity as a whole, regulate industrial over-exploitation of forest resources and involve local people in the conservation of forests. Along with the Ministry of Environment and Forests, they have designated protected forest areas, wildlife sanctuaries, biodiversity hotspots for special attention and management.

## SUSTAINABLE MANAGEMENT

India has about $63.5 \%$ million hectares of forest which forms $20 \%$ of the geographical area of the country. For a healthy environment, $33 \%$ of land should be under forest cover. It is extremely difficult for the forest department to safeguard the forest with its flora and fauna along with sustainable harvest of forest produce, solely by its own efforts. There have been enough instances of local communities working traditionally for conservation of forests with trees. Magsaysay Award recipient Sunderlal Bahuguna gave momentum to the 'Chipko Andolan' in Tehri Gharwal district in Uttrakhand. This was a movement started by the villagers especially the women at Reni village who tried to stop the commercial wood contractors from cutting the tree by embracing the trunk of the trees (Chipko). Earlier in 1731, the Bishnoi community in Rajasthan for whom conservation of forest and wildlife is a religious duty, sacrificed 363 lives along with Amrita Devi Bishnoi for the protection of Khejri trees in Khejrali village near Jodhpur in Rajasthan. The Government of India has recently instituted an 'Amrita Devi Bishnoi National Award for Wildlife
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-224.jpg?height=396&width=259&top_left_y=1612&top_left_x=1553)
Conservation' in her memory.
While the government and conservation enthusiasts have a major role to play in protection and conservation

## Chipko Andolan

of forest, the acceptance of locals who live in harmony with natural resources is vital for forest conservation measures. This has been examplified by the West Bengal Forest Department who recognised their failure in reviving the Sal forests in the south western district of the state in 1972. Traditional method of surveillance and policing had led to a complete alienation of the people from the administration resulting in frequent clashes between forest officials and villagers. This was also a major factor in fuelling the militant peasant movements led by the Naxalites. The department was forced to change its strategy and made a beginning of a farsighted forest officer A.K. Banerjee. In the Arabari forest range of Midnapore district, he involved the villagers in the protection of 1,272 hectares of badly degraded Sal forest. In return, for help in protection, villagers were allowed fuel wood and fodder collection on payment of a nominal fee and given employment in both silviculture and harvesting operations. Also 25 percent of the final harvest was given to the village community. The result was an active and willing participation of the local community. The Sal forest of Arabari underwent a remarkable recovery by 1983-a previously worthless forest was valued ₹ 12.5 crores. Such instances have now been repeated and clearly pointed to a model in which decentralized economic growth and ecological conservation go hand-in-hand.

## Conservation of Forest

Conservation is protection, augmentation and scientific mangement of resources so as to maintain them at their optimum level provide benefit to the present as well as future generations. Forests must be conserved because of their immense economic, protective and regulative properties. It is carried out by the following methods.

1. Afforestation. Growing of forests on uprotected barren lands. Van Mahotsava is a tree plantation movement carried out twice a year (February and July) by both government and voluntary agencies.
2. Reforestation. Developing forest cover in the area which has been damaged or cleared during exploitation.
3. Separation of Commercial Forestry. Useful plants required by industry should be planted separately preferably on waste land. Growing industry required plants is called production plantation.
4. Grazing. Grazing should be regulated according to the availability of pasturage.
5. Sustained Yield Block Cutting. A forest is divided into a number of blocks depending upon the period required by forest trees to mature. In one year, trees of one block are felled. The block is reforested immediately. In this way, annual deforestation is compensated by annual reforestation. The forest is conserved indefinitely and provides sustained yield.
6. Social Forestry. It is growing of useful multipurpose trees and shrubs on common village lands, roadsides, railsides, etc, by cooperative efforts of people of the area. The plants provide fodder, small timber and fire wood to villagers.
7. Agroforestry. Multipurpose trees and shrubs are planted on the sides of crop fields to provide fodder, fuel and timber.

NOTE: Both social forestry and agroforestry reduce pressure on the forests. Trees are also planted at all vacant places and along roads in urban areas (urban forestry) for reducing air and noise pllution.

## CONNECTING TOPIC

## Red Data Book

The International Union for the Conservation of Nature and Natural Resources (IUCN) maintains an international list, published as the Red Data Book. The aim of the list is to impart information to the public and policy makers about the urgency of conservation problems. The list recognises 8 Red list categories of species.
(i) Data deficient (DD) - appropriate data on abundance and distribution of the species is unavailable.
(ii) Not evaluated (NE) - species not evaluated against the criteria of IUCN
(iii) Low risk/Least concern (LC) - species is characterized by IUCH but not being a focus of species conservation.
(iv) Vulnerable (VU) - species susceptible to become endangered unless the circumstances affecting its reproduction and survival improve.
(v) Endangered (EN) - species is at risk of becoming extinct, as it is few in number and threatened by various parameters like predation, deforestation etc.
(vi) Critically Endangered (CR) - species that face a high risk of extinction in the wild.
(vii) Extinct in the wild (EW) - species that are not known to have any living specimens in the wild and are only zoos or artificial environments.
(viii) Extinct (EX) - when the last existing member of the species dies.

The 2000 Red list contains assessment of more than 18,000 species, 11,000 of which are threatened.
According to the Red list, in India
44 plant species - Critically endangered e.g. Berberies nilghirensis
113 plant species - Endangered e.g. Bentinckiet nicobarica
87 plant species - Vulunerable e.g. Cupressus cashmeriana
18 animal species - Critically endangered e.g. Pigmy hog
54 animal species - Endangered e.g. Red Panda
143 animal species - Vuunerable e.g. Black buck
The list also provides information to international agreements such as the Convention on Biological diversity and the convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora.

## Biodiversity Conservation

Earth's rich biodiversity is vital for the very survival of mankind. Biodiversity conservation is mainly aimed at protection, enhancement and scientific management of biodiversity.
There are two basic strategies of biodiversity conservation -
(i) In-situ conservation: The conservation of plants/animals in their natural habitats. National parks, wildlife sanctuaries and biosphere reserves are the examples of protected areas.
(ii) Ex-situ conservation: The conservation of plants/animals outside their natural habitats. It includes offsite collections (botanical gardens, zoos, arboreta etc.) and gene banks (preserves genetic material like freezing cuttings from plants or seeds, freezing of sperm and eggs etc.).

The famous bird dodo (Raphus cucullatus) that primarily lived in the island nation of Mauritius was hunted to extinction, known as human driven extinction. This bird was popularized by its appearance in Lewis Carroll's book "Alice's Adventures in Wonderland." Dodos were first mentioned by Dutch sailors in the late 16th century. They ate the beast to extinction after finding that the bird was incredibly easy to catch due to the fact it had no fear of humans.

## WATER FOR ALL

Water is an essential component of all forms of life. After air, it is also the most abundant substance present in the earth's biosphere. Oceans, seas, and other saline inland water bodies make $97.3 \%$ of the total global supply of water which is not available for human consumption due to high salt content. Fresh water accounts for only $2.7 \%$ of the total estimated global water supply. Of this percentage, $75 \%$ of the fresh water is frozen in polar ice caps and glaciers. Some fresh water is also present underground. The fraction of fresh water available for human beings is less than $0.003 \%$ of the total global water available.
There are three main sources of fresh water available. The first is rainwater resources of India. India receives an overall rainfall of about 105 to 117 cm annually resulting in 3 million $\mathrm{m}^{3}$ of water. Most of this water is collected in few months during monsoon. The second source is surface water, there are 14 major river systems which include Ganga, Brahmaputra, Godavari, Krishna, Kaveri, etc. Other surface water sources include ponds, lakes and streams. The third source is ground water which is the water that percolates down the surface soil into pore spaces of rocks. The total volume of ground water found in the aquifer is estimated to be $42.3 \times 10^{10} \mathrm{~m}^{3}$. Ground water provides soil moisture for plants and supplements for streams and lakes. Water table indicates the level at which ground water is found. The agricultural sector and urban sectors use this water for various proposes which has resulted a decline in its level.

Study the rainfall pattern in India and identify the regions where water is abudant and where it is scarce.
Rainfall pattern in India is -
Scanty rainfall - parts of Rajasthan, Gujarat, Punjab and Haryana
Moderate rainfall - Indo-Gangetic plains and Southeastern parts of the country.
Heavy rainfall - Kerala, North eastern states and Western Ghats.
Regions in India with water scarcity - Rajasthan, Gujarat, Maharashtra, Uttar Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, Chattisgarh, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu.

In India, most of the water supply and regeneration is dependant on the monsoon rains. There are some worrying facts about the water situation. The effects of world climate change are being felt all over the planet. For a country like India, where agricultural activities are still dependent on regular monsoon rainfall, it is a cause of worry for adequate and reliable water supply. Erratic rainfall like heavy rains in areas receiving minimal rainfall (Rajasthan floods of 2006 in Barmer), scanty rainfall in areas receiving good rainfall (Karnataka and A.P. drought of 2006), melting of glaciers are causing problems in surface water supply, ground water recharge and agriculture.
Major diversion of water from the rivers has resulted in many downstream areas having reduced ground water recharge. This includes urban areas which have huge requirements of water and depend on dams and reservoir supply. Cities have an additional problem of clearing vegetation from land to build houses, roads, etc. Large concreting of surface areas does not allow water to seep into the ground to be stored in aquifiers leading to water shortages. Because of this, there is water run off or water remains on the surface which increases the surface run off and floods in cities causing heavy damage.

## Measurement of Water Pollution

The contamination of river water usually occurrs due to the presence of coliform bacteria. Coliform is a group of bacteria found in human intestines. The presence of coliform in the river water indicates its contamination by disease-causing micro-organisms. Although coliform itself is harmless, but its presence in river water indicates that other harmful intestinal bacteria might also be present. The pH of river water is measured by using universal indicator paper. If the pH of river water is below 7 , then the river water will be acidic and hence polluted.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-226.jpg?height=159&width=419&top_left_y=1256&top_left_x=201)

## Ganga Action Plan

A multicrore 'Ganga Action Plan' project was launched in 1985 to clean the river Ganga and make pollution free.
Ganga Action Plan (GAP) was formulated to reduce pollution load of river Ganga by more than $75 \%$. The plan has been carried out in two phases since 1985. The water quality has been tested from time to time by checking coliform (group of harmless bacteria in human intestine) number/ 100 ml . The important aspect of GAP has been (i) Diversion of sewers away from river (ii) Treatment of sewage and changing it into an energy source (iii) Construction of community management system (vi) Enforcement of setting up of effluent treatment plants by the industry. (iii) Development of Ghats and interceptions.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-227.jpg?height=387&width=524&top_left_y=374&top_left_x=1347)
Fig.: Varanasi (the holiest place on the Ganga River)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-227.jpg?height=95&width=161&top_left_y=817&top_left_x=251)

Did You
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-227.jpg?height=199&width=120&top_left_y=829&top_left_x=456)

Till 2014, over ₹ 4,000 crore had been spent to clean Ganga, but the river has remained dirty. The National Democratic Alliance (NDA) government had launched the Namami Gange in mid-May 2015. It was being implemented by the National Mission for Clean Ganga (NMCG), and its state counterparts-State Programme Management Groups. The National Ganga Council (NGC) was later created and to give it utmost importance, the Prime Minister was made the head of it. It was the biggest-ever initiative. Around ₹ 20,000 crore was allotted and a deadline was set: "Ganga will be clean by 2019" which has now been extended to 2020.

## Learn More

Water pollution is a serious health hazard in India. The principal sources of water pollution and water pollutants are domestic sewage and industrial effluents. The domestic sewage contains human faeces, urine, kitchen/cloth washings, organic wastes, industrial wastes, etc. Sewage provides food for decomposers, so the population of microorganisms increases.
The microorganisms/decomposers cause decomposition of sewage and takes up most of the oxygen dissolved in water. So, the BOD (Biological or Biochemical oxygen demand) of water increases. BOD of pure drinking water is less than 1ppm (parts per million) or $\mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{l}$. A weak organic waste will have a BOD below $1500 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{l}$, medium organic waste between 1500-4000 $\mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{l}$, while it is above $4000 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{l}$ in strong organic waste. The degree of water pollution is directly proportional to BOD.COD (Chemical oxygen demand) is the amount of oxygen required to oxidize all pollutant materials in one litre of water at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in five days. The value of COD is much higher than BOD.
In sewage, phosphorus and nitrogen compounds are present which are necessary for the growth of algae. In polluted water, these accumulate and result into excessive growth of algae on water surface. Excessive growth of algae is called water bloom. Presence of extra nutrients brings about dense growth of plankton algae (termed as eutrophication) which increases organic loading and brings about reduction in the oxygen content of water causing death of aquatic animals.
Industrial effluents (or industrial discharge) contain heavy metals (mercury, lead, copper, arsenic, and calcium), inorganic pollutants (acids, alkalies and bleaching liquors) and organic pollutants (phenol, naptha, proteins, aromatic compounds, cellulose fibres, etc.). The non biodegradable pollutants like DDT, pesticides, phenolic compounds etc. are not decomposed by microorganisms. These get accumulated in tissue in increasing concentration along the food chain and the phenomenon is called biological magnification.

## Dams

Dams are large water-storing bodies across the river to regulate the flow of water. Many dams have been constructed across the major rivers which serve as storehouse of river water. The stored water is allowed to flow dowstream in order to generate electricity and carry water to long distances for the purpose of irrigation. Some examples of famous dams and canals in India are :

1. Bhakra Dam built on the river Sutlej in Punjab.
2. Sardar Sarovar Dam built on river Narmada in 1940 in Gujarat.
3. Tehri Dam on river Ganga in Tehri, Uttranchal (highest dam).
4. Tawa Dam on the Tawa river in Hoshangabad (MP).
5. Mettur Dam in Kaveri river in Tamil Nadu (oldest dam).

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-227.jpg?height=410&width=545&top_left_y=2067&top_left_x=1306)
Fig. Tehri Dam : The highest dam in India

## Benefits of Dams

(i) Canal system originating from the dams can transfer large amounts of water to great distances which helps to raise agricultural production in distant places. For example, Indira Gandhi Canal originating from Bhakra dam has brought greenery in deserts of Rajasthan.
(ii) Dams ensure continuous water supply in the surrounding areas.
(iii) Flowing water from a height is used for generating electricity.

## Problems associated with construction of Dams

(i) Construction of dams creates displacement of a large number of peasants and tribals without adequate compensation or rehabilitation. These social problems result in protests like Narmada Bachao Andolan. The people who have been displaced by the construction of Tawa Dam in 1970s are still fighting for the benefits they were promised. The other social problems are created by the mismanagement of the officials. It has been observed that people close to the source of water grow water intensive crops like sugarcane and rice whereas people further downstream do not get sufficient water to grow these crops. This causes social problems due to the unequal distribution of water.
(ii) Construction of dam is an expensive project. Some people think that such expenditure does not generate the proportionate benefits. Others believe that no progress can happen without constructing dams as it is the most efficient way to use water resources.
(iii) Construction of dams result in deforestation and loss of biodiversity.

## Water Management

In our country, there is no shortage of water, but proper management is lacking. Some of the methods for managing and conserving water resources are as follows :
(i) Rain water harvesting : The government and various organisations have been trying to find an alternative of dams. Several states in the country have been using the age-old practices of water harvesting which are still in use today and are as listed below:

| S.No. | State | Water Harvesting Practices |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. | Rajasthan | Khadins, tanks and Nadis |
| 2. | Maharashtra | Bandharas and Tals |
| 3. | Madhya Pradesh and Uttar Pradesh | Bundhis |
| 4. | Bihar | Ahars and Pynes |
| 5. | Himachal Pradesh | Kulhs |
| 6. | Jammu and Kashmir | Ponds |
| 7. | Tamil Nadu | Eris |
| 8. | Kerala | Surangams |
| 9. | Karnataka | Kattas |

Khadins in Rajasthan consist of a long (100-300 m long) earthen embankment built across the lower edge of the sloping farmland. The area enclosed by the embankment is called the 'bund' which collects huge amount of rain water that flows down the slopes. The overflowing water is carried through the pathways which fills the shallow well dug behind the bund.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-228.jpg?height=367&width=1234&top_left_y=1852&top_left_x=388)
Fig. The Khadin System in Rajasthan

Kulhs was a canal irrigation system developed in Himachal Pradesh about 400 years ago. Stream waters were channelled into the downhill villages. Villages had made a common agreement about water use. These kulhs were under looked by few people who were being paid by the villagers. The water from these kulhs was used for irrigation as well as percolated into the soil to feed springs. Ever since these kulhs were taken over by the irrigation department, they became defunct.
In the present day, rain water harvesting comprises of water storage and ground water recharging. The rain water collected from the roof top of a house or a building is then filtered and stored in proper tanks. This stored water is either used for human consumption, irrigation or can be channelised through a drain pipe into a well to raise the water table.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-229.jpg?height=456&width=552&top_left_y=376&top_left_x=785)
Fig.: Modern method of rain water harvesting

(ii) Treatment of waste water : Waste water usually contains organic and inorganic impurities which must be removed by primary and secondary treatments. This water can then be used for industrial and irrigational purposes. The benefits of using treated waste water are :
(a) Conservation of water resources.
(b) Increases crop yield by conserving nutrients and reducing the need for artificial fertilizers.
(c) Reduces pollution of surface water resources.
(d) Low cost method for sanitary disposal of municipal waste water.
(iii) Storage of water : Rain water should be stored in huge tanks for use in agriculture. Dams and reservoirs also store water which can be supplied to canals and rivers throughout the year.

CASE - I : In which state, Kulhs is used and why did it become defunct?
KulhsisacanalirrigationsystempracticedinhillyareasofHimachalPradesh.Earlieritwasmanagedbythepeoplein villages,but later when it was taken over by the irrigation department,it became defunct.

CASE - II : Ahar pyne irrigation system is practiced in which part of the country and why it still exists?
AharpyneisatraditionalirrigationsystemandispracticedinSouthplainsofBiharforcenturies.ItsavestheplainsofSouth Bihar from recurrent floods and utilizes water; hence it is still in use.

CASE - III : In which areas, Khadin is used for water harvesting and why?
It is used in Western Rajasthan to retain monsoon water and soak plots to raise crops.

CASE - IV : Why is Khadin better than dams?
In comparison to Khadin, a lot of space is required to build dams and they can also cause floods.

Q2. The neglected use of Kuhls has affected the crop pattern in Himachal Pradesh. Explain with an example?

## COAL AND PETROLEUM

Fossil fuels consist of deposits of dead organisms. The organic matter takes centuries to form. Fossil fuels principally consist of carbon and hydrogen bonds. There are three types of fossil fuels which can all be used for energy production; coal, oil and natural gas. They are a non-renewable source of energy formed from plants and animals that lived up to 300 million years ago and are found in deposits beneath the earth. The fuels are burnt to release the chemical energy that is essential to modern society. Over $85 \%$ of our energy demands are met by the combustion of fossil fuels.

## Coal

Coal is a hard, black colored rock-like substance. It is made up of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen and varying amounts of sulphur. There are three main types of coal - Anthracite, Bituminous and Lignite. Anthracite coal is the hardest and has

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-230.jpg?height=477&width=794&top_left_y=472&top_left_x=1018)
Fig.: Fossil fuels-synthesis and combustion

more carbon, which gives it a higher energy content. Lignite is softest and is low in carbon but high in hydrogen and oxygen content. Bituminous is in between. Today, the precursor to coal peat - is still found in many countries and is also used as an energy source.

## Petroleum

(comes from the Latin word petra, "rock," and oleum, "oil.") :
Oil is a liquid fossil fuel that is formed from the remains of marine microorganisms deposited on the sea floor. After millions of years, these deposits end up in rock and sediment where oil is trapped in small spaces. It can be extracted by large drilling platforms. Oil is the most widely used fossil fuel. Crude oil consists of many different organic compounds which are transformed to products in a refining process. It is applied in cars, jets, roads, roofs etc. Oil cannot be found everywhere on earth. Some scientists say that tiny diatoms are the source of oil. Diatoms are sea creatures of the size of a pin head. They do one thing just like plants; they can convert sunlight directly into energy. The various steps in this process are-
(1) Diatoms died and fell on the sea floor, (2) Here, they were buried under sediment and other rock, (3) The rock squeezed the diatoms and carbon eventually turned into oil under great pressure and heat. As the earth changed, moved and folded, pockets where oil and natural gas can be found, were formed.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-230.jpg?height=444&width=800&top_left_y=1596&top_left_x=603)

Oil has been used for more than 5,000-6,000 years. We are currently in an energy crisis. Fossil fuels are the lifeblood of our modern society but our supply has a finite end. Fossil fuels take millions of years to make. We are using up the fuels that were made more than 300 million years ago before the time of the dinosaurs. Once they are gone it is almost impossible to get them back once again. So, it's best not to waste fossil fuels. They are not renewable; they can't really be made again. We can save fossil fuels by conserving energy.

## Pollution caused by Burning Coal and Petroleum based Fuels

When coal and petroleum based fuels are burnt, the products are carbon dioxide, water, sulphur dioxide and nitrogen oxides. If incomplete combustion takes place in an insufficient supply of oxygen then carbon monoxide is also produced . Of all the products of combustion, only water is harmless while others pollute the environment.

## Few examples are-

(i) Sulphur dioxide causes respiratory disorders. It dissolves in rain water making it acidic and causing damage to plant life, buildings, monuments and metal structures.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-230.jpg?height=90&width=100&top_left_y=2549&top_left_x=1822)
(ii) Nitrogen oxide also has similar effects like sulphur dioxide.
(iii) Carbon dioxide has green house effect and leads to global warming.
(iv) Carbon monoxide is a poisonous gas which gets into our blood stream, binds with RBCs and causes suffocation. Excess carbon monoxide can also cause death.

## Methods of conservation of fossil fuels

(i) Burning coal causes pollution. Coal can be converted to liquid fuel and Compressed Natural Gas (CNG) through coal gasification.
(ii) Wastage during extraction and transportation of fossil fuel should be avoided by using modern technology.
(iii) Oil wells and coal mines should be protected from fire to avoid wastage, pollution and loss of life or property.
(iv) Alternative sources of energy such as hydroelectricity, nuclear, solar, wind power and biogas plants should be used.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-231.jpg?height=159&width=424&top_left_y=794&top_left_x=255)

1. Calculate the amount of petrol or diesel used by the vehicle/s at your home in a month.
2. Observe the number of vehicles in your area and calculate how much of fuel is used by the people in your community.
3. Get an estimate of fuel consumption in your community.
4. Can you think of ways to reduce it?

## Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs):

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has four choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONLY ONE is correct.

1. Which gas is responsible for the global warming?
(a) $\mathrm{O}_{2}$
(b) $\mathrm{N}_{2}$
(c) $\mathrm{H}_{2}$
(d) $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$

(a) there is lack of proper care.
(b) mass scale hunting for foreign trade.
(c) its natural habitat is destroyed.
(d) natural calamity occur.
3. Which one of the following is a non-replenishable resource?
(a) Minerals
(b) Forests
(c) Mineral fuel
(d) Hydroelectricity
4. Maximum air which we breathe is present at
(a) Troposphere
(b) Stratosphere
(c) Ionosphere
(d) Mesosphere
5. Domestic cooking gas cylinder is filled with
(a) Alcohol
(b) Diesel oil
(c) Liquid petroleum gas
(d) Coal gas
6. 'Social forestry' aims at
(a) growing different types of plantations together.
(b) growing one type of trees on the land.
(c) management of forests by village bodies.
(d) management of forests by cooperative societies.
7. Wildlife conservation means the protection and preservation of:
(a) Ferocious wild animals only
(b) Wild plants only
(c) Non-cultivated plants and non-domesticated animals
(d) All the above living in natural habitat
8. If the Bengal tiger becomes extinct
(a) Hyenas and wolves will become scarce
(b) The wild area will be safe for man and domestic animals
(c) Its gene pool will be lost forever
(d) The population of beautiful animals like deers will be stabilized
9. Which one of the following resources is least developed due to economic factors as well as inadequate knowedge?
(a) Ocean resource
(b) Mineral resource
(c) Forest resource
(d) Soil resource
10. Chipko Andolan (Movement) which was started in 1980 in Garhwal / Himalayas (Gopeshwar) near Alkananda river was for the first time initiated by
(a) Chandar Prasad Bhatt
(b) Sunder Lal Bahuguna
(c) Baba Amte
(d) Vinoba Bhave
11. The problems for criticism about large dams are that they
(a) displace large number of peasants and tribes without proper rehabilitation.
(b) swallow up huge amounts of public money without the generation of proportionate benefits.
(c) contribute enormously to deforestation and the loss of biological diversity.
(d) all of the above.

## Assertion \& Reason:

DIRECTIONS : Each of these questions contains an assertion followed by reason. Read them carefully and answer the question on the basis of following options. You have to select the one that best describes the two statements.
(a) If both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
(b) If both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
(c) If Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
(d) If Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

1. Assertion: Conservation of biological diversity under natural conditions is ex-situ conservation.
Reason: Increase of Manipur deer from 17 animals to 150 in Calcutta and Delhi zoos is one of an example of these.
2. Assertion: Every biological system resist a change and wants to remain in state of equilibrium.
Reason: Climax communities of an ecosystem are produced after several changes it has gone through succession.
3. Assertion: Regions in Gangetic plains are very fertile.

Reason: It has mainly alluvial soils.
4. Assertion: Cultural eutrophication is nutrient enrichment of water bodies due to human activites like passage of sewage, industrial effluents etc.
Reason: The prime contaminants from sewage and industrial effluents are nitrates and phosphates, which act as plant nutrients and overstimulate the growth of algae.
5. Assertion: An estimation of BOD gives an indication of water pollution.
Reason: It is measure of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ requirement of bacteria living in that media.

## True / False:

DIRECTIONS : Read the following statements and write your answer as true or false.

1. The fossil fuels, coal and petroleum, will ultimately be exhausted.
2. The wildlife resources are renewable resources.
3. Combustion of fossil fuels pollutes our environment.
4. An environmentally friendly decision is reuse jam and pickle bottles.
5. Geo-thermal energy is a conventional source of energy.
6. Improper use of land converts fertile land into unusable land.
7. Check dams are built along seasonal flooded gullies for water harvesting.
8. Coal and petroleum are non-renewable inexhaustible resource.
9. An important protective function of forests is reduction of atmospheric pollution.
10. Forest department has been able to maintain biodiversity by growing Pine, Teak and Eucalyptus on large tracts.
11. Use of sewage, domestic waste, farm yard manure, etc. will decrease the fertility of the degraded lands.
12. The most rapidly dwindling natural resource in the world is forests.
13. A natural resource is a substance/commodity that is a gift of nature which is very useful to mankind.
14. Biodiversity means variations present in the species of the flora of an area.
15. Solid waste serve as useful resource for providing energy for our industries.
16. Khadins, Ahars and Kattas are ancient structures that are example of water harvesting.
17. A successful forest conservation strategy should involve protection of only consumers.
18. Sustainable development does not consider the view points of stakeholders.

## Passage Based Questions:

## DIRECTIONS : Study the given paragraph ( $s$ ) and answer the following questions.

1. In a village, farmers started cultivating crops all around a lake which was always filled with water. They added lot of materials W to the soil in their fields to increase the yield of crops. Soon they found that the whole surface of water in the lake was covered with a green layer made up of tiny organisms X . After some time, the fish present in the lake started dying in large numbers because they could not get sufficient Y due to a process Z which had occurred in lake water and drained out all the Y present in the water of lake.
(a) What do you think materials W are?
(b) Name (i) X, and (ii) Y.
(c) What is the process Z known as?
(d) What happens during the process Z which uses up all the Y present in lake water?
(e) What does the example tell us about the use of materials like W?
2. A person buys two electrical devices P and Q for lighting purposes in his house. The device P consumes only 5 units of electricity in a month but device Q consumes 15 units of electricity in a month when used for the same number of hours daily. The device Q wastes a lot of electricity by radiating energy C but P does not do so.
(a) What type of device is (i) P, and (ii) Q?
(b) Name one component which is present in Q but not in P .
(c) Name the energy C.
(d) Which device is more energy efficient : P or Q?
(e) Which fossil fuel is most likely to be conserved if all of us switch over to devices like P?

## Very Short Answer Questions:

## DIRECTIONS: Give answer in one word or one sentence.

1. What is meant by biodiversity of an area ?
2. The major ill effect of mono culture practice in forests is on the
(a) biodiversity which faces large destruction
(b) local people whose basic needs can no longer be met from such forests
(c) industries
(d) forest department
3. Expand the term UNEP and IPCC.
4. Name a non-renewable resource and a renewable resource.
5. Name the plant leaves of which are used in making Bidis.
6. Name two gases other than $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ produced by burning of coal and petroleum .
7. Presence of which bacteria indicates contamination of the Ganga water?
8. Give two advantages of a dam.
9. How coal and petroleum were formed ?
10. What is the composition of coal and petroleum ?
11. Which resources are also known as fossil fuels ?
12. Why was Ganga Action Plan launched ?
13. Water is a valuable resource. List two ways, that you would suggest every family member to save this resource.
[2015]
14. Why is biogas considered an excellent fuel?
[2019]
15. Answer question numbers 3(a) to 3(d) on the basis of your understanding of the following paragraph and the related studies concepts.
The Tehri dam is the highest dam in India and one of the highest in the World. The Tehri dam withholds a reservoir of capacity $4.0 \mathrm{~km}^{3}$ and surface area $52 \mathrm{~km}^{2}$. It is used for irrigation, municipal water supply and the generation of 1000 MW of hydro electricity.
The Tehri Dam has been the object of protests. Environment activist Shri Sunder Lal Bahuguna led the "Anti Tehri Dam Movement" from 1980s to 2014. The protest was against the displacement of town inhabitants and environmental consequences of the weak ecosystem. The relocation of more than $1,00,000$ people from the area has led to protracted legal battles over resettlement rights and ultimately resulted in the delayed completion of the project.
(a) How is hydropower harnessed?
(b) Define 1 MW .
(c) Mention two disadvantages of constructing Tehri Dam.
(d) What happens when water from great heights is made to fall on blades of turbine?
[2020]

## Short Answer Questions:

## DIRECTIONS : Give answer in 2-3 sentences.

1. How is water pollution measured ?
2. Why is segregation of waste materials necessary ?
3. What are the disadvantages of using fossil-fuel ?
4. Suggest some approaches towards the conservation of forest.
5. Mention two advantages of watershed management.
6. Why do we need to use our resources carefully ?
7. How burning of fossil fuel is effecting our environment?
8. Who are stakeholders in respect of forests?

Which one of these cause maximum damage to forest and how?
9. What are the three problems which crop up due to building of large dams?
10. In which way ground water is more advantageous as compared to surface water ?
11. What harm has been caused to alpine meadows since nomadic shepherds were stopped from grazing their cattles in such meadows?
12. "Forests have productive, protective and regulative function". Explain.
13. Explain, with an example, 'people's participation' in the management of forests.
14. Why are forest considered "biodiversity hot spots?" List two ways in which an individual can contribute effectively to the management of forests and wildlife?
[2015]
15. What damage is caused to forests by the following:
[2015]
(i) Building rest houses for tourist in national parks
(ii) Grazing domestic animals in national parks.
16. "Reuse is better than recycling of materials". Give reasons to justify this statement.
[2016]
17. Management of forest and wild life resources is very challenging task. Why ? Give any two reasons.
[2017]
18. Explain two main advantages associated with water harvesting at the community level.
[2017]
19. (a) Water is an elixir of life, a very important natural resource. Your Science teacher wants you to prepare a plan for a formative assessment activity, "how to save water, the vital natural resource". Write any two ways that you will suggest to bring awareness in your neighborhood, on 'how to save water'.
(b) Name and explain any one way by which the underground, on 'how to save water'.
[2017]
20. What is a dam? Why do we seek to build large dams? While building large dams, which three main problem should particularly be addressed to maintain peace among local people? Mention them.
[2018]
21. What is water harvesting? List two main advantages associated with water harvesting at the community level. Write two causes for the failure of sustained availability of groundwater.
[2019]
22. Explain the role of forests in conserving the environment. How do the forests get depleted ? What are its consequences?
[2019]

## Long Answer Questions:

DIRECTIONS : Give answer in four to five sentences.

1. What is the damage caused to the environment on exploitation of natural resources?
2. What are the concepts behind management of resources like coal, petroleum etc.?
3. What is meant by sustainable management ? The environmentalists are insisting upon "sustainable natural resources management?" State its four advantages.
4. Eco-club of your school is organising a debate on the topic 'Nature's Fury unleashed by Human's Greed' (Uttrakhand Disaster).
(i) List three arguments that you will use to convince the audience that humans are responsible for this disaster.
(ii) List any three values that will be inculcated with this debate.
[2015]

## Reasoning Based Questions:

1. Give any one reason why there is an energy crisis.
2. Is water conservation necessary ? Give reasons.
3. Why do we need to manage our natural resources?
4. Why is the process of 'reuse' better than that of 'recycling'.
5. Explain, why, despite good rains, we are not able to meet the demand for water of all the people in our country.
6. Although coal and petroleum are produced by the degradation of biomass, even then we need to conserve them. Why?
7. Give two reasons why the construction of the Sardar Sarovar Project on the river Narmada and the Tehri Dam on the river Ganga faced opposition.

## HOTS Questions:

1. Quote three instances where human intervention saved the forests from destruction.
2. Why is replenishment of forests necessary? State four reasons.
3. Although coal and petroleum are produced by degradation of biomass, yet we need to conserve them. Why ?

## Exercise 2 <br> Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar)

## Text - Book Questions:

1. What changes can you make in your habits to become more environment friendly?
2. What would be the advantages of exploiting resources with short-term aims?
3. How would these advantages differ from the advantages of using a long term perspective in managing our resources?
4. Why do you think there should be equitable distribution of resources? What forces would be working against an equitable distribution of our resources?
5. Why should we conserve forests and wildlife?
6. Suggest some approaches towards the conservation of forests.
7. Find out about the traditional systems of water harvesting/management in your region.
8. Compare the above system with the probable systems in hilly/mountainous area or plains or plateau regions.
9. Find out the source of water in your region/locality. Is water from this source available to all people living in that area?

## Text - Book Exercise:

1. What changes would you suggest in your home in order to be environment friendly?
2. We saw in this chapter that there are four main stakeholders when it comes to forests and wildlife. Which among these should have the authority to decide the management of forest produce? Why do you think so?
3. How can you as an individual contribute or make a difference to the management of (a) forests and wildlife (b) water resources
(c) coal and petroleum?
4. List five things you have done over the last one week to -
(a) conserve our natural resources
(b) increase the pressure on our natural resources.
5. On the basis of the issues raised in this chapter, what changes would you incorporate in your life-style in a move towards a sustainable use of our resources?

## Exemplar Questions:

1. Given below are a few statements related to biodiversity. Pick those that correctly describe the concept of biodiversity.
(i) Biodiversity refers to the different species of flora and fauna present in an area.
(ii) Biodiversity refers to only the flora of a given area.
(iii) Biodiversity is greater in a forest.
(iv) Biodiversity refers to the total number of individuals of a particular species living in an area.
(a) (i) and (ii)
(b) (ii) and (iv)
(c) (i) and (iii)
(d) (ii) and (iii)
2. Among the statements given below select the ones that correctly describe the concept of sustainable development:
(i) Planned growth with minimum damage to the environment.
(ii) Growth irrespective of the extent of damage caused to the environment.
(iii) Stopping all developmental work to conserve the environment.
(iv) Growth that is acceptable to all the stakeholders.
(a) (i) and (iv)
(b) (ii) and (iii)
(c) (ii) and (iv)
(d) (iii) only
3. In our country, there are attempts to increase the height of several existing dams like Tehri and Almati, dams across Narmada. Choose the correct statements among the following that are a consequence of raising the height of dams
(i) Terrestrial flora and fauna of the area is destroyed completely.
(ii) Dislocation of people and domestic animals living in the area.
(iii) Valuable agricultural land may be permanently lost.
(iv) It will generate permanent employment for people.
(a) (i) and (ii)
(b) (i), (ii) and (iii)
(c) (ii) and (iv)
(d) (ii) and (iv)
4. The pH of water sample collected from a river was found to be acidic in the range of $3.5-4.5$, on the banks of the river were several factories that were discharging effluents into the river. The effluents of which one of the
following factories is the most likely cause for lowering the pH of river water?
(a) Soap and detergent factory
(b) Lead battery manufacturing factory
(c) Plastic cup manufacturing factory
(d) Alcohol distillery
5. In our country, vast tracts of forests are cleared and a single species of plant is cultivated. This practice promotes
(a) biodiversity in the area.
(b) monoculture in the area.
(c) growth of natural forest.
(d) preserves the natural ecosystem in the area.
6. Select the incorrect statement?
(a) Economic development is linked to environmental conservation
(b) Sustainable development encourages development for current generation and conservation of resources for future generations
(c) Sustainable development does not consider the view points of stakeholders
(d) Sustainable development is a long planned and persistent development
7. Expand the abbreviation GAP:
(a) Governmental Agency for Pollution Control
(b) Gross Assimilation by Photosynthesis
(c) Ganga Action Plan
(d) Governmental Agency for Animal Protection
8. The important message conveyed by the 'Chipko Movement' is
(a) to involve the community in forest conservation efforts.
(b) to ignore the community in forest conservation efforts.
(c) to cut down forest trees for developmental activities.
(d) government agencies have the unquestionable right to order destruction of trees in forests.
9. Prepare a list of five items that you use daily in the school. Identify from the list such items that can be recycled.
10. Suggest a few measures for controlling carbon dioxide levels in the atmosphere.
11. (a) Locate and name the water reservoirs in Figures (a) and (b).
(b) Which has an advantage over the other and why?

12. Suggest a few useful ways of utilising waste water.
13. Why are the Arabari forests of Bengal known to be a good example of conserved forest?
14. In a village in Karnataka, people started cultivating crops all around a lake which was always filled with water. They added fertilisers to their field in order to enhance the yield. Soon they discovered that the waterbody was completely covered with green floating plants and fishes started dying in large numbers. Analyse the situation and give reasons for excessive growth of plants and death of fish in the lake.

## Exercise 3 Foundation Builder

## Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs):

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has four choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONLY ONE is correct.

1. Which is preserved in National Park?
(a) Flora
(b) Fauna
(c) Both (a) and (b)
(d) None of these
2. New approach of conservation is the establishment of -
(a) Sancturies
(b) Reserve forests
(c) National parks
(d) Biosphere reserves
3. Which one of the following is not a fossil fuel?
(a) uranium
(b) coal
(c) natural gas
(d) petroleum
4. Genetic diversity in agricultural crops is threatened by -
(a) Introduction of high yielding varieties
(b) Intensive use of fertilizers
(c) Entensive intercropping
(d) Intensive use of biopesticides
5. Overuse of resources leads to :
(a) floods and droughts
(b) energy crisis
(c) imbalance in biological diversity
(d) all the above
6. Decrease in species diversity in tropical countries is mainly due to:
(a) Urbanisation
(b) Pollution
(c) Deforestation
(d) Soil erosion
7. Which of the following animal has become almost extinct in India?
(a) Wolf
(b) Rhinoceros
(c) Hippopotamus
(d) Cheetah
8. Which of the following is not done in a wild life sanctuary?
(a) Fauna is conserved
(b) Flora is conserved
(c) Soil and flora is utilised
(d) Hunting is prohibited
9. A renewable source of energy is
(a) Petroleum
(b) Coal
(c) Nuclear fuel
(d) Trees
10. The biosphere utilizes :
(a) geothermal energy
(b) solar energy
(c) tidal energy
(d) atomic energy
11. Plants and animals are known as:
(a) Biotic resources
(b) abiotic resources
(c) Machines
(d) None of these
12. Marked climatic variations take place in the:
(a) troposphere
(b) stratosphere
(c) ionosphere
(d) exosphere
13. Soil erosion can be prevented by
(a) deforestation
(b) afforestation
(c) overgrazing
(d) removal of vegetation
14. Afforestation should be with
(a) exotic species
(b) indigenous species
(c) bamboos
(d) sequoia
15. Forests participate in
(a) controlling pollution
(b) preventation of soil erosion
(c) maintenance of ecological balance
(d) all the above
16. Sal forest is
(a) tropical rain forest
(b) tropical evergreen forest
(c) temperate deciduous
(d) deciduous
17. Which of the following is the correct statement?
(a) Cultural eutrophication is an accelerated form of eutrophication.
(b) In the presence of prime contaminants, such as nitrates and phosphates, the growth of algae is arrested.
(c) The water from electricity generating units enhances the growth of indigenous fauna and flora.
(d) All of the above
18. Chipko Andolan is concerned with
(a) conservation of fossil fuels.
(b) development of new breeds of forest plants.
(c) zoological survey of India.
(d) forest conservation.
19. Which of the following is not a natural resource?
(a) Mango tree
(b) Snake
(c) Wind
(d) Wooden house
20. A successful forest conservation strategy should involve
(a) protection of animals at the highest trophic level
(b) protection of only consumers
(c) protection of only herbivores
(d) comprehensive programme to protect all the physical and biological components
21. Pollution in big cities can be controlled to a large extent by
(i) Improving traffic condition and road.
(ii) Road side plantation
(iii) Proper disposal of garbage and domestic as well as municipal wastes.
Choose the correct statement(s).
(a) (i) and (ii)
(b) (ii) and (iii)
(c) (i) only
(d) All of these
22. Abundance of coliform bacteria in a water body is indicative of pollution from
[NTSE]
(a) petroleum refinery
(b) metal smelter
(c) fertilizer factory
(d) domestic sewage
23. 'Chipko Movement' in the year 1974 in Garhwal Himalayas involved
[KVPY]
(a) protecting tigers
(b) preventing soil erosion by planting trees
(c) preventing pollution by closing down industries
(d) hugging trees to prevent the contractors from felling them
24. From which district of Rajasthan did Chipko movement begin?
[NTSE]
(a) Jodhpur
(b) Jaipur
(c) Ajmer
(d) Jaisalmer

## Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs):

DIRECTIONS: This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has four choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONLY ONE is correct.

1. Which of the following is the most important cause for animals and plants being driven to extinction?
(a) Habitat loss and fragmentation
(b) Drought and floods
(c) Economic exploitation
(d) Alien species invasion
2. The concept of 'Biosphere Reserve' was evolved by
(a) Government of India
(b) Botanical Survey of India
(c) UNESCO
(d) UNDP
3. Which one of the following is not a method of in situ conservation of biodiversity?
(a) Biosphere Reserve
(b) Wildlife Sanctuary
(c) Botanical Garden
(d) Sacred Grove
4. Which of the following species is endangered?
(a) Panthera leo persica
(b) Pythan molurus
(c) Chelonia mydas
(d) Siberian crane
5. According to the Endangered Species Act, what is the definition of a threatened species?
(a) An endemic species that is found nowhere else in the world.
(b) A species that is in a sink habitat.
(c) A species that is in danger of extinction in all or a large part of its range.
(d) A species that is likely to become endangered.
6. The Wildlife (Protection) Act 1972 was first amended in:
(a) 1991
(b) 1995
(c) 2001
(d) 2007
7. Which one of the following statement is correct?
(a) The greater the BOD of waste water, more is its polluting potential.
(b) The greater the BOD of waste water, less is its polluting potential.
(c) The lesser the BOD of waste water, more is its polluting potential.
(d) None of the above
8. Which one of the following expanded forms of the following acronyms is correct?
(a) IPCC - International Panel for Climate Change
(b) UNEP - United Nations Environmental Policy
(c) EPA - Environmental Pollution Agency
(d) IUCN - International Union for Conservation of Nature and Natural Resources
9. In an area where DDT had been used extensively, the population of birds declined significantly because:
(a) birds stopped laying eggs.
(b) earthworms in the area got eradicated.
(c) cobras were feeding exclusively on birds.
(d) many of the birds laid, did not hatch.
10. Measuring Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD) is a method used for:
(a) estimating the amount of organic matter in sewage water.
(b) working out the efficiency of oil driven automobile engines.
(c) measuring the activity of Saccharomyces cerevisae in producing curd on a commercial scale.
(d) working out the efficiency of RBCs about their capacity to carry oxygen.
11. Which of the following is mainly produced by the activity of anaerobic bacteria on sewage?
(a) Laughing gas
(b) Propane
(c) Mustard gas
(d) Marsh gas
12. Eutrophication is often seen in:
(a) Deserts
(b) Fresh water lakes
(c) Ocean
(d) Mountains
13. Which of the following is the most important cause of animals and plants being driven to extinction?
(a) Over - exploitation
(b) Alien species invasion
(c) Habitat loss and fragmentation
(d) Co-extinction
14. The region of Biosphere Reserve which is legally protected and where no human activity is allowed is known as:
(a) Buffer zone
(b) Transition zone
(c) Restoration zone
(d) Core zone
15. A river with an inflow of domestic sewage rich in organic waste may result in:
[JSTSE]
(a) drying of the river very soon due to algal bloom.
(b) increased population of aquatic food web organisms.
(c) an increased production of fish due to biodegradable nutrients.
(d) death of fish due to lack of oxygen.
16. Which is the national aquatic animal of India? [JSTSE]
(a) Gangetic shark
(b) River dolphin
(c) Blue whale
(d) Sea-horse
17. Depletion of which gas in the atmosphere can lead to an increased incidence of skin cancers?
[JSTSE]
(a) Nitrous oxide
(b) Ozone
(c) Ammonia
(d) Methane
18. The species of plants and animals found exclusively in a particular area are called:
[JSTSE]
(a) Endemic
(b) Endangered
(c) Biological
(d) Alien
19. Ozone depletion is caused by:
[JSTSE]
(a) CFCs
(b) $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
(c) $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$
(d) CO
20. In stratosphere, which of the following elements acts as a catalyst in degradation of ozone and release of molecular oxygen?
[JSTSE]
(a) Carbon
(b) Cl
(c) Oxygen
(d) Fe
21. Which of the following pairs of gases is mainly responsible for green house effect?
[JSTSE]
(a) Ozone and Ammonia
(b) Oxygen and Nitrogen
(c) Nitrogen and Sulphur dioxide
(d) Carbon dioxide and Methane
22. The life supporting zone of the earth is
[JSTSE]
(a) Biosphere
(b) Atmosphere
(c) Lithosphere
(d) Hydrosphere
23. According to Robert May, the global species diversity is about
[JSTSE]
(a) 20 million
(b) 50 million
(c) 7 million
(d) 1.5 million
24. Which of the following regions of the globe exhibits highest species diversity?
[JSTSE]
(a) Madagascar
(b) Himalayas
(c) Amazon forests
(d) Western Ghats of India

Assertion \& Reason:

1. (d) Ex-situ conservation is the preservation of components of biological diversity outside their natural habitats. Hence, increase of Manipur deer from 17 animals to 150 in calcutta and Delhi zoos is one of an example of ex-situ conservation.
2. (b)
3. (a)
4. (a) Cultural or accelerated eutrophication is nutrient enrichment of water bodies due to human activities like passage of sewage, industrial effluents and run off from fertilised fields rich in nitrates and phosphates. Nutrients present in sewage, agriculture wastes and fertilisers cause dense growth of plants and planktonic algae. Soon planktonic algae increase in number and impart a characteristic colouration to water depending upon the pigments present in them. The excess growth of planktonic algae that causes colouration of water is called algal bloom.
5. (a)
6. sustainable
7. renewable resources
8. solar
9. Agroforestry
10. sustainable development
11. over exploitation
12. contamination
13. biodiversity
14. electricity, irrigation
15. Carboxyhaemoglobin
16. oxygen
17. renewable resources, non-renewable

## Very Short Answer Questions:

1. Biodiversity of an area means the number of species of plants and animals found in the area.
2. (a) Biodiversity which faces large destruction.
3. UNEP-United Nations Environment Programme.

IPCC-Intergovernmental Panel on Climatic Change.
4. (i) Non-renewable Resource-Petroleum/Coal.
(ii) Renewable Resource-Forest
5. Tendu.
6. Sulphur dioxide and Nitrogen oxide.
7. The presence of faecal coliform bacteria in aquatic environment indicates that the water has been contaminated with the faecal material of man or animal.
8. Advantages of Dam :
(i) Control of floods and drought.
(ii) Generation of electricity.
9. By the degradation of biomass millions of years ago inside earth under heat, pressure and anaerobiasis.
10. Carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen and sulphur.
11. Coal and petroleum
12. As the quality of the water in the Ganga was very poor, so to reduce the pollution level in the river, Ganga Action Plan was launched.
13. Turning-off the water taps when they are not in use. Checking the leakage of water pipelines and getting them repaired.
14. Biogas is considered as an excellent fuel for the following reasons:
(i) It is a pollution free gas as the combustion process is smoke free and has high calorific value.
(ii) It is a clean fuel as it does not leave any residue on burning.
Match the Following:

1. (A) → s, (B) → p, (C) → q, (D) → r
2. $(\mathrm{A}) \rightarrow \mathrm{r},(\mathrm{B}) \rightarrow \mathrm{p},(\mathrm{C}) \rightarrow \mathrm{s},(\mathrm{D}) \rightarrow \mathrm{q}$
3. (A) → t; (B) → u; (C) → v; (D) → p
(E) $\rightarrow \mathrm{q}$; (F) $\rightarrow \mathrm{w}$; (G) $\rightarrow \mathrm{s}$; (H) $\rightarrow$ (r)

(b) (i) Algae
(ii) Oxygen

## (Brief Explanations of Selected Questions) <br> \section*{Selected Questions)}

## SOLUTIONS

(b) A megawatt is a unit for measuring power. i.e., 1 $\mathrm{MW}=1000000 \mathrm{~W}$.
(c) Construction of Tehri Dam has resulted in large scale destruction of biodiversity in catchment area and large scale displacement of people.
