## Short Answer Questions:

1. Water Pollution can me measure through the various broad categories. It can be defined into three major categories.
(i) Physical: It includes the measurement of water temperature, turbidity, total suspended particle in the water sample.
(ii) Chemical: It includes the measurement of pH value, Chemical Oxygen Demand(COD) and Biological Oxygen Demand(BOD). There are some other factors which also falls under this categories like oil, nutrients and metal present in the water sample.
(iii) Biological: It includes the presence of Microbes in the water, the total nutrients aquatic lives are getting from the water. Water pollution can also be measured by the aquatic habitat present in the water sample.
2. If waste materials are segregated at source:
(a) Fresh peels of vegetables and fruits, cooked food like chapattis and other left-overs can be used to feed cattle.
(b) Organic wastes (garbage) can be used for composting.
(c) Paper, plastics, metals and glass materials can be recycled.
(d) Metallic containers, glass bottles, cardboard cartons, wooden furniture can be reused.
3. (i) Fossil-fuel is non-renewable, if used at current rate they will last for few years (e.g., coal for 200 years and petroleum for 40 years from now).
(ii) Burning of fossil fuel causes air pollution due to release of oxides of sulphur and nitrogen.
(iii) Fossil fuels may also contribute to public health issues. Air pollution that comes from fossil fuel consumption can trigger symptoms that are similar to asthma.
(iv) Fossil fuels can damage the environment through human error. It can also spill during transport, creating environmental damage as the product spills out. This is particularly problematic for petroleum products.
4. (i) Reforesting those forest lands from where large number of trees have been cut (Silviculture).
(ii) By taking help from local people.
(iii) Establishing national parks and sanctuaries.
(iv) By law enforcement.
5. Watershed management emphasises scientific soil and water conservation in order to increase the biomass production.

## Advantages:

(i) The main aim is to develop primary resources of land and water.
(ii) To produce secondary resources of plants and animals for use in a manner which will hot cause ecological imbalance.
6. We need to use our resources carefully as:
(a) our resources are limited and the demand for all resources is increasing at an exponential rate.
(b) sustainable management of natural resources is required so that these resources last for the generations to come.
(c) we require equitable distribution of these resources.
7. Fossil-fuel contains hydrogen, nitrogen and sulphur in addition to carbon. When fossil-fuels are burnt carbon monoxide, carbon dioxide, water, oxide of nitrogen and sulphur are produced. Products like carbon monoxide, oxide of nitrogen and sulphur are poisonous in high concentration. At the same time excessive production of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ causes global warming as it is one of the green house gases.
8. Stakeholders in respect of forest are those people who utilise various forest products. These are :
(i) The people who live in and around the forest.
(ii) Industrialist engaged in manufacture of furniture, biris, plywood and sports goods.
(iii) Forest department of government.
(iv) Wild-life and nature enthusiasts, the industrialists cause maximum damage to forests as they consider the forest as merely a source of raw material for their factories.
9. The three problems that arise due to construction of large dams are :
(i) Social problem : Displacement of large number of peasants and tribals.
(ii) Economic problem : These projects swallow up large amounts of public money without the generation of proportionate benefits.
(iii) Environmental problem : As they contribute to deforestation and the loss of biodiversity.
10. Ground water is more advantageous as compared to surface water due to following reasons:
(i) Ground water generally does not get polluted, if it is away (at least 20 mtr .) from the sanitary works. The water gets filtered while percolating through sand \& stones. Therefore groundwater remains pure and clean.
(ii) Since bore well is closed, no risk of getting contaminated.
(iii) Since it is closed no danger of children or animals falling into it.
(iv) Temperature of deep water remains stable. It feels cool in summer and warm in winter.
(v) Since bore are deep, chances of water remaining available in summer are more.
11. Without the regular grazing of grass by cattles like sheep and goats the grass first grows taller and taller and then falls over. Due to this growth, fresh grass is prevented resulting in loss of green cover.
12. Forest,plants are important as they serve as the primary products on which other living beings depend and serve as basic food, raw materials for growth in the country. Forests are the great source of many herbs and material having medicinal value. This is how it play a productive role.
Forest play a protective role by acting as a natural habitat for Flora and fauna and also protect soil from erosion as well as by enhancing the quality of environment. Forest play an important role in regulation as it modify the local climate, thus maintaining the ecological balance by providing oxygen and absorbing carbon dioxide.
13. There are many examples which suggest that involvement of local communities is necessary for any conservation effort. The Bishnoi community of Rajasthan is one such example. Amrita Devi Bishnoi is still remembered with reverence for the way she fought for protecting the khejri trees in Khejrali village. She; along with 363 other people; sacrificed her life for the protection of khejri trees in 1731. The 'Amrita Devi Bishnoi National Award for Wildlife Conservation' has been named in her honour.
14. Biodiversity is an estimate of the number of different life forms available in an area. Various species are available in a forestwhich include bacteria, fungi, ferns, plants, nematodes, insects, birds, reptiles and mammals. Forests are therefore, known as biodiversity hot spots. An individual can contribute in the management of forest and wildlife by
(i) Avoiding of clearing of forests and killing of wildlfe.
(ii) Educating people about necessity of forest and wildlife in our life.
15. (i) Building of rest house for tourist in park causes disturbance in natural habitat. Restriction in movement waste disposal poaching/hunting.
(ii) Allowing animals to graze in national parks causes ecological imbalance, less food for herbivores, overgrazing could result in the loss of habitat.
16. (i) Reuse implies to the use of the same material over and over again.
(ii) Resources are saved and no energy is consumed in reuse of materials.
(iii) Certain used materials are converted into other useful materials in recycling.
(iv) In recycling of materials, thus energy is consumed and the resources may get wasted.
17. Management of forest and wildlife resources are consider as a challenging task because of :
(i) Various stakeholders of forest, Management of forest and wildlife has to consider the needs of all these stakeholders which becomes a challenging task.
(ii) Sources of raw material industries would consider the forest as or its factories and huge interest group demand the government for access to these raw materials at extremely low rates. This further poses a challenge to manage the forest and wildlife reasources.
18. Two main advantages of water harvesting at the community level include:
(i) Water evaporation does not occur, rather it percolates into the ground. Therefore, it results in the recharging of wells.
(ii) Water storage provides moisture to the vegetation within the area.
19. (a) Two ways to bring awareness on "how to save water" are:-
(i) By organising a demonstration of the households practices to minimize the wastage of water.
(ii) By organising poster competitions on "how to save water" every week every year to create awareness.
(b) Rainwater harvesting is better a way by which the underground water table does not go down further. In this we can collect rainwater and store it in the form of pits and lakes ensuring that the water seeps through the soil and recharges the aquifers.
20. A dam is built to control water through placement of a blockage of earth, rock and/or concrete across a stream or river. Large dams can ensure the storage of adequate water not just for irrigation, but also for generating electricity. Criticisms about large dams address three problems in particular are:
(i) Social problems because they displace large number of peasants and tribals without adequate compensation or rehabilitation.
(ii) Economic problems because they swallow up huge amounts of public money without the generation of proportionate benefits.
(iii) Environmental problems because they contribute enormously to deforestation and the loss of biological diversity.
21. Water harvesting - Rainwater harvesting (RWH) is a simple method by which rainfall is collected for future usage. The collected rainwater may be stored, utilised in different ways or directly used for recharge purposes. The two main advantages associated with water harvesting at the community level are :

- Over-exploitation of water resources will be reduced.
- Helps to recharge the natural wells and provides moisture for vegetation over a wide area.
The two main causes for the failure of sustained availibility of water are :
- Increase in Industries lead to extra requirement of water for manufacturing goods and products.
- Increasing effects of global warming.

22. Role of forests in conservation :
(a) It prevents soil erosion and helps in maintaining fertility of soil.
(b) It provides shelter to wild animals.
(c) It reduces atmospheric pollution.
(d) It increases humidity and frequency of rainfall.

The forests get depleted due to forest fires, urbanisation, industrialization, overgrazing by animals, felling of trees etc. The consequences of deforestation will be :
(a) Change in climate and global warming
(b) Soil erosion and floods
(c) Extinction of wildlife, etc.

## Long Answer Questions:

1. Natural resources exploitation, exploration, mining and processing have caused different types of environmental damages which include ecological disturbances, destruction of natural flora and fauna, pollution of air, water and land, instability of soil and rock masses, landscape degradation, desertification and global warming.

- The environmental damage has in turn resulted in waste of arable land as well as economic crops and trees. Since much of the damage is inevitable if the natural resources must be developed, both the government and the natural resource industry must be involved in taking precautionary and remedial measures that can minimize the ill-effects of natural resources exploitation.
- Emphasis should shift from waste disposal to waste minimization through sorting, recycling, bioremediation, afforestation, sewage treatment and pollution control, while the government should provide the regulatory legislation with appropriate sanctions or where these regulatory bodies already exist, the enforcement of laws and policy implementation is of paramount importance.
- The oil and gas industries, mining companies and other natural resources exploitation bodies are expected to carry out mandatory precautions, remedies or compensation for damage done.

2. Coal and petroleum are resources which are nonrenewable. They will exhaust in due course of time. Coal and petroleum have been formed of bio-mass degradation millions of years ago. Therefore, they have carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen and sulphur. When these are burnt, the products are carbon dioxide, water, oxides of nitrogen and oxides of sulphur. All of these are pollutants of the atmosphere. More use of fossil fuels causes more and more release of these pollutants. Thus, management of fossil-fuels is to be seen from the environmental and economical perspective.
3. Sustainable Management: It is controlling the use of resource in such a way as to provide for its equitable and continuous availability not only to the present generation but also for future generations without any harmful impact on the environment. Environmentalists insist on sustainable natural resources management because of the following reasons:
(i) Equitable Distribution: The resources would be made available to all sections of the society instead of a few rich persons.
(ii) Controlled Exploitation: The exploitation is restricted so as to meet the just demands of the society.
(iii) Minimum Wastage: Technology is employed to ensure minimum wastage during processing of the resource.
(iv) Disposal of Waste: The waste of the processing units is not allowed to degrade the environment. Instead proper safety measures are taken to convert the waste into useful products.
4. (i) The three arguments that could be need in order to convince the audience that human beings are responsible for the aforesaid disaster include :

- Mining
- Exploitation of Natural Resources
- Clearing of forests
- Construction of concrete jungle

Mining of ores for obtaining various metals and other raw materials impact the ecosystem in a negative way. Excessive mining may lead to formation of sink holes, ground water pollution which would ultimately lead to loss of biodiversity. Overuse of natural resources could lead to their early exhaustion which could affect the ecosystem in a major way. Moreover, clearing of forest for constructing concrete jungle would cause an imbalance of the ecosystem. All these particles resulting from human greed would cause the nature to act in an unpredictable manner.
(ii) The three values which could be inculcated in this debate are : raising of social awareness among the public, eco-friendly approach towards use of natural resources, and proper understanding of the functioning of society. The measures could help in protection and improvement of the natural environment.

## Reasoning Based Questions:

1. One of the main reason of energy crisis has been the steady increase in the world's population and its demands for fuel and products.
2. Water conservation is necessary because of the following reasons:
(i) Rainwater serves as a boon for us. So we can save rainwater for our daily needs.
(ii) Prevents water pollution.
(iii) Demand for wayer is needed in agricultural fields, animal farms etc.
(iv) Urban and industrial areas require a higher amount of water than rural areas.
(v) Increases energy savings and generate efficient use of water resources.
3. We need to manage our natural resources because of the following reasons:
(i) The resources of the earth are limited. Long term perspective is required to meet the needs of the present as wrell as for the generations to come.
(ii) As the human population is increasing rapidly, the demand for resources increases day by day. Thus proper management of these resources can ensure that they last for the generations to come.
4. Reusing is better than recycling because it saves the energy that comes with having to dismantle and remanufacture products. It also significantly reduces waste and pollution because it reduces the need for raw materials, saving both forests and water supplies.
5. Despite good rains, we are not able to meet the demand for water of all people because:
(i) Our population is increasing rapidly.
(ii) Due to lack of sufficient vegetation cover on ground, only a little rain water seeps into the ground and get stored as ground water.
(iii) The high yielding varieties of crops require much more water for irrigation.
(iv) Discharge of untreated sewage and industrial waste into water bodies.
6. Coal and petroleum are fossil fuels. Although fossil fuels are formed by the degradation of biomass, they are formed from the remains buried in the soil millions of years ago. It is the continuous pressure, heat and time which convert the organic matter into coal and petroleum. Due to the slow and long process of formation of these fuels, it is advisable to conserve them since they are on the verge of exhaustion.
7. Opposition for these projects were mainly due to two reasons:
(i) Social: As they displace large number of peasants and tribals without adequate compensation or rehabilitation.
(ii) Environmental: As they contribute enormously to deforestation and the loss of biodiversity.

## HOTS Questions:

1. The three instance where human intervention saved the forest from destruction are -
(i) Silent Valley (Kerala) : A dam was proposed to be built up here but opposition from environmentalists and others forced the government to convert it into biosphere reserve.
(ii) Garwal Himalayas (Uttarkhand) : 'Chipko Andolan' prevented the destruction of forests and saved the environment from deterioration.
(iii) Kelase Forests (Karnataka) : The felling of trees in the forests was opposed by 'Apiko Chaluvati' led by Pandurang Hegde.
2. The replenishment of forest is necessary because :
(a) It is used to conserve soil.
(b) It provides shelter to wild animals.
(c) It reduces atmospheric pollution.
(d) It controls flood and increases frequency of rainfall.
3. Coal and petroleum have been produced from large amounts of biomass entrapped inside the earth under high temperature, pressure and anaerobic conditions. Such a situation develops only rarely like big upheavals on earth. At present no more coal or petroleum is being formed. All that is available has been formed millions of years ago. Being rich source of energy, coal and petroleum are being consumed in ever increasing amount in industry, transport, kitchens, etc. If the trend continues, soon they will be exhausted. Therefore, they must be conserved by developing more efficient machines, hybrid engines and using hydrogen as a fuel.

## Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar)

## Text - Book Questions:

1. The changes in our habits that can be made to become more environment friendly are as follows:
(i) Segregate waste into recyclable and non-recyclable parts.
(ii) Switch-off the lights when not in use.
(iii) Plastic, paper or glass must be recycled or reused and not dumped with other wastes.
(iv) Air conditioner, geyser and other electrical appliances should be use efficiently.
(v) Prefer walking instead of vehicles for travelling short distance.
2. There is a need to exploit our resources carefully. The demand for the resources is increasing at an alarming rate and these should be used in such a manner that they last for the generations to come. The advantage of exploiting resources with short-term aims will make the resources available in large quantity and the speed of development will be accelerated. Ultimately, hardly any resources will be left for the future generations. Therefore, using reserves with a short-term aim will prove suicidal and disastrous. We should use resources with a long-term aim which form the basis for sustainable management.
3. The management of our natural resources requires a long-term perspective. The advantages of long-term perspective in managing our resources are as follows:
(i) Resources will last for future generations and they will not get fully exhausted.
(ii) Resources will be distributed equitably among all.
(iii) Environmental problems and damage to the environment will not be caused, if long- term perspective is kept in mind. Thus, by sticking to long-term perspective, ecological imbalance will not be caused.
4.     - There should be equitable distribution of resources among all people, so that all are benefitted from the development of these resources. Our natural resources are limited and the human population is increasing at a tremendous rate. The demand for the resources is also increasing at an alarming rate. The benefit of the development of resources should reach the poor, the needy and the local people, for that their equitable distribution would be just and very appropriate.

- Many forces can go against the equitable distribution of natural resources. Geographical factors are the most important factors which do not allow equitable distribution of resources. Economy is another factor which prevents equitable distribution of resources. Beside this, human greed, corruption, and the lobby of the rich and powerful are also the forces working against an equitable distribution of resources.

5. Wildlife and forests should be conserved for the following reasons:
(i) Conservation of wildlife and forests is vital for the ecological stability of a particular area.
(ii) Forests are necessary for the formation of humus and for maintaining soil fertility.
(iii) Forests are the habitat for wildlife and they are an important constituent of the various food chains and food webs. Forests are needed for the smooth functioning of biogeochemical cycles.
(iv) Forests purify air and protect the earth from greenhouse effect by removing carbon dioxide and converting it into oxygen. Thus, forests protect us from the harmful effects of global warming.
(v) Forests provide employment to a large number of people and also help in generating revenue.
6. Forest conservation is the practice of planning and maintaining forested areas for the benefit and sustainability of future generations. The conservation of forest can be done by the following approaches:
(i) We should not bring about over-exploitation and deforestation of forests, the activities of tourist department should be checked.
(ii) People should show their participation in saving the forest by protesting against the cutting of the trees.
(iii) Not to allow the destruction of forests for making roads, dams and hotels etc.
(iv) There should be proper laws for the exploitation of forest resources.
(v) Afforestation is one of the most important approach for the conservation of forest.
7. There are various traditional systems of water harvesting and management in our region. These are:

- Bundhis in Madhya Pradesh and Uttar Pradesh
- Kulhs in Himachal Pradesh
- Khadins, tanks and nadir in Rajasthan
- Ahars and pynes in Bihar
- Eris (tanks) in Tamil Nadu
- Surangams in Kerala
- Ponds in Kandi belt of Jammu and Kashmir

8. Water harvesting system in hilly and mountainous areas is different from plains and from plateau region. In hilly areas like Himachal Pradesh a local system of irrigation was developed called kulhs. The water flowing in the streams was diverted in, manrjnaale channels which took this water to numerous villages down the hillside. Whereas water in plains is collected in check dams or tanks, tals or bundhis.
9. The main source of water in my region i.e., Delhi is Yamuna river. Water in Delhi comes from river Yamuna which is then treated and supplied to the residents through pipes. Some water is also taken from the ground through bore-wells. This water is not available to all those people living in the areas which are not approved by municipal authorities. In these areas, the ground water is the main source which is obtained through hand pumps and tube-wells.

## Text - Book Exercise:

1. By applying the formula of three Rs, we can be environment friendly i.e., Reduce, Recycle and Reuse
(a) Reduce: We must save water and electricity. Their wastage should be stopped.
(b) Recycle: The wastes which can be recycled should be separated from other wastes.
(c) Reuse: We can reuse same materials instead of throwing them. e.g., paper envelop.
2. The forest dwellers should have the authority to decide the management of forest produces because forest dwellers have traditionally been dependent on forests for their livelihood but at the same time are aware that excessive use of forest produce will be detrimental for their own survival. They have learnt to respect the forest and always resist from overexploitation of resources and have some traditional knowledge which can really help in forest conservation.
3. As an individual we can make difference in the management of natural resources in the following ways:
(a) For forest and wildlife:

- I will not buy any animal products which are made by killing them like fur, skin, tusk, horn etc.
- As an individual, will ensure afforestation in my area.
- By reducing the use of paper. This would help in minimising the need for cutting down trees.
(b) For water resources:
- By preventing the wastage of water by closing the tap when not in use.
- Recycling the water from washing machine for toilets use.
- I will also ensure that all surface water sources and the groundwater are prevented from being contaminated.
(c) For coal and petroleum:
- Using bicycle for going to school.
- By organising awareness campaigns to educate people about the benefits of using environment-friendly modes of transport.
- By using electric gadgets that use less power.

4. (a) To conserve natural resources :
(i) Saved electricity by switching off unnecessary lights and fans.
(ii) Used disposable paper plates and glasses in party.
(iii) Tree plantation in school
(iv) Used bicycle instead of scooter for going market.
(v) Convinced my friends to do all the above things.
(b) To increase pressure on our natural resources :
(i) Wasted food in party
(ii) Used plastic bags
(iii) Wasted rain water by not harvesting it
(iv) Used D.D.T.
(v) Wasted petrol by using a four wheeler for giving alone at the party.
5. The changes that we make in our lifestyles so that we can use the natural resources on a sustainable basis are as follows:
(i) Use of biogas as domestic fuel should be encouraged.
(ii) Rainwater should be stored in underground tanks or should be allowed to percolate in a soil and used to recharge the groundwater table.
(iii) Switch-off the electrical appliances when not in use.
(iv) We should use public transport and avoid the use of private vehicles.
(v) We should follow the three R's (Reduce, Reuse and Recycle) and incorporate in our life-style.

10. Hint :

(b) Use of CNG or clean fuel.

11. (A) The water reservoir is a pond in Figure (a) and underground water body in the Figure (b).

(a) It does not evaporate.
(b) It spreads out to recharge wells.
(c) Provides moisture for vegetation over a wide area.
(d) It is protected from contamination by animal and human wastes.

13. The Arabari forests located in Midnapur district of West Bengal were badly degraded. West Bengal Forest Department, 1972 planned to revive the degraded woods by involving the villagers of the surrounding area to the protect 1272 hectares of this forest. In return villagers were benefitted by getting employment in silviculture and harvesting. They were allowed to collect wood and fodder on payment of nominal fee. The people cooperated with full vigour as the regained their rights to forest produce. In this way, by the active and willing participation of the local people, the sal forests of Arabari were conserved.
14. Use of fertilizers increased the level of organic nutrients which in turn support the growth of small, green, aquatic plants and the surface of water was completely covered with plants. The process is called as eutrophication. Consumption of oxygen by these plants led to death of fishes and other aquatic animals. Since water surface is covered with these planktons, underwater plants deprived of light and died.

17. (a) Cultural or artificial eutrophication is an accelerated form of eutrophication. Cultural eutrophication occurs when human activity introduces increased amounts of these nutrients, which speed up plant growth and eventually choke the lake of all of its animal life.
The prime contaminates are nitrates and phosphates which act like plant nutrients.
The water from electricity generating units reduces the number of organisms sensitive to high temperature and may enhance the growth of plants and fish in extremely cold areas, only after causing damage to indigenous flora and fauna.

19. (d) Domestic sewage contains faecal matter, having coliform bacteria E. coli. If a water body has coliform bacteria, it indicates pollution from domestic sewage.
20. (d) The chipko andolan or the chipko movement is a movement that practiced methods of Satyagraha where both male and female activists from Uttarakhand played vital roles. It was headed by social activist Sunder Lal Bahuguna. to save trees from felling. The movement got its name due to people's action of hugging trees in order to prevent them from cutting down by state forest contractors.
21. (a) Chipko movement was an environmental movement aimed at protection and conservation of trees. It was started from Jodhpur in Rajasthan.

## Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs):

1. (a) Habitat loss and fragmentation are the most important cause for animals and plants being driven to extinction. Habitats of various organisms are altered or destroyed by uncontrolled and unsustainable human activities (such as deforestation, slash and burn agriculture, mining, and urbanisation). This will lead to breaking up of the habitat into small pieces, which effect the movement of migratory animals and also, decrease the genetic exchange between populations leading to a declination of species.
2. (a)
3. (c) Botanical garden is an ex situ conservation (offsite conservation) method to conserve biodiversity.
4. (b) 5. (d) 6. (a) 7. (a)
5. (d) IPPC - International Plant Protection Convention UNEP - United Nations Environment Programme EPA - Environmental Protection Agency
6. (d) High concentration of DDT disturbs calcium metabolism in birds which caused thinning of eggshell and their premature breaking.
7. (a) BOD is a measure of organic matter present in water. It refers to amount of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ consumed by microbes to decompose all the organic matter in 1 L of water at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for 5 days. The greater the BOD of waste water, more is its polluting potential.
8. (d) Marsh gas is another term for methane, produced by plants decomposing under water.
9. (b) Eutrophication is often seen in fresh water lakes. The main causes of eutrophication in these water systems are sediments and thermal stratification, with agricultural runoff from fertilisers and manure, containing nitrates and phosphates and discharge of partially treated or untreated sewages or phosphate containing detergents.
10. (c) The only primary cause of human-induced extinction events is simply human overpopulation of planet Earth. The most important causal anthropogenic activities is habitat destruction and fragmentation.
11. (d) Biosphere reserve is a protected area for wildlife with multipurpose activities. It has three zones
(a) Core zone - does not have any human interference.
(b) Buffer zone - has limited human activity.
(c) Transition zone - allowshumansettlement,grazing cultivation etc.
12. (a)
13. (a) Biosphere is a life supporting zone.
14. (a)
15. (b) River Dolphin is the National Aquatic Animal of India. This mammal is also said to represent the purity of the holy Ganga, as it can only survive in pure and fresh water. Platanista gangetica has a long pointed snout and also has visible teeth in both the upper and lower jaws.
16. (d) Domestic sewage rich in organic waste leads to increase in Biological Oxygen Demand, which leads to decrease D.O. (dissolved oxygen) which leads to death of fishes.
17. (b)
18. (d) Gases, responsible for Green house effect are carbon dioxide and methane.
A green house gas is a gas that absorbs and emits radiant energy within the thermal infrared range. Greenhouse gases cause the greenhouse effect. The primary greenhouse gases in Earth's atmosphere are water vapour, carbon dioxide, methane, nitrous oxide and ozone. Without greenhouse gases, the average temperature of Earth's surface would be about " $18{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\left(0^{\circ} \mathrm{F}\right)$, rather than the present average of $15^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\left(59^{\circ} \mathrm{F}\right)$.
19. (b) The ozone layer or ozone shield refers to a region of Earth's stratosphere that absorbs most of the Sun's ultraviolet (UV) radiation. This layers contains high concentrations of ozone ( $\mathrm{O}_{3}$ ) relative to other parts of the atmosphere.
20. (c) The largely tropical Amazon rain forest in South America has the greatest biodiversity on earth.
21. (c) Robert May estimated global species diversity at about 7 million. Although some extreme estimates range from 20 to 50 million.

## Case Study

1. In Rajasthan, the extreme weather conditions like drought reduces the water availability in Khadin during rainy season, thereby reducing the productivity of grasses and crops.
2. In Himachal Pradesh, farmers have now opted for transplanted rice variety as compared to the direct-seeded ones because of the neglected use of Kulhs. The latter requires direct monsoon water for irrigation which was only possible through Kuhls.

## 7. Biotechnology

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-248.jpg?height=387&width=398&top_left_y=381&top_left_x=1410)

Foundation

## (CONCEPTMAP)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-248.jpg?height=1711&width=1466&top_left_y=828&top_left_x=335)

Biotechnology allows the manipulation and transfer of selected genes between different organisms, species, genera and phyla. Once transferred, these genes may be transferred to offspring of the modified individual through normal reproductive processes. Genetic engineering has resulted in the production of Genetically Modified Organisms (GMOs). Genetically Modified Organisms (GMOs) can therefore be defined as organisms in which the genetic material has been altered in a way that it does not occur naturally.

## PRINCIPLES OF BIOTECHNOLOGY

The science of biotechnology is based mainly on two core technologies. These are :

1. Genetic engineering which refers to artificial synthesis, isolation, modification, combination, addition and repair of the genetic material (DNA) to introduce it into the host organism so as to alter its phenotype as per the human needs.
Genetic engineering was started by Paul Berg, Herbert Boyer and Stanley Cohen, (1973) who generated first recombinant DNA molecule by combining a gene from a SV 40 virus with the DNA of bacteriaphage lambda.
Collins and Hohn (1979) developed a method for obtaining recombinant plasmids (having a small piece of lambda DNA) packed into the protective coat of $\lambda$ phage and then transduced into E. coli.
2. Biochemical engineering processes to enable the growth of desired microbe/eukaryotic cell in large quantities in a sterile medium (Tissue culture technique) for the manufacture and multiplication of biotechnological products (antibiotics, vaccines, enzymes, medicines, hormones, etc.)

## Recombinant DNA Technology

Recombinant DNA technology, popularly known as 'genetic engineering' is a stream of biotechnology which deals with the manipulation of genetic material by man in vitro. It involves a number of techniques where a DNA sequence (with desired gene) is cut into fragments and then introduced into host cells with or without the help of carriers (called vectors) to alter its phenotype, as per the requirement.

## Basic Tools of Recombinant DNA Technology

1. Enzyme :

A number of specific kind of enzymes are employed in genetic engineering. These include lysing, cleaving, synthesising, and joining enzymes.
(i) Lysing enzymes: These are used to open up the cells to get DNA for genetic experiments. Lysozyme is commonly used to dissolve the bacterial cell wall.
(ii) Cleaving enzyme: These are used to break DNA molecule. They are of 3 kinds-
(a) Exonucleases, which cut off nucleotides from 5' or 3' ends of DNA molecule;
(b) Endonucleases, which cleave DNA duplex at any point except the ends; and
(c) Restriction endonucleases, which cleaves DNA duplex at specific points called restriction sites in such a way that fragments produced after cleavage either have single-stranded free ends or unpaired free bases on the free ends. The single-stranded free ends are called 'sticky ends' because they can join similar complementary ends of DNA fragment from some other source. The free ends with no unpaired bases are called blunt ends. Restriction endonucleases are also called molecular scissors or a chemical scalpel. These enzymes are used in genetic engineering to form recombinant DNA molecules.
They are found in bacteria and their function is to recognise and cleave foreign DNA such as DNA of infecting virus in order to provide protection against them. Recognition site of restriction enzymes is a sequence of 4 to 8 base pairs that reads same on the two strands ( $5^{\prime}$ to $3^{\prime}$ direction or from $3^{\prime}$ to $5^{\prime}$ direction). Such sequences are called palindromes. For example, restriction endonuclease EcoRI is found in the bacterium Escherichia coli which recognises the base sequence GAATTC in DNA duplex and cleaves its strands at different positions i.e., between G and A producing sticky ends. EcoRV restriction enzyme recognises the base sequence GATATC and cleaves both the strands exactly at the opposing nucleotide leaving blunt ends on the resulting DNA fragments.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-249.jpg?height=359&width=930&top_left_y=2129&top_left_x=603)
Fig.: Action of restriction enzymes (a) EcoRI producing sticky ends (b) EcoRV producing blunt ends

(iii) Synthesising enzymes: These play a role in the synthesis of DNA strands complementary to existing DNA or RNA templates. They are of 2 types: (a) Reverse transcriptases, which helps in the synthesis of complementary DNA strands from an RNA template; (b) DNA polymerases, which aid in the synthesis of complementary DNA strands from DNA templates.
(iv) Joining enzyme: These enzymes help in joining two DNA fragments. For example, DNA ligase from Escherichia coli is used to join DNA fragments by forming a phosphodiester bond. This enzyme is called molecular glue.
(v) End modification enzyme: These enzymes make changes to the ends of DNA molecules such as alkaline phosphatases cuts off phosphate group from the $5^{\prime}$ end of linearised circular DNA to check its recircularisation.

## 2. Cloning Vectors (Vehicle DNA)

The DNA used as a carrier for transferring a fragment of foreign DNA into a suitable host is called vehicle DNA. It is also called cloning vector or gene carrier. The desired gene is introduced into a vector wherein recombinant DNA (r-DNA) is formed. The vector carrying r-DNA divides in the host independently, thereby forming several copies of r-DNA. Five types of DNA are used as vehicles : plasmid DNA, bacteriophage DNA, DNA of plant and animal viruses, transposons (jumping genes), and artificial DNA. The first two are more commonly used vectors.
(i) Plasmids: Plasmids are small, double stranded, closed circular, symbiotic DNA molecules that occur naturally in bacteria, outside the bacterial chromosome (extra chromosomal DNA). A bacterial cell may possess one or many copies of one or more plasmids. They are inherited from parent bacterial cell to daughter cells and have capability of self replication in the cytoplasm of bacterial cell. The circular molecule of DNA can be broken to yield a linear molecule which passes from one bacterial cell to another (transformation). There are many kinds of plasmids which differ from one another in size and in composition of genes. They vary in size from 2 kb to more than 400 kb .
(ii) Bacteriophage vectors: Bacteriophages (or phages) are viruses that infect bacteria by introducing their DNA into the bacterial cells. The injected DNA molecule of the virus integrates into the bacterial chromosome. This viral DNA multiplies with the bacterial chromosome as prophage. The prophage may dissociate from the bacterial chromosome and assemble. They can be introduced into host cells by a process called transformation (discussed later) into a number of phages which burst out of the cell and reinfect the neighbouring cells (Lytic pathway). This property of bacteriophages to transfer their DNA into specific bacterial cells where they get integrated and multiply along with bacterial chromosome gave scientists the idea that specially designed phage vectors (containing foreign DNA) would prove best tools for gene cloning. Moreover, they are more advantageous than plasmid vectors because - (i) They can be used for large DNA fragments; and (ii) They can easily be detected at the time of cloning experiments.

## 3. Passenger DNA

It is the DNA which is transferred from one organism into another by combining it with the vehicle DNA. Three types of DNA are used as passengers : complementary, synthetic and random.
(i) Complementary or Copy DNA (cDNA): It is synthesised on RNA templates with the help of reverse transcriptase enzyme and necessary nucleotides. The DNA strand is isolated from the hybrid RNA-DNA complex by using enzyme RNase H which digests the RNA strand from DNA-RNA duplex. A complementary DNA strand is then synthesised on the isolated single-stranded DNA template with the help of DNA polymerase. The cDNA duplex so formed can be joined to vehicle DNA for introduction into a host cell.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-250.jpg?height=554&width=673&top_left_y=1829&top_left_x=678)
Fig.: cDNA synthesis

(ii) Synthetic DNA (sDNA): It is synthesised with the help of DNA polymerases on DNA template or from free deoxyribonucleotides without a template.

Artificial Synthesis of DNA on Template: In 1961, Kornberg and his coworkers synthesised DNA from a mixture of deoxyribonucleoside triphosphates, DNA polymerase enzyme, metal ions and a segment of viral DNA to act as a template and a primer. The synthetic DNA showed biological activity. When introduced into bacteria, this DNA coded for the formation of virus particles similar to the one from which template DNA was taken.
Artificial Synthesis of DNA without a Template: Hargobind Khorana and his colleagues produced the first synthetic DNA in 1965 by purely chemical means. They synthesised the gene coding for yeast alanine tRNA which needed only 77 base pairs. Unfortunately, this gene did not function. Khorana and his associates in 1979 synthesised the gene coding for tyrosine-tRNA of E. coli. This gene had 207 nucleotide pairs. It functioned in the bacterium E. coli when introduced in combination with a phage DNA. Genes for hormones somatotropin and insulin have also been artificially synthesised.
(iii) Random DNA: It refers to small fragments formed by breaking a chromosome of an organism with the help of restriction enzyme.

A team of 17 researchers at the J. Craig Venter Institute has created the largest man-made DNA structure by synthesising and assembling the 5,82,970 base pair genome of a bacterium, Mycoplasma genitalium. A chemical synthesiser joined together various short iterations of man-made adenine, cytosine, guanine and thymine that were then assembled into a working genome that can successfully produce the proteins that enable life. Using stretches of DNA, known as cassettes, roughly 1,000 base-pairs in length, the researchers assembled a simplified version of M. mycoides genome from scratch. The final synthetic genome was then inserted into an existing Mycoplasma capricolum cell. The synthetic cell then went on to behave as a M. mycoides, producing proteins from the instructions encoded by the synthetic genome and even dividing and growing.

## Methods of gene transfer:

The method of introducing the recombinant DNA into host cell depends on its type.
There are two distinct techniques for introducing foreign genetic material into a host cell genome - indirect and direct transfer.

- Indirect transfer:

The indirect transfer through a vector requires - (i) selection and isolation of the desirable fragment(s) of DNA which contains gene sequence(s) that needs to be cloned known as insert; (ii) Generation of recombinant DNA( $r$ DNA) molecule by insertion of these inserts (DNA fragments) into a vector that can replicate within a host cell (Recombinant DNA $=$ Vector + insert). For instance, insulin gene is cut off from rat's DNA and linked to a bacterial plasmid, gives recombinant DNA. (iii) Introduction of the $r$ DNA molecules into host cells. The process of transferring a DNA into a prokaryotic host cell is called transformation. In this method, bacterial cells are first treated with a specific concentration of a divalent cation such as calcium to create transient pores in the cell wall. Then the cells with recombinant DNA are incubated on ice followed by placing them briefly at $42^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ (heat shock) in order to force recombinant DNA into the host cells.

- Direct transfer:

The direct introduction of DNA involves -
(i) Electroporation, i.e., application of electric impulses to change the porosity of protoplasts so that they directly imbibe the purified DNA.
(ii) Micro-injection, i.e., direct injection of DNA fragments into the nucleus of the host cell with the help of a micropipette.
(iii) Biolistic or gene gun i.e., bombarding cells with high velocity micro-particles of gold or tungsten coated with DNA to facilitate entry of the foreign genes.
(iv) Other methods of gene transfer are- liposome mediated gene transfer, calcium phosphate precipitation method, transformation by ultrasonication (DNA uptake by ultrasonic waves) etc.
Thus, genetic engineering can be defined as the generation of new combination of heritable material by the insertion of desired genes or DNA of the cell into any carrier system so as to allow their incorporation into a host organism.

## SOLUTION

The enzyme will produce DNA fragments with sticky ends.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-252.jpg?height=310&width=364&top_left_y=374&top_left_x=822)

## Process of Recombinant Technology

Recombinant DNA ( $r$ DNA) process involves a sequence of several steps:

## Step 1: Isolation of a genetic material (DNA)

In order to isolate the gene of interest, complete DNA should be isolated in pure form i.e., it should be free from other macromolecules such as proteins, lipids, carbohydrates and even RNA. DNA is enclosed within the membranes of cell and nucleus so the cell is opened to release DNA along with other macromolecules. The cells or tissues are treated with lysing enzymes such as lysozyme (to digest bacterial cell wall), cellulase (for digesting plant cell wall), and chitinase (to digest fungal cell wall). DNA is treated with ribonuclease and protease to remove RNA and protein, respectively. Other molecules can be removed by appropriate treatments and purified DNA is ultimately collected as fine threads by precipitation after the addition of chilled ethanol.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-252.jpg?height=106&width=385&top_left_y=1135&top_left_x=808)

## Gene Library

A gene library is a collection of cloning vectors which contains the DNA fragments representing the entire genome.
Setting up of Gene Library: A chromosome of an organism is broken into small fragments comprising one or a few genes with the help of restriction endonucleases. Each fragment is joined to a suitable vehicle DNA, forming recombinant DNAs of different nature. These are then introduced into a host (bacterial, yeast, plant or animal) cell. The foreign DNA fragment replicates as the host cell divides. This produces a group of cells, each containing a foreign DNA fragment comprising one or a few genes. The daughter cells identical to the parent cell are referred to as a clone of cells. The process is called DNA cloning. Each clone contains a different fragment of the same foreign DNA. The various clones representing all the genes of an organism are known as gene library of that organism.
Complementary DNA (c-DNA) formed from an RNA template by using the enzyme reverse transcriptase may be cloned as explained above for setting up a gene library. The collection of c-DNA fragments is termed c-DNA library.
Use of Gene Library: From a gene library, it is possible to identify a clone containing the specific gene. This gene can be multiplied by growing the relevant clone in a culture. The base sequence in this gene can be found using different methods. From the base sequence, the sequence of amino acids in a polypeptide can be worked out on the basis of the triplet code.

## Step 2: Cutting at DNA at specific locations

Next step is the isolation of a gene of interest from purified DNA. To do this, purified DNA is incubated with a restriction enzyme. When cut, each fragment of DNA has an overhanging piece of single-stranded DNA on its ends called "sticky ends". Cloning vector is also treated with the same restriction enzyme, resulting in a single-stranded DNA molecule with sticky ends complementary to those found on the fragmented source DNA. Now, both the 'gene of interest' from the source DNA and the cut vector with space are mixed and ligase is added. This results in the preparation of recombinant DNA.

## Step 3 : Amplification of gene of interest using PCR

PCR stands for Polymerase Chain Reaction. It is the process of amplifying a desired gene of interest i.e., multiple copies of the gene (or DNA) of interest is synthesised in vitro (outside living cells). It involves two sets of primers (small chemically synthesised oligonucleotides that are complementary to the regions of DNA of interest) and the enzyme DNA polymerase [isolated from a bacterium, Thermus aquaticus]. This enzyme remains active during the high temperature induced denaturation of double stranded DNA.
PCR consists of three steps:
(i) Step 1 - Denaturation: In this step, reaction mixture is heated to $94^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to separate the two strands of DNA. These separated DNA strands act as template.
(ii) Step 2 - Annealing: The temperature of reaction mixture is lowered to $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to allow the primers to bind the complementary regions on the template DNA.
(iii) Step 3 - Extension: Now, the temperature is raised to $75^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and DNA polymerase adds nucleotides complementary to template strand to the free hydroxyl end of each primer.
These steps are repeated in cyclic manner many times until the desired level of amplification is achieved.
The amplified fragment can now be used to ligate with a vector for further cloning.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-253.jpg?height=960&width=1050&top_left_y=614&top_left_x=552)
Fig.: Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) : Each cycle has three steps : (i) Denaturation (ii) Primer annealing and (iii) Extension of primers

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-253.jpg?height=81&width=104&top_left_y=1672&top_left_x=1009)

## Learn More

## Gene Bank

Gene bank is a place for the storage of genetic material in the form of clones of known DNA fragments, seeds, spores and frozen spermatozoa, eggs and embryos. A gene bank provides materials for use in genetic engineering and breeding experiments, particularly concerned to the extinct species. Preserved human sperm, ova and embryos can also be used for fertility purposes in human females.

## Step 4 : Insertion of recombinant DNA into the host cell/organism

The vector containing the desired DNA sequence is next transferred to a suitable host cell or organism. There are several methods of introducing the ligated DNA into recipient cells.

## Host cell

In genetic engineering, a suitable host cell is required to obtain a large number of copies of specific DNA or to get the gene product (protein). Most commonly used host cells are bacterial cells, yeast cells, mammalian and plant cells. E. coli is the most commonly used bacterial cell as host. In addition to E. coli, other bacteria such as Bacillus, Pseudomonas and Streptomyces may be used as hosts for gene cloning experiments.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/6815bd1d-c783-4de4-939b-5b7db4dc3af8-254.jpg?height=703&width=597&top_left_y=383&top_left_x=703)
Fig.: Processes of Recombinant DNA Technology

## Step 5 : Obtaining the foreign gene product

When a piece of alien DNA in a cloning vector is transferred into a bacterial, plant or animal cell, the alien DNA gets multiplied or produces a desirable protein (when expressed under appropriate conditions).
The protein so obtained is called a recombinant protein. The cells harbouring cloned genes of interest may be grown on a small scale in the laboratory. The cultures may be used for extracting the desired protein and then purifying it by using different separation techniques.
To produce products in large quantities, bioreactors, where large volumes (100-1000 litres) of culture can be processed, are used. Thus, bioreactor is a vessel in which raw materials are biologically converted into specific products, individual enzymes, etc., using microbial plant, animal or human cells. A bioreactor provides the optimal conditions for achieving the desired product (temperature, pH , substrate, salts, vitamins, oxygen). The most commonly used bioreacters are of stirring type.

## Step 6 : Downstream processing

After completion of the biosynthetic stage, the product is subjected through a series of processes before it is ready for marketing as a finished product. The processes include separation and purification, which are collectively referred to as downstream processing. The product has to be formulated with suitable preservatives. Such formulation has to undergo thorough clinical trials as in case of drugs. Strict quality control testing for each product is also required. The downstream processing and quality control testing vary from product to product.
So, we have learnt the tools and process of recombinant DNA technology. Can you now explain how a plant or a mouse can be modified to make them glow? Scientists have altered the genome of tobacco plant by inserting a gene called luciferase from firefly so that the plant emits light in dark. Similarly, a fluorescent protein from a jellyfish is inserted in the genome of a mouse making it glow.

## If 'denaturation' step of PCR is missed, how it will affect the process?

## SOLUTION

During denaturation step of PCR, double strands of DNA separates at high temperature to produce single stranded DNA templates. If the denaturation step is missed, the single stranded template needed for annealing of primers will not be produced. Hence, PCR reaction will not proceed further and no amplification of DNA will occur.

## APPLICATION OF BIOTECHNOLOGY

Biotechnology is applied to various fields and many industries such as food, pharmaceuticals, medicine, agriculture, etc.

## Application of Biotechnology in Agriculture

- The three options for increased food production are :
(i) Agrochemical-based agriculture
(ii) Organic agriculture
(iii) Genetically engineered crop-based agriculture
- The Green Revolution succeeded in increasing the yield of crops mainly due to
(i) use of improved varieties of crops and
(ii) use of agrochemicals
- Further increase in the yield with the existing varieties of crops are not possible using conventional methods of breeding.
- Agrochemicals (fertilisers and pesticides) cause pollution of soil and water and are too expensive for the farmers. Thus, use of genetically modified crops is a possible solution for sustained food production.
- The combined use of recombinant DNA technology and tissue culture techniques has led to the production of genetically modified plants in many crop plants. The use of genetically modified plants has been useful in the following ways :
(i) Genetic modification has made the crops more tolerant to abiotic stresses like cold, heat, drought, salinity, etc.
(ii) It has reduced the dependence of crops on chemical pesticides as they are made pest-resistant. For example, a gene from a soil bacterium, Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) which produces a protein (Bt toxin) with insecticidal property, is introduced in crop plants to provide protection against insects.
(iii) Post-harvest losses are much reduced.
(iv) As the plants have increased efficiency of mineral usage, the early exhaustion of fertility of soil is prevented.
(v) Food produced from GM (Genetically Modified) crops has enhanced nutritional value.
(vi) Genetic modification has been used to create tailor-made plants to supply resources to industries such as starch, fuels, pharmaceuticals, etc.
Transgenic plants (of some species) are easier to produce because plants have a lot of totipotent tissue (meristem) that can be grown in culture and then induced to develop into a whole plant. One of the most common methods utilises a natural gene transfer system called the Ti-plasmid of Agrobacterium tumefaciens which transfers a piece of DNA called T-DNA into the genome of infected plants.


## Application of Biotechnology in Medicine

- The rDNA technology has been used in the production of safe and more effective therapeutic drugs.
- The recombinant therapeutics do not induce unwanted immunological responses, that are commonly observed with similar products isolated from non-human sources.
- At present about thirty recombinant therapeutics have been approved for human use, of which twelve are being marketed in India also.
(i) Genetically engineered insulin (humulin)
- Human insulin consists of two short polypeptide chains : chain A and chain B, linked by disulphide bridges.
- Insulin is secreted as prohormone which has to be processed before it becomes a mature and functional hormone.
- The prohormone contains another polypeptide called C -peptide, which is removed during maturation.
- In 1983, Eli Lilly, an American company prepared two DNA sequences coding for chains A and B of human insulin and introduced them into the plasmids of Escherichia coli to produce insulin chains.
- The two chains produced were extracted and combined by creating disulphide bridges from human insulin.
(ii) Gene therapy
- In this method, genes are inserted into the cells and tissues of an individual to correct certain hereditary diseases.
- It involves the delivery of a normal gene into the individual or embryo to replace the defective mutant allele of the gene.
- The first clinical gene therapy was given in 1990 to a four year old girl with adenosine deaminase (ADA) deficiency.
- ADA deficiency can be cured by bone marrow transplantation in some children but it is not completely curative.
- For gene therapy, lymphocytes were grown in a culture and functional ADA cDNA is then introduced into these lymphocytes using retro-virus as vector.
- These lymphocytes are then transferred into the body of the patient. However the patient requires periodic infusion of such genetically engineered lymphocytes.
- If a functional gene is introduced into the bone marrow cells at early embryonic stage, it would be a permanent cure.


## (iii) Molecular diagnosis

- Recombinant DNA techniques like PCR (Polymerase Chain Reaction) and ELISA (Enzyme linked immuno sorbent assay) are used for early diagnosis of disorders.
(a) PCR is used to detect HIV in suspected AIDS patients and to detect mutations in gene in suspected cancer patients.
(b) ELISA is based on the principle of antigen-antibody interaction. It is used to detect infection by pathogen by the presence of antigens (proteins, glycoproteins, etc.) or the antibodies synthesised against the pathogen.


## Transgenic Animals

- Transgenic animals are those animals that have had their DNA manipulated to possess, and express a foreign gene.
- Transgenic animals are used in the following ways :
(i) Transgenic animals can be specifically designed to study gene regulation and how they affect the normal functions of the body. e.g., information is obtained about the biological role of insulin-like growth factor.
(ii) Transgenic animals are designed to increase our understanding of how genes contribute to the development of diseases; they are made to serve as models for human diseases.
(iii) Transgenic animals that produce useful biological compounds can be created by introducing a portion of DNA that codes for that product from other organism(s) e.g., alpha-1 antitrypsin, a human protein used to treat emphysema. The first transgenic cow, Rosie, produced the human protein-enriched milk ( $2.4 \mathrm{~g} /$ litre); it also contained human alpha-lactalbumin, a more nutritionally balanced product for human babies.
(iv) Transgenic mice are being developed for use in testing the safety of vaccines (e.g., polio vaccine).
(v) Transgenic animals with more sensitivity to toxic substances are being developed to test the toxicity of drugs.


## Ethical issues

The modification and use of such organisms for public services has resulted in some ethical problems. Hence, the Indian Government has set up organisations which are authorised to make decisions regarding the validity of genetic modifications and the safety of introducing genetically modified organisms for public services. One such organisation is the Genetic Engineering Approval Committee (GEAC).

## Biopiracy

- The industrialised/developed nations are rich financially, but poor in biodiversity and traditional knowledge, while the developing and underdeveloped countries are rich in bioresources and traditional knowledge.
- These developed countries use the bioresources and traditional knowledge of the other countries without proper authorisation and/or compensation to the countries concerned (Biopiracy). For example, basmati rice grown in India is distinct for its unique flavour and aroma, but an American company got patent rights on Basmati through the US patent and trademark office; the new variety of Basmati has been developed by this company (crossing an Indian variety with the semi-dwarf varieties).
- Now some nations are developing laws to prevent such unauthorised exploitation of their bioresources and traditional knowledge.


## Further considerations

The use of whole animals or plants for experimental purposes also requires careful consideration. Investigators must focus on the regulations, restrictions and requirements for the conduct of work with GMOs in most countries and institutions.
Countries may have national authorities that establish guidelines for work with GMOs, and may help scientists classify their work at the appropriate biosafety level. In some cases, classification may differ between countries and they may decide to classify work at a lower or higher level, when new information on a particular vector/ host system becomes available.
Risk assessment is a dynamic process that takes into account new developments and the progress of science. The performance of appropriate risk assessment will assure that the benefits of recombinant DNA technology remain available to humankind in the years to come.

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has 4 choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONLY ONE is correct.

(a) Hydrolases
(b) DNA polymerases
(c) Restriction endonuclcases
(d) Ligases

(b) Transduction

(c) Beadle and Tatum

(a) clone and genetically modified genes.
(b) introduction of foreign genes.
(c) genetic engineering.
(d) purified genes.

(a) bacteriophages
(b) bacterial cells
(c) plasmids
(d) all prokaryotic cells

(b) Size only
(c) Charge to size ratio

(b) Prophage

(b) Escherichia coli and Agrobacterium tumefaciens
(c) Vibria cholerae and a tailed bacteriophage
(d) Diplococcus sp. and Pseudomonas sp.
9. Restriction endonuclease:
(a) Synthesises DNA
(b) Cuts the DNA molecule randomly
(c) Cuts the DNA molecule at specific sites
(d) Restricts the synthesis of DNA inside the nucleus

(a) the phenomenon of transduction in bacteria is well understood.
(b) we can see DNA by electron microscope.
(c) we can cut DNA at specific sites by ligases.
(d) restriction endonucleases purified from bacteria can be used in vitro.

(a) first generation vaccine

(a) antibiotic
(b) enzyme

(a) Streptomyces griseus
(b) Streptomyces rimosus

(a) Lactobacillus bulgaricus
(b) Penicillium citrinium

## Assertion \& Reason:

DIRECTIONS : Each of these questions contains an Assertion followed by reason. Read them carefully and answer the question on the basis of following options. You have to select the one that best describes the two statements.
(a) If both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
(b) If both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
(c) If Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
(d) If Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

1. Assertion: Insulin is an important life saving drug for diabetic patients.
Reason: It is now possible to produce insulin by using recombinant DNA technology.
2. Assertion: The cut pieces of DNA are linked with plasmid DNA.
Reason: Plasmid DNA fails to act as vectors.
3. Assertion: Exonucleases remove nucleotides from the ends of DNA.
Reason: Endonucleases make cuts as specific positions within the DNA.
4. Assertion: Restriction digestion is a process of cutting DNA by restriction enzyme.
Reason: DNA ligase joins two DNAs.
5. Assertion: Restriction endonucleases are also called 'molecular scissors'.
Reason: When fragments generated by restriction endonucleases are mixed, they join together due to their sticky ends.
6. Assertion: All endonuclease cut DNA at specific sites.

Reason: Endonucleases are found in viruses.
7. Assertion: Biotechnology produces transgenic microorganisms that act as microfactories for proteins.
Reason: To produce proteins of human use like insulin, transgenic micro-organisms can be developed.
8. Assertion: Using biotechnology, human insulin can be produced in bacterial cells.
Reason: To produce human insulin A , B and C polypeptides of the human insulin are produced in the bacterial cells, separately extracted and combined by creating disulphide bonds.
9. Assertion: Humulin is better than conventional insulin.

Reason: Conventional insulin produces many side effects.

23. (a) Restriction endonuclease is an enzyme that cuts dsDNA into fragments at or near specific recognition sites, called palindromic sequences within the molecule.
These enzymes are found in bacteria and archaea and provide a defence mechanism against invading viruses.
24. (a) A vector is a DNA molecule which is used as a vehicle to carry the gene of interest to another cell.
25. (c) Golden rice (Oryza sativa) is a genetically modified crop. It synthesises $\beta$-carotene which is the precursor of vitamin A .

28. (d) Selectable markers in recombinant DNA technology help in identification and elimination of non-transformants and selectively permit the growth of the transformants.
29. (c) PCR is based on three simple steps required for any DNA synthesis reaction: (i) denaturation of the template into single strands; (ii) annealing of primers to each original strand for new strand synthesis; and (iii) extension of the new DNA strands from the primers.
30. (b) Biopiracy refers to the use of bioresources by multinational companies and other organisation without proper authorisation from the countries and people concerned with compensatory payment.
31. (b) A restriction enzyme or restriction endonuclease is an enzyme that cleaves DNA into fragments at or near specific recognition sites within DNA known as restriction sites. Restriction enzymes are one class of the broader endonuclease group of enzymes. Each restriction endonuclease functions by inspecting the length of a DNA sequence. Once it finds its specific recognition sequence, it will bind to the DNA and cut each of the two strands of the double helix at specific points in their sugar phosphate backbone. These enzymes predictably cut both strands because the sequences they recognise are palindromic. Which means the recognition sequences of identical bases on both DNA strands.
32. (a) Golden rice is a form of rice with biosynthesis of beta-carotene (a form of vitamin A) which gives its golden or yellow colour. It is considered a type of genetically modified rice (GMO) and a fortified food that is produced to combat vitamin A deficiency in areas where this vitamin is scarce. It was in the late 1990s that two European scientists developed Golden Rice after inserting beta carotene-enriched gene from daffodil to rice.
33. (b)
34. (a) Bacillus thuringiensis is a source of Cry- proteins. Thermus aquaticus is a source of thermostable DNA polymerase (Taq polymerase) used in PCR. Agrobacterium tumefaciens is a cloning vector. The construction of $1^{\text {st }}$ recombinant DNA molecule was performed using native plasmid of Salmonella typhimurium.

37. (c) Restriction endonucleases make cuts at specific positions within the DNA. They function by inspecting the length of a DNA sequence. Restriction endonuclease binds to the DNA and cut the two strands of double helix at specific points in their sugar-phosphate backbones. They are used in genetic engineering to form recombinant molecules of DNA. DNA ligases join the DNA fragments.
